speaker: Gergely
text_en: Kelsey Hightower is known as one of the most influential voices in the Kubernetes community. But you wouldn't guess from how his career started. At 19, he dropped out of college to be a DSL modem installer, became a self-taught developer, and still went on to become a distinguished engineer at Google. At age 43, he then retired at the very top of the industry. Today, we cover Kelsey's unconventional path into tech, and how he kept creating new opportunities for himself, often unknowingly. The inside story of the container wars, Puppet, Docker, Terraform, CoreOS, and how If you're an engineer and thinking about your long-term career trajectory, whether that's getting into a staff plus level, going independent, or even quietly planning to leave the industry, this episode is for you.
text: Kelsey Hightower 被认为是 Kubernetes 社区最有影响力的声音之一。但从他的职业起点，你完全猜不到他后来会走到这一步。19 岁时，他从大学退学，去做 DSL modem 安装员，后来靠自学成为开发者，最终还当上了 Google 的 distinguished engineer。43 岁时，他又在行业最巅峰的时候选择了退休。今天我们会聊聊 Kelsey 这条非传统的科技行业之路，以及他是如何不断为自己创造新机会的，很多时候甚至是在无意中做到的。我们还会讲到容器大战背后的内幕，Puppet、Docker、Terraform、CoreOS，以及如果你是一名工程师，正在思考自己长期的职业发展路径——无论是冲击 staff 及以上级别、转做独立工作，还是悄悄计划离开这个行业——那这一期就是为你准备的。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: This episode is longer than a normal episode, frankly, because I was so glued to my chair, mostly listening to Kelsey's stories and thinking. This episode is presented by Antithesis. Verify your system's correctness without human review or traditional integration tests and avoid bugs or outages. Before we start, I'd like to mention our presenting sponsor and thesis and maybe offer a little history lesson. Over the last two decades, software development has gone through a mindset shift from an imperative approach to declarative one. Infrastructure is a perfect example. Think about how tools like Puppet and Ansible allow declaring how individual servers should be configured. Then came Terraform, the ability to declare the desired end state of your whole infrastructure, servers, networks, databases, and their relationships. And then with Kubernetes, we stop scripting container life cycles. Instead, we write manifestos that say things like, I want the replicas of this application exposed behind the surface with this much CPU and memory. Once we didn't have to specify every little detail of our infrastructure anymore, deploying software became much faster. But then the bottleneck became how quickly we could test and verify the code to be deployed. Testing remained imperative. We had to write tests for every little detail. And now with LLMs we're on the verge of a declarative shift in the way code is written as well. Just tell the model what you want and let it figure out the details. And it's going to make the verification bottleneck a million times worse. Anthesis is a declarative testing tool that can keep up with your AI coding agents. You state the properties you want your software to have and Antistasis figures out how to check them for you. Verify your code as fast as agents can write it and ship with ground and confidence. Head to antistasis.com slash pragmatic.
text: 这一期比平时更长，说实话，是因为我整个人都听入迷了，几乎一直钉在椅子上，听 Kelsey 讲故事、边听边想。这期节目由 Antithesis 赞助。它可以在不依赖人工审核或传统集成测试的情况下验证你的系统正确性，帮你避免 bug 和故障。开始之前，我想先提一下我们的冠名赞助商 Antithesis，也顺便讲一点历史。过去二十年里，软件开发的思维方式经历了一次转变：从命令式走向声明式。基础设施就是个很好的例子。想想 Puppet 和 Ansible 这类工具，它们让你可以声明单台服务器应该如何配置。后来又有了 Terraform，你可以声明整个基础设施期望达到的最终状态，包括服务器、网络、数据库以及它们之间的关系。再后来有了 Kubernetes，我们不再去写脚本控制容器生命周期，而是写 manifest，告诉系统：我希望这个应用有多少个副本，通过这个 service 对外暴露，分配这么多 CPU 和内存。自从我们不再需要指定基础设施里的每一个细节后，软件部署速度快了很多。但新的瓶颈变成了：我们能多快测试并验证即将部署的代码。测试依然是命令式的，我们得为每个细节都写测试。现在随着 LLM 的出现，代码编写方式也正站在一次声明式转变的门口。你只要告诉模型你想要什么，让它去搞定细节。但这会让验证这个瓶颈恶化一百万倍。Antithesis 就是一种声明式测试工具，能够跟上你的 AI 编码 agent。你只需要说明你希望软件具备哪些性质，Antithesis 会自己想办法替你检查。让你的代码验证速度跟得上 agent 写代码的速度，带着扎实的把握和信心去发布。访问 antithesis.com/pragmatic。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Kelsey, welcome to the podcast. It's so nice to see you in person. Yo, I'm actually happy to be here mainly because I kind of look at your stuff over the years. So it's an honor to be here in Amsterdam as well.
text: Kelsey，欢迎来到播客。很高兴终于在线下见到你。哟，我其实也很高兴能来，主要是这些年我一直有在看你的内容。所以能来到 Amsterdam 上节目，我也觉得很荣幸。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: How did you make your first dollar at a job?
text: 你人生中第一份工作赚到的第一块钱，是怎么来的？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Oh, my first dollar at a job, McDonald's. right that counts so in high school you get the job that's closest to you so it was in walking distance of my house as soon as I turned legal age 14 get a work permit and I went there and it was one of those jobs where you know you go you fill out the application the same day you typically get your information or you're going to get hired the same day when can you start I'm like right now The Pragmatic Engineer
text: 哦，第一块钱啊，McDonald's。对，这也算吧。高中时你找工作，通常就是找离家最近的，所以那家店走路就能到。我一到法定年龄 14 岁，办了工作许可就去了。那种工作你知道的，去填申请表，通常当天就会告诉你情况，甚至当天就直接录用。问你什么时候能上班，我说，现在就行。The Pragmatic Engineer

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: you know people have expectations and I learned how to run the whole store so by the time I turned 15 I was a assistant manager so nights and weekends you know other managers would leave they would give this 15 year old the keys and I knew how to do everything there including close out the store right so you have to count all the money you have to fax this huge spreadsheet to corporate every night and then my mom would pick me up And so it was really good learning how to really be responsible even for adults at that age. So that's how I got my first dollar. How did you get into tech? How did you get into programming? In high school, since I moved from California to Atlanta, so you're going from one side of the country to another side of the country. And I missed maybe three to six months of school. And in order to graduate on time, I had to take some extra classes. And so as someone who played sports, ran track, played football, played basketball, and it's like you know there's this computer programming not you know computer club technology student association there was a class component and then there was after school component and I was like I don't know man this computer stuff that's for the you know you know I'm trying to be a cool kid but the one thing I did I really enjoyed it right so I had a liking to AutoCAD I even competed at the state level in AutoCAD. So we drove down and you compete. They give you a task and you sit in front of the computer. It was my first year doing it, but I really liked the idea of taking a specification, designing it, and I probably would have gotten first place if I would have got the product to work because you also have to print it out so that the judges can review your work. So I got second place, even though I didn't print it out.
text: 你知道，人们是有期待的。我后来把整家店怎么运转都学会了，所以到 15 岁的时候，我已经是 assistant manager 了。晚上和周末，其他经理会先走，直接把钥匙交给这个 15 岁的小孩，而我知道那里的所有流程，甚至包括打烊。打烊时你得清点所有现金，还要每晚把一张很大的电子表格传真给总部，然后我妈再来接我回家。所以那段经历特别锻炼人，让我在那个年纪就学会了真正地负责任，甚至是对成年人负责。这就是我赚到第一块钱的方式。那你是怎么进入 tech 的？又是怎么开始学编程的？高中时，我从 California 搬到 Atlanta，相当于从美国一边搬到另一边。我大概耽误了三到六个月的学业。为了按时毕业，我必须多修一些课。那时候我一直是搞体育的，跑田径、打橄榄球、打篮球。结果学校里有个计算机编程相关的东西，不只是电脑社团、Technology Student Association，那里面既有课程部分，也有课后活动。我当时想，哎呀，这种电脑的东西不是给那种……你懂的，我那时候还想当个酷小孩。但我真正接触之后，发现自己其实很喜欢。我那时特别喜欢 AutoCAD，甚至还参加过州级的 AutoCAD 比赛。我们会开车过去参赛，现场给你一个任务，你坐在电脑前完成。那是我第一年参加，但我真的很喜欢这种感觉：拿到一个规格说明，然后把它设计出来。其实如果最后我把成品弄出来，我大概能拿第一，因为你还得把作品打印出来给评委看。结果我虽然没打印出来，还是拿了第二名。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: This is the 3D modeling.
text: 就是 3D 建模那种？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, AutoCAD, you know, just AutoCAD. Yeah, so part of the curriculum was, you know, you build bridges. We did this thing with chapter team where, you know, you have a coat of arms and you're kind of doing like a debate. Do you kind of I learned all of these things related to business, but CAD was one of the things I liked most. Also in that class, one of the classmates taught me TI Basic. Is that a version of Basic? Well, so TI Basic, so you know the graphing calculator, it's like a TI-86. You can program it. Oh right so in class they're like hey you know it's not just a graphing calculator you can actually program it and I was like what's that and it's like look we can you know everyone at that time you would create the snake game right so it's basically get a magazine copy and paste the code and then you run it and now you're playing snake based on the code you wrote and so you would toy around with this concept so that was probably the first introduction to programming was literally programming my TI-86 calculator. And after high school, did you go to college or you considered college, right? I considered college because in Georgia at the time, and still today, there's a thing called the HOPE program. So if you have a B average or above, you can go to any public school for free. And public schools in Georgia include Georgia Tech, Georgia State. These are pretty good universities. And so I decided to go to one that was near me The first two weeks I was like, this is too slow. This is not the pace that I want to move at. And also remember, it's 1999 when I'm graduating. And so when you turn on the TV, people are standing in line for the next version of Windows. There's a lot of euphoria. AOL is starting to phase out and we're starting to touch on high-speed internet. And I was like, yo, look at the pace this is moving. But also, you're hearing the narratives. Bill Gates drops out of college. These people are not necessarily glorifying the degree anymore. It's all about the skill. Now, unfortunately for me, I didn't know anyone that was a programmer. I didn't know anyone that was like a system administrator because at that time, all the systems were like Sun Microsystems or IBM Mainframe. Those are still the things that are in the enterprise. So when I look at the job openings, I'm seeing a bunch of skills that I don't even know how to acquire. And so instead of going to college, you know, I'm still doing fast food, delivering pizzas at this time at Pizza Hut. And I remember going to a bookstore and they had the A-plus certification guide. And I looked in some of the job posts and said, hey, you need to be A-plus certified. Take this support role or whatever it was. And I was like, you know what? That doesn't require college. The book is only $35 and I remember buying the book and it is an official certification process it looks like it was part of the job market and so I remember buying that book and reading it cover to cover over and over again and you're learning all the fundamentals right you're learning about you know how motherboards and how memory and all these things work and there's an OS component and there's a little practice exam in the back And so for someone like me, having that fast feedback loop of like, you put the CD in, you take the exam. And even though it was multiple choice, you kind of felt like if I got anything wrong, I would just go back to the book and make sure that I understood what was written there. And then you go take the test again and had a little randomization to it. So you can just rely on absolutely remembering everything. And I remember going to the facility to take the test. And you know, you're in that little room. and they wanna make sure you don't cheat. So there's a camera pointed at you and you're just going through it. And so the nice thing about those tests, there's no trick questions. Either you know it or you don't. And I think they maybe give you an hour, hour and a half. And I remember finishing that thing in like 10 minutes. And when I walked down, you know, you wait, the dial-up goes and they calculate your score and say, hey, you passed. And then you walk out and you're like A-plus certified. And that was like the first time in my career that I felt like, oh, so if you put the effort in, You can gain the certificate. And when I got that certificate, I remember there was like a job fair where, hey, anyone that has A plus and network plus certification, you can be part of the contractors that were replacing people's dial-up with DSL at the time. And so that's how I, I guess, officially got into tech.
text: 对，AutoCAD，就是 AutoCAD。对，所以课程里有一部分是，比如说设计桥梁。我们还参加过 chapter team 之类的活动，要做徽章设计，还会有点像辩论那样的内容。总之我学了很多和商业相关的东西，但 CAD 是我最喜欢的之一。也是在那门课上，有个同学教了我 TI Basic。那是 Basic 的一个版本吗？对，TI Basic，就是你知道那种图形计算器，比如 TI-86，你可以给它编程。哦，对。老师在课上会说，嘿，这不只是个图形计算器，你其实还能给它写程序。我当时就想，那是什么？然后别人就说，你看，我们可以……那时候大家都会做贪吃蛇游戏。基本上就是找本杂志，把代码抄进去，然后运行，接着你就能玩自己写出来的贪吃蛇了。你就会开始摆弄这个概念。所以我第一次接触编程，大概就是给我的 TI-86 计算器写程序。高中毕业后，你上大学了吗？还是说你有考虑过上大学，对吧？我考虑过，因为当时在 Georgia，现在也还有，一个叫 HOPE program 的项目。如果你的平均成绩在 B 或以上，就可以免费上任何公立学校。Georgia 的公立学校里包括 Georgia Tech、Georgia State，这些都算很不错的大学。所以我决定去一所离家近的学校。结果前两周我就觉得，这也太慢了，不是我想要的节奏。而且你要记得，那是 1999 年，我毕业那年。你打开电视，会看到人们为了 Windows 的新版本排长队。整个社会都很亢奋。AOL 开始慢慢退场，高速互联网开始出现。我当时就想，哇，这发展速度也太快了。而且你听到的叙事也是那样：Bill Gates 从大学退学。大家已经不再那么神化学历了，重点变成了技能。可惜对我来说，当时我一个程序员都不认识，也不认识什么系统管理员，因为那时候企业里跑的系统大多还是 Sun Microsystems 或 IBM Mainframe 这些东西。所以我去看招聘信息时，看到一堆我连怎么学都不知道的技能要求。于是我没继续上大学。那时候我还在做快餐，也在 Pizza Hut 送披萨。我记得有一次去书店，看到一本 A-plus certification guide。我又看了看一些招聘启事，上面写着，嘿，你需要 A-plus 认证，才能做 support 之类的岗位。我就想，你知道吗，这不需要大学学历，这本书才 35 美元。我记得我把那本书买了下来。这看起来像是一个正式认证流程，而且确实是就业市场的一部分。所以我就把那本书从头到尾反复看了很多遍。你会学到所有基础知识，比如主板、内存这些东西是怎么工作的，还有操作系统相关内容，书后面还有个小型模拟考试。对我这种人来说，这种快速反馈循环特别有用：你把 CD 放进去，做题。虽然是选择题，但你会觉得，只要有一道做错了，我就得回去翻书，确保自己真的理解了上面写的内容。然后你再考一次，它还有一点随机性，所以你不能只靠死记硬背。我记得后来我去考场参加正式考试。你知道的，就是那种小房间，他们得确保你不作弊，所以会有摄像头对着你。你就一路做下去。这类考试好的一点是，没有什么文字陷阱。会就是会，不会就是不会。我记得考试时间大概是一小时到一个半小时，但我大概 10 分钟就做完了。走出来之后，你得等一下，系统拨号上传，算出你的分数，然后告诉你，嘿，你通过了。你走出考场的时候，就成了 A-plus 认证持有者。那是我职业生涯里第一次真正感觉到：哦，原来只要你肯投入努力，你就能拿到这个证书。拿到证书之后，我记得有个招聘会说，凡是有 A-plus 和 network plus 认证的人，都可以加入承包商团队，去帮人们把原来的拨号上网升级成当时的 DSL。所以我想，那大概就是我正式进入 tech 的方式。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Is it fair to say that You saw that this could be the most efficient way to get into tech at the time? I think I said I saw it as the only way. Why was college never like telling you like, okay, that could be a way? Was it just you didn't see examples?
text: 这么说公平吗：你当时觉得，这是进入科技行业最高效的方式？我记得我说过，我当时觉得这几乎是唯一的路。为什么大学从来没让你觉得，哦，好吧，那也可以是一条路？还是说你就是没看到相关的例子？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, I never saw the examples. I never saw the end game. A lot of the stuff that they were teaching the curriculum, it didn't make sense that you would pay all that money you know look maybe it wasn't a good school maybe it was the wrong class that I took there's so many factors that could have went into this but when I looked at it none of the people that at the time that I was looking up to this is not the path that they seem to be taking and so I had enough of school right if you're 18 at that time you're like look that that's enough of this because at the time I kind of felt school was this because it was so easy for me actually You know, it's like it was easy to get straight A's. I didn't feel like there was a serious challenge. So it's like, hey, I want to go and do four more years of this. And I would later learn that, look, bachelors is a lot of the similar that you go through K through 12. You kind of remember stuff. You listen to the lessons. But then masters, you challenge the material. And of course, if you make it to PhD, ideally you're adding something new to the field. And I never saw anyone that has made it that far. So I never put that in part of my calculus. So But just having that immediate feedback loop of like getting this A-plus certification and feeling like, oh, I'm ready to participate in the actual economy, the ecosystem. So to me, I was like, this seems like a better path. And it felt like a path that I would control.
text: 对，我从来没见过那样的例子。我也从来没看到最终会通向什么结果。他们教的很多课程内容，在我看来都不太说得通——你要花那么多钱。也许学校不够好，也许是我选错了课，可能有很多因素造成这种感觉。但当我去看那些我当时仰望的人时，他们走的似乎都不是这条路。所以我对上学已经有点受够了。你想啊，18 岁的时候，你会觉得，行了，这些已经够了。因为那时候我对学校的感觉有点像——其实对我来说太容易了。你知道吗，拿全 A 很轻松，我并不觉得那是什么真正的挑战。所以我就会想，我还要再去过四年这种日子吗？后来我才明白，本科很多东西其实和 K 到 12 年级差不多，你就是记东西、听课。但到了硕士阶段，你开始挑战这些材料；当然，如果你读到 PhD，理想情况下你是在给这个领域增加新的东西。而我从来没见过有人走到那么远，所以我当时根本没把这算进我的考量里。不过，拿到这个 A-plus 认证之后，那种立刻得到反馈的感觉很强，会觉得，哦，我已经准备好参与真正的经济体系、这个生态了。所以对我来说，这看起来是一条更好的路。而且这条路让我觉得是我自己能掌控的。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Yeah. And then what was your first job that you could get with the certification? This was the Comp TA, right?
text: 对。那你拿到这个认证之后找到的第一份工作是什么？这是 Comp TA，对吧？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah. So at that time, Bell South was, you know, the biggest telco probably in America. You know, they had been broken up by that time from the AT&T days. At that time, the people who did phone lines, right? So those are the official Bell South technicians. They drive the fancy trucks. They have all the equipment. And when they made the shift to high-speed internet, that means you had to actually touch the computer. And I think as a union, they're like, look, we don't touch the computer. We don't even go into the house. We get to, you know, the D mark and we stop. And so they had contractors come in. And the contractor's job were to come in, have to do a little bit of wiring. So if you had to run some cable, you did that. Crate Cat5 cables, you did that.
text: 对。当时 Bell South 算是美国最大的电信公司了，至少可能是最大的之一。那时候它已经从当年 AT&T 的体系里拆分出来了。当时他们主要是做电话线路的，对吧？那些才是 Bell South 的正式技术员，开着很气派的工程车，设备也很齐全。后来他们开始转向高速互联网，这就意味着你得真的去碰电脑了。我觉得作为工会成员，他们的态度大概是：我们不碰电脑，我们甚至都不进屋。我们只做到 demarc 那里就停了。所以他们就找了承包商来做。承包商的工作就是进屋，做一点布线。如果需要拉线，你就拉；要压 Cat5 网线，你就做。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Ooh, yeah. One of my first jobs was actually cabling, so I still, I forgot the exact color code.
text: 哦，对。我最早的一份工作之一其实也是做布线，所以我现在还——不过具体线序我已经忘了。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Orange, green, white, green, blue, white, blue, something like that. It's burning your head now. And, you know, so you did whatever it took. And the other thing you had to do was you had to open the computer. You had to make a decision, right? If they had a new enough computer, they can use a USB modem. Those were terrible. They always broke and you would always come out for a repair. But for a new install, if you really wanted to do a good job, you install a NIC, right? Cat5 port on the back of their computer. And at that time, like we're talking Windows 98. And so usually... I don't know 20% of the time as you're installing the drivers the computer would crash and now you have a whole nother situation you have to now troubleshoot getting this thing back online or back operational but if everything went smoothly they now had a network card and then you had an external modem that then you connected to you know the phone line and they had this high speed internet connection and then you connected the network cable and I did that for about let's say a year and then I started doing the businesses so you're going to people's homes like you're going door to door And then when you go to a business, you would hook up one computer, but there's obviously eight computers there, and only one of them has internet access. And I remember at the time, you know, the business owner could be like a small insurance company. And they would say like, hey, how do we get all the other ones online? And the first time someone asked me that, I'm like, I don't know, man, I don't, we put it on one computer.
text: 橙、绿、白绿、蓝、白蓝，差不多是那样。现在这东西已经刻在你脑子里了。总之，反正需要做什么你就做什么。还有一件事是，你得把电脑打开，然后做个判断，对吧？如果对方的电脑比较新，就可以用 USB modem。那玩意儿特别烂，老是坏，你总得回来维修。但如果是新装机，你真想把活干漂亮的话，就会装一块 NIC，也就是在电脑后面加一个 Cat5 网口。那时候我们说的还是 Windows 98。所以通常……我也不知道，大概 20% 的时候，你在装驱动的过程中电脑会直接崩掉，然后你就又多了一个麻烦，得开始排查，想办法把这台机器重新弄上线，或者至少恢复正常运行。但如果一切顺利，他们就有了网卡，然后再接一个外置 modem，连到电话线上，这样他们就有了高速上网连接，再把网线接上。我大概做了差不多一年，后来开始接企业客户。之前你是去别人家里，基本上就是挨家挨户跑。等你去了公司，你会给一台电脑接上网，但显然那儿有八台电脑，结果只有一台能上网。我记得那时候，老板可能是一家小保险公司的人，他会问，哎，其他电脑怎么也连上？第一次有人这么问我的时候，我心想，我也不知道啊哥们，我们的工作就是给这一台装上。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: But like pay grade, right?
text: 但这已经超出你的职级了，对吧？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, we make sure it works. But then I decided like, well, let me go learn. And that's when I remember like going to like Office Depot where they sell computer equipment and things like this. And I went in the store and I asked them, it was like, oh, you can get one of these Linksys routers, right? The infamous blue spaceship looking Linksys router. I still remember them. And those things were probably like 50 bucks. And I remember just buying one and figuring out how to get multiple computers to use one connection. And so eventually I was like, look, I can't do it as part of the job because we have to do this and we have to leave. But here's my card. And they will call you. And I remember one of the first installations that I did on my own, they wrote me a check. And I was like, yeah, you could just write it out to Kelsey Hightower. He was like, no, we don't write checks to people. We write checks to companies. And I remember right there on the spot, I'm like, oh, man, I need a company. And I just made one of DigitalGate. and they wrote that on the check. And I'm sitting there like, so how do you cash this? So I went to the bank and they're like, sir, you're going to have to get a business license. The business license, you could just do business as, you have to do this. So I'm figuring out, now I'm 19 years old, like, okay, I got to go get a business license. I have to figure all this stuff out. So I do everything. I open a business account just so I can cash the check. But at that point, I'm like, oh, this pays more than this does. And so I got really good at doing those network installs. I really got good at troubleshooting because sometimes someone gave them a USB modem, lightning comes, the USB modem is fried, and then you would swap them out for a network card. And eventually I decided, I can probably do my own business. And I decided to get some office space. So I opened a small computer store right outside of Atlanta. I would buy parts from the distributors. I was just like 19, 20 years old. And I wasn't buying enough to really qualify for an account. But luckily, one of the smaller computer stores I used to buy parts from gave a recommendation to the distributors like, hey, this is our guy. He's just getting started. And they gave me an account. and I was able to buy motherboards and GPUs and people would come over and like they bring their kid and they would have a parts list I want a computer with all these things and we would assemble machines and you know sell them but also it was the headquarters for all the other service calls so I did that for like three four years you know that ended up evolving into at the same time but now we're talking like 2000 2001 A lot of the music studios were moving from analog gear and the large mixing boards to Pro Tools, right? The little rack mount unit and they all need it max. They all need these conversions. So I added that to my abilities and then I had a small setup in the store and artists and musicians would come in and say, hey, we want exactly that in our studio. And I would get the order and I added it to the list of things I could do.
text: 对，我们的职责就是确保它能用。但后来我就想，那我自己去学学看。于是我记得我去了 Office Depot 这种卖电脑设备之类东西的地方。我进店里问他们，他们就说，哦，你可以买一个 Linksys 路由器，对吧？就是那个臭名昭著、长得像蓝色小飞船的 Linksys 路由器。我到现在都记得。那东西大概 50 美元。我记得我就买了一个，然后自己琢磨怎么让多台电脑共用一条网络连接。后来我就想，行，这个不能算在本职工作里，因为我们得装完就走。但这是我的名片。然后他们就会给你打电话。我记得我第一次自己单干做安装的时候，对方给我开了一张支票。我当时说，嗯，你直接写给 Kelsey Hightower 就行。结果那人说，不，我们不给个人开支票，我们只给公司开。我当场就愣了，心想，糟了，我得有个公司。于是我当场现编了一个，叫 DigitalGate。然后他们就把这个名字写在支票上。我坐在那儿还在想，所以这玩意儿要怎么兑现？后来我去了银行，他们说，先生，你得先办营业执照。你可以用 DBA 的形式经营，总之这些手续都得办。于是我那时候 19 岁，就开始摸索：好，我得去办营业执照，我得把这些事情都搞明白。所以我把这些都办了，还开了个公司账户，就为了把那张支票兑现。但从那一刻起，我就意识到，哦，这个比那份工作赚得多。于是我在做这些网络安装上越来越熟练，排障也越来越厉害。因为有时候别人给客户装的是 USB modem，一打雷，USB modem 就烧了，这时候你就会给他换成网卡。后来我就觉得，我大概可以自己做生意了。于是我决定租个办公室。我在 Atlanta 郊外开了一家小电脑店。我从分销商那里进零件。那时候我才 19、20 岁，采购量其实还不够大，按理说都不够资格开账户。但幸运的是，我以前常去买零件的一家小电脑店给分销商做了推荐，说，嘿，这是我们的人，他刚起步。于是他们给我开了账户。我就能买主板、GPU 这些东西。有人会带着孩子过来，手里拿着一张配置单，说我想要一台带这些配置的电脑。然后我们就帮他们组装机器，再卖给他们。不过那家店同时也是我处理其他上门服务电话的总部。我大概这样做了三四年。后来这件事又继续演变了。与此同时，时间差不多到了 2000、2001 年。很多音乐工作室开始从模拟设备和大型调音台，转向 Pro Tools，就是那种小型机架式设备。而且他们都需要 Mac，都需要做这些转换。所以我又把这项能力加进了自己的技能列表里。我还在店里搭了一个小型演示环境，艺人和音乐人会进来，说，嘿，我们想在自己的录音室里也搞一套一模一样的。然后我就接单，把这项服务也加进了我能做的事情清单里。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: I mean, at this point, you now have a small business. Sounds like it's going well. And suddenly you take a job at an employee job at Google. When I look back, how did that come? Because up to now, it's almost like this is like, you know, the story oftentimes will continue. You become an entrepreneur. You grow your business. You, you know, you just take it from there.
text: 我是说，到这个时候，你已经有了一门小生意。听起来还做得不错。结果你突然去 Google 找了一份员工的工作。现在回头看，这一步是怎么发生的？因为到这儿为止，感觉故事通常都会继续往下走：你成了创业者，把生意做大，然后就一路这么干下去。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Even during that time as that store owner, I managed a comedian and we went on the road and. So you were helping out your managing? Yeah, I had a buddy from my school. He was a comedian. Turns out he was actually really good at it. I went to go see him at a club he's like hey I need a manager I was like are you even funny like I know you're from high school but I don't remember you being like funny enough that I would pay to see you tell jokes and he was like I have a show tonight and so I gave him a ride on the north side of town and the interesting part it was a predominantly black audience in Atlanta okay makes sense and he did these jokes and they laughed and it was one of these comedy clubs where if you're not funny you have like three seconds and they would just boo you off the stage and that's the end of it and he held it I was like wow you survived that that's incredible and you were pretty funny and then we drove about an hour north and the audience is predominantly white and so on the drive there I'm like there is no way you can do those jokes in this room I gotta see how this is gonna go and at the time they're only paying the comedians like $50 so you don't make a lot in the early days and he totally pivoted the set and he held that audience too and I was like all right I can be your manager I know business I know kind of logistics I know how to you know make a plan together and I did that for a number of years and You know, he won some televised competitions. We went on the road with bands like Earth, Wind & Fire and some large comedians from Kings of Comedy and Queens of Comedy. And I actually picked up some IT work with the company behind it called Latham Entertainment. They had been doing movies at this time. and so now I'm like you know doing IT for them and I got the small business I got the comedian and so look I was able to save a lot of money but man luckily I was 20 years old because I had all this energy but I was working quite a bit eventually you settle down you get a family and you do the math and it turns out people kind of over glorify entrepreneurship I think a lot of people believe there is tremendous upside right the type of entrepreneurship we talk about with software companies the upside is crazy But when you're doing like selling parts or service business, unless you plan to open lots of stores and grow a larger employee base, it's not the same growth trajectory as software companies. And so I kind of did the math. After four years of doing that, I said, look, I want to settle down. And if you've been an entrepreneur before, you know employees get paid first. The owner gets paid last. And there are months where you get paid last or you don't get paid at all. And now you're kind of drawing from savings because it's not their problem. But I did well overall. Like I did very successful. But I remember it's like, you know, I think I'm ready. And so I looked around and Google had data centers nearby, and I felt like I had a great combination of skills. I understood, you know, the racking stack part of the world. I understood the physical part of the networking stack. I understood everything from Linux to Windows. I had an entrepreneur mindset. I didn't think there was nothing I could not do. and I remember going to that interview and they were hiring data center technicians. But what it paid, and in my mind I'm like, you only have to work for eight hours, you don't have to issue any invoices, and you get paid every two weeks no matter what? No inventory? This is crazy, no way they're doing this. And so I go and I remember doing that interview and I didn't know Linux that well. And luckily for me, I knew FreeBSD well. And as I'm answering the questions, I'm like, look, I am not an expert on Linux, not the way Google was asking these questions. And it's like three people on the other side of this table just rapid firing. And I remember I was like, I know FreeBSD. I swear I got lucky. This one of the interviews, I think they had a FreeBSD tattoo on their leg, the little Beastie logo. And I saw this logo, I'm like, oh, I'm saved. And so we started going down the FreeBSD questions. And I pass and I get this job in this data center. And it was good because it's like, hey, I'm working with my colleagues, but it felt a bit slow because you only get one job. You come in, you do this thing, and I got really good at it because to me, I kind of saw it as like a bit of a competition. Who is the best data center tech here? What do their metrics look like? How do I exceed their metrics? I want to learn how to do every particular thing in this data center because previously as a business owner, the more skills you have, the more money you can make. And then I just started switching jobs every three to six months because I just wanted to explore everything to just amass my abilities. And doing the math, I think every jump was like a 25% pay raise. I mean, coming from a small base, it didn't feel big at the time. But after a few jumps, my salary doubled.
text: 其实在那段时间里，我在当店主的同时，还做过一个喜剧演员的经纪人，我们还一起到处巡演。 所以你当时是在帮他做经纪？对，我有个学校里的朋友，他是个喜剧演员。后来发现他其实真的很厉害。我去俱乐部看他演出，他就说，嘿，我需要个经纪人。我当时还说，你真的好笑吗？我知道你是我高中同学，但我不记得你有好笑到让我愿意花钱看你讲段子。他说，我今晚有场演出。于是我开车送他去城北。有意思的是，那场在 Atlanta，观众基本上都是黑人。嗯，这也说得通。然后他讲那些段子，大家都笑了。那种喜剧俱乐部是这样的：如果你不好笑，三秒钟之内观众就会把你嘘下台，演出直接结束。但他稳住了全场。我当时想，哇，你居然能扛住这种场子，太厉害了，而且你确实挺好笑。接着我们又往北开了差不多一个小时，到了另一个场子，那边观众基本上都是白人。所以路上我就在想，你绝对不可能在这个场子里讲刚才那些段子，我得看看你打算怎么搞。那时候喜剧演员一场也就拿 50 美元，早期根本赚不到什么钱。结果他完全调整了整套内容，而且也把那个场子拿下来了。我就说，行，我可以当你的经纪人。我懂生意，也懂一些后勤安排，知道怎么把事情规划起来。后来我做了好几年。你知道的，他还赢过一些电视上的比赛。我们跟 Earth, Wind & Fire 这样的乐队，还有 Kings of Comedy、Queens of Comedy 里的一些大牌喜剧演员一起巡演。我还顺带接到了一些 IT 工作，是背后的那家公司，叫 Latham Entertainment。当时他们也在拍电影。所以那时候我一边给他们做 IT，一边经营自己的小生意，还带着这个喜剧演员。总之，我确实攒下了不少钱，不过说真的，还好那时我才 20 岁，精力特别旺盛，因为我真的忙得要命。后来人总会安定下来，成家，然后你开始算账，就会发现大家其实有点把创业神化了。我觉得很多人都相信创业有巨大的上行空间。没错，我们常说的软件公司那种创业，上行空间确实夸张。但如果你做的是卖零件，或者服务型生意，除非你打算开很多店、扩张员工规模，不然它的增长曲线跟软件公司根本不是一回事。所以我大概算了算。做了四年之后，我就想，行了，我想安定下来。而且如果你以前创业过，你就知道，员工先拿工资，老板最后拿。有些月份你是最后拿，甚至根本拿不到。然后你还得从存款里往外贴，因为这又不是员工的问题。不过整体上我做得很好，算是很成功。但我记得当时就觉得，我应该准备好了。于是我四处看看，发现 Google 附近有数据中心，我觉得自己的技能组合很合适。我懂机架和整套硬件那一块，懂网络栈里偏物理层的部分，也懂从 Linux 到 Windows 的各种东西。我还有创业者的心态，我觉得没什么是我做不了的。我记得去面试的时候，他们招的是数据中心技术员。但那个薪水，在我脑子里就是：你只要工作 8 小时，不用开发票，而且不管怎样每两周都能拿一次工资？还不用管库存？这也太离谱了吧，他们真这么干？于是我就去了。我记得那场面试里，我对 Linux 其实没那么熟。好在我对 FreeBSD 很熟。我一边回答问题一边说，你看，我不是 Linux 专家，至少不是 Google 这种问法下的专家。桌子对面坐着三个人，问题像连珠炮一样打过来。我记得我当时说，我懂 FreeBSD。我发誓我真是运气好，其中一个面试官，我记得他腿上好像有个 FreeBSD 的纹身，就是那个小 Beastie 标志。我一看到那个标志，就想，哦，得救了。然后我们就开始聊 FreeBSD 的问题。最后我通过了，拿到了数据中心这份工作。那工作挺不错，因为你会和同事一起干活，但节奏上又感觉有点慢，因为你一次就做一件事。你来上班，做这件事。我后来把这个做得特别好，因为对我来说，这有点像比赛。这里最厉害的数据中心技术员是谁？他们的指标是什么样？我怎么超过他们的指标？我想学会这个数据中心里每一项具体工作。因为以前当老板的时候，你会的技能越多，能赚的钱就越多。后来我就开始每三到六个月换一次岗位，因为我就是想把所有东西都摸一遍，把自己的能力尽可能攒起来。算下来，我觉得每跳一次大概就是 25% 的涨薪。虽然起点不高，当时感觉没那么夸张，但跳了几次之后，我的薪水翻倍了。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Puppet was a bit of an inflection point in your career.
text: Puppet 算是你职业生涯里的一个转折点。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: You know what? I would say the biggest inflection point in my career was there were two of them before I get into Puppet Labs. So you come from Google, you see this huge operation. There's hundreds of thousands of servers. The cables are perfect.
text: 你知道吗？要我说，我职业生涯里最大的转折点，其实在 Puppet Labs 之前就有两个。所以你从 Google 那样的大型体系出来，看到的是一个庞大的运作系统。那里有几十万台服务器，线缆也都整理得一丝不苟。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: They're immaculate. Oh, by the way, can you help us imagine like what a data center back then looked like at Google and what did you do as part of the job?
text: 整洁得离谱。对了，顺便能不能帮我们想象一下，当年 Google 的数据中心是什么样？你的工作具体又是做什么的？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, so in 2000, maybe four, maybe 2005, That data center is like a warehouse. I mean, it's huge. So think about a place where, and I'm pretty sure they always exaggerated the numbers. So a exaggerated number to think about is like, think about 200,000 servers in one place. everything is immaculate so a lot of people have worked in data centers and it's a mess wires are all over the place you know you're ad hoc adding and removing servers but Google was systematic those machines came off a truck they were wrapped perfectly when you wheeled them into their spot you connected the network cables they would pixie boot they would burn in so part of the job was you know you walked around with a crash cart so depending on what your abilities were some people had pretty straightforward jobs you had a crash cart had all your tools on it and You would walk around and you would find machines that were needing a repair. So if you have 200,000 machines, it's okay if like 300 of them are broken, right? The system can ride around that. But if they were broken, they needed to be repaired. So you would go to Rack A Server 7. You would pull it out and you would look at it and say, oh yeah, the SATA card is on fire. Like it's literally burned. It's burning. It was burnt. And so he's like, I can diagnose this one with my eyes. It needs to replace the SATA card. But the thing is, you would go into the system and you would say, this SATA controller needs to be replaced. So you will be making your prediction. and then you would replace it and you would go through its burning process. It would join back to the fleet. And then the way you were measured was how good were your predictions? Oh. Right. So you said it's a SATA controller. And to me, moving fast, you look as if I think that SATA controller, I'm not going to waste any more time on this one because I'm trying to get my numbers up. So I just swap the SATA controller. You bring in all the cables, you slide it back. It goes through its process and you move on to the next machine.
text: 可以。大概在 2004 年，也可能是 2005 年，那种数据中心就像一个仓库，真的非常大。你可以想象一个地方——当然，我很确定他们总会把数字说得夸张一点——所以你可以先按一个夸张的数字来想，比如一个地方有 20 万台服务器。所有东西都整整齐齐。很多人在数据中心工作过，知道那种地方往往乱糟糟的，线到处都是，服务器也是临时加一台、拆一台。但 Google 是系统化的。那些机器从卡车上卸下来时，包装得非常规整。你把它们推到对应位置，接上网线，它们会通过 PXE 启动，然后做 burn-in 测试。所以这份工作的一部分就是，你会推着一辆 crash cart 到处走。根据你的能力不同，有些人的工作会比较直接。你那辆 crash cart 上放着所有工具，然后你在机房里走来走去，找到那些需要维修的机器。如果你有 20 万台机器，那其中有 300 台坏了，其实也没关系，对吧？整个系统能扛得住。但坏了就得修。所以你会走到 A 机架第 7 台服务器，把它抽出来，看一眼，然后说，哦，对，SATA 卡烧了，是真的烧坏了，已经烧焦了。那种情况你用眼睛就能判断：这个得换 SATA 卡。但问题是，你要先在系统里登记，说这台机器需要更换 SATA 控制器。也就是说，你得先做出自己的判断。然后你把它换掉，再让它重新走一遍 burn-in 流程，最后重新加入整个机器集群。衡量你的方式就是：你的判断到底准不准？哦，原来如此。对。比如你说是 SATA 控制器。像我这种追求速度的人，看一眼就会觉得，应该就是 SATA 控制器，我不想在这台机器上浪费更多时间了，因为我得把自己的数字做上去。所以我就直接把 SATA 控制器换掉，把线都接回去，把机器推回机架，让它自己走流程，然后我就去修下一台。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Before you get the feedback.
text: 在你拿到反馈之前。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, and fun fact, there's a guy named Tim Hawkins, very popular in the Kubernetes community. He's like the network lead. So back then, Tim Hawkins is working at the other side of Google, at the bigger side of Google. And one of his first projects was a little tool that you would put on the motherboard. It had about nine lights on it. and the lights would flash back and forth and it would tell you what DIMM slots were potentially bad. The technicians that were fast, they didn't waste time like running a program to do an extensive test of the memory. You learn how to use this little device. and you would walk up to the motherboard. So the thing I would do is like, before I do anything, put this on the motherboard, get the reading, and I learned to trust it over time. Dim one, dim two are bad. And the goal would be, all right, I have all this memory on my cart. You swatch those two dim slots, make sure that that's done first, reboot the machine, and you might be like, I think that's the only thing wrong. And again, you were put in the system. I believe I only need to do DIMM 1 and 2. And the way you were measured is how long before that machine gets kicked back to repair. So if it doesn't get kicked back within, I don't know, 30, 60 days, you did a good job. If you didn't, your scores would be low. So for the technicians that were just reckless, they wouldn't even try. You're just swapping the wrong part. You're swapping the wrong hard drive. And so your stuff is always coming back for repair. You were not efficient. So I got to the point where I can maintain high 90s, but also repair Let's call it three times more machines than other people. So lots of machines, rate of return. And then I learned how to do the network switches. And there's power audits where you're lifting up tiles from the floor and you're making sure that everything looks good. You need to be careful not to touch them because you could die. And so you learn everything about a data center, like the service loop. You're running all this Cat5 cable. It has to have a perfect service loop. Fiber runs on a different part of the rack, right? So you don't ever mix these things. So as a person still, like, I'm in my early 20s, I'm thinking this is how all data centers look. It's not the case.
text: 对。顺便说个有意思的事，有个叫 Tim Hawkins 的人，在 Kubernetes 社区里很有名，他算是网络方向的负责人之一。那时候 Tim Hawkins 在 Google 的另一边、更大的那一边工作。他最早做的项目之一，是一个可以接到主板上的小工具，上面大概有 9 个灯。那些灯会来回闪，然后告诉你哪些 DIMM 插槽可能有问题。那些动作快的技术员，不会浪费时间去跑什么很全面的内存测试程序。你会学着用这个小设备。你走到主板前，我通常的做法是，先别急着干别的，先把这个东西接上去，读一下结果。时间久了，我也学会信它了。DIMM 1、DIMM 2 有问题。那目标就是，好，我手推车上有一堆内存条，先把这两个 DIMM 插槽对应的内存换掉，先把这件事做完，再重启机器。然后你可能会想，我觉得问题应该就这一个。然后你还是要在系统里登记：我认为只需要处理 DIMM 1 和 2。衡量你的方式是，这台机器多久之后会再次被打回维修。如果它在 30 天、60 天之内都没有再被打回来，那你这次就修得不错。如果做不到，你的分数就会很低。所以那些特别莽的技术员，甚至都懒得认真判断，就是乱换零件，换错部件，换错硬盘。结果他们修过的机器老是返修，效率就很差。后来我做到什么程度呢？我能把准确率维持在 90% 多，同时修的机器数量大概是别人的 3 倍。也就是说，修得多，返修率还低。然后我又学会了怎么处理网络交换机。还有电力审计，你得把地板砖掀起来，确认下面一切正常。你得非常小心，别碰到那些东西，因为真有可能会死。于是你会把数据中心里所有东西都学会，比如 service loop。你在布这些 Cat5 线缆的时候，service loop 必须做得非常标准。光纤走的是机架的另一侧，对吧？这些东西绝对不能混在一起。所以那时候我还只是个二十出头的人，我当时还以为所有数据中心都长这样。其实根本不是。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: But it's crazy because just as I think back, you know, I was a manager before as well, and of course a software engineer for a long time, but The way your performance was continuously measured and fed back to you and you were evaluated based on it, it feels way more strict, should I say, than, you know, like folks who work at software engineers, including at Google. I mean, the frequency, the expectations.
text: 但这真的很疯狂，因为我回头想想，你知道，我以前也当过经理，当然也做了很长时间的软件工程师，但你们那种持续被衡量绩效、持续收到反馈、并且据此被评估的方式，感觉要严格得多，我该这么说吧，比起那些做软件工程师的人——包括在 Google 的。我是说，那种频率，那种期待值。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: The reason why I didn't feel as bad as some of the metrics people are using now is because I felt like I can control the outcome. It didn't feel like it was a thing that was just a metric that didn't do anything. If I felt like my score was taking a hit, and I was like, you know what, I am being a bit sloppy in how I'm diagnosing these machines. And I remember one time where I almost had my score dip below 90, I started writing additional shell scripts to start and combining different functions together. It's like, you know what, I can't be moving this fast. There's a way for me to diagnose multiple things as a machine at one time. And so I would diagnose the SATA array, all the hard drives while I'm doing the memory component. And then when it rebooted, I would just run the script one more time as I'm putting my cart back together to catch that one more thing. And once I started doing that, I can move as fast as I want it and the scores are So to me, when the scores actually match the things that you're doing, then it's a healthy feedback. And again, no one really talked about it unless you needed to talk about it. And so I kind of leveraged it for a personal thing. I pulled it up in the morning. I kind of looked at my performance metrics and in many ways I calibrated my strategy based on this detailed feedback that I was getting. So I think I appreciated that level, that granularity back then because it felt like it was something that was helpful for me, not just my manager.
text: 我之所以没觉得像现在有些人用的那些指标那么糟，是因为我觉得结果是我自己能控制的。它不像那种只是个指标、实际上没什么作用的东西。如果我感觉自己的分数在掉，我就会想，你知道吗，我在诊断这些机器的时候确实有点粗糙了。我记得有一次，我的分数差点掉到 90 以下，我就开始多写一些 shell 脚本，把不同的功能组合起来。我当时想，不能再这么硬冲速度了。我其实有办法让一台机器同时做多项诊断。于是我会在检查内存组件的同时，去诊断 SATA 阵列和所有硬盘。然后机器重启的时候，我会一边把我的推车重新整理好，一边再跑一次脚本，把最后那一项也补上。等我开始这么做之后，我想跑多快都可以，分数也很稳。所以对我来说，当这些分数真的能对应到你实际在做的事情时，那它就是一种健康的反馈。而且再说一次，除非有必要，不然其实也没人老拿这个来说事。所以我某种程度上把它当成了一个个人工具。我早上会把它调出来，看一下自己的绩效指标，很多时候我会根据这些细致的反馈来校准自己的策略。所以我觉得，当时我很欣赏那种层次、那种颗粒度，因为它让我感觉这东西对我有帮助，不只是对我的经理有帮助。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: When you said you had two kind of big inflection points, one of them was
text: 你刚才说你有两个比较大的转折点，其中一个是

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Google was a big one like that was definitely one of course my entrepreneurship and when I got to web hosting I went to a company called Pier One they were a spin-off of Rackspace and they were all about fully automated self-hosting right so this was back in like what 2005, 6,
text: Google 是一个很大的转折点，那个肯定算一个。当然还有我自己创业的经历。后来我进入虚拟主机行业，去了一个叫 Pier One 的公司，它是 Rackspace 分拆出来的，他们当时主打的是全自动化自助托管，对吧，所以那大概是 2005、2006 年那会儿。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: 7? Yeah this is like yeah 2005, 2006 oh they were already all about full automated that was their thing their
text: 2007？对，这差不多就是 2005、2006 年。哦，他们那时候就已经在搞全自动化了？那就是他们的主打方向，他们的

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: tagline was latency kills And so back then you would go online, a lot of the customer base was like people hosting their own game servers, right? So if you wanted to play a game, one thing you could do back then is host a game server, but you needed a game server that multiple people could hit. And so you would go along to server beach, so this is a spin out of Rackspace. So Rackspace is more like, you know, we'll buy a server and once you get it, it's a lot of manual steps. Rackspace was more of a let's automate everything so the machine would pixie some PHP scripts would run if you ordered a RAID setup then we would configure the RAID while the machine was netbooted and then we would put you on the VLAN that you belonged on We would install the right operating system based on what you've ordered. And we just took a form. We just went through all of these steps. And then when we were done, and it took maybe about an hour, when we were done, you had an IP address, login credentials. And if you wanted email and plus, you know, website management, all these things, you got it. And when you were done, you gave it back. And then we put it back into the pool, ready for the next customer. And so when I saw how we were doing that, I was like, yo, this is... Okay, you can automate things end-to-end. The other thing that was important here, we were doing things like updating the firmware for RAID controllers because once you pixie a server, now you're in memory and you have access to all the hardware. You haven't committed to an operating system yet, but you have enough to do whatever you want. So if you want to configure the RAID controller, And back then there wasn't like clean APIs. We were literally running curl scripts and command line utilities trying to get this machine into the right shape. And then when we were done, we would put it into the fleet. So at that early age, I'm like, oh, there is nothing you cannot do. When we had to automate the Windows 2000 servers back then, we would just build tools that would literally screen scrape, log in to Active Directory. And then we would screen scrape mouse movements so that way we can patch software on those Windows servers. So the concept of like, I need a specific tool to do is like, no, back then it was like, you do whatever's necessary because these people are only paying like $99 a month. They don't have time for you to spend a whole week. When they call, random customer, you don't know their setup. You don't know their infrastructure. You have minutes to get them back online. So everybody learned to move quickly. No complaining. When you get that ticket, it's on you to figure it out. Maybe you link to your teammates for help. So I kind of learned how to move fast. But the inflection point came from I started that job in tech support. So people would call. My MySQL isn't working. DNS isn't working. They can't even describe what isn't working sometimes. And so I realized that we were all in the phone queue. When the phone would ring, it would just round robin between everyone. Then if you couldn't solve it fast enough or you couldn't solve it at all, you created a ticket. and when the tickets sat there and then ideally you get to it later but the ticket queue was just getting long and when a shift change happened we just had all these tickets piling up and of course customers are now mad three days no response so one day I said look I'm just not gonna log in the queue. I'm just gonna resolve the tickets. And I'm back in that entrepreneurial mindset. I'm building little scripts to take a ticket. I see the issue. This is my SQL issue. We need to vacuum the database. This is easy. Run that one. Ticket close. Hey, sir, everything is good to go. Please try again. Close. Oh, this is not even an issue. Close. Plus, oh, we've got upgrade PHP. Close. And then the ticket queue is zero. It's just empty all day. And so someone's like, hey, Kelsey's not logged into the phone queue. He's not even on the phone. He's not taking calls.
text: 宣传语是“latency kills”。那时候你会上网，很多客户其实都是自己托管游戏服务器的人，对吧？如果你想玩游戏，当时有一种做法就是自己托管一个游戏服务器，但你需要的是一个能让很多人同时连上的游戏服务器。所以你会去 Server Beach，这也是 Rackspace 分拆出来的。Rackspace 更像是这样：我们给你买一台服务器，但你拿到之后，后面还有很多手工步骤。Rackspace 更偏向于“把一切都自动化”——机器会通过 PXE 启动，跑一些 PHP 脚本；如果你下单时选了 RAID 配置，那我们就在机器通过网络启动的时候把 RAID 配好；然后我们会把你放到你所属的 VLAN 上；再根据你下单的内容安装正确的操作系统。我们基本上就是接一个表单，然后把这些步骤全走一遍。大概一个小时左右，全部做完之后，你就拿到一个 IP 地址、登录凭据。如果你还想要邮箱，再加上网站管理之类的这些东西，你也都能拿到。等你用完了，把机器还回来，我们再把它放回资源池，准备给下一个客户。所以当我看到我们是怎么做这些事的时候，我就想，哇，这就……原来事情真的可以端到端自动化。还有一件很重要的事是，我们当时还会做像 RAID 控制器固件升级这种事情，因为一旦你通过 PXE 启动了一台服务器，你其实就在内存环境里了，而且你已经能访问所有硬件。你还没有真正提交到某个操作系统，但你已经有足够的能力去做任何你想做的事。所以如果你想配置 RAID 控制器——而且那时候也没有什么干净的 API——我们真的是在跑 curl 脚本和命令行工具，想尽办法把这台机器调整到正确状态。做完之后，我们再把它放进整个机器集群里。所以我在很早的时候就意识到，哦，原来没有什么事情是做不到的。那时候我们还得把 Windows 2000 服务器也自动化，我们就自己造工具，真的去做屏幕抓取，登录到 Active Directory，然后再通过抓取鼠标移动来打补丁、更新那些 Windows 服务器。所以那种“我需要一个特定工具才能做这件事”的概念，在当时根本不是重点。那时候的思路是：不管需要什么手段，你都得把事做成。因为这些客户一个月只付 99 美元，他们没时间让你花一整周去处理。客户一打电话过来，往往是个随机客户，你根本不知道他们的配置，不知道他们的基础设施。你只有几分钟时间让他们恢复上线。所以每个人都学会了快速行动，别抱怨。工单到了你手上，你就得自己想办法搞定。也许你会找队友帮忙。所以我就是那时候学会了怎么快速推进事情。不过真正的转折点在于，我一开始那份工作是在技术支持。客户会打电话来说，我的 MySQL 不工作了，DNS 不工作了，有时候他们甚至都说不清到底哪里不工作。然后我意识到，我们所有人都在电话队列里。电话一响，就按轮询分给每个人。如果你没法很快解决，或者根本解决不了，你就创建一个工单。然后工单就堆在那里，理想情况下你之后再处理，但工单队列只会越来越长。等到交班的时候，工单就越积越多。客户当然就生气了，三天都没人回复。有一天我说，算了，我不登录电话队列了。我就专门去解决工单。我又回到了那种创业者思维，开始写一些小脚本来处理工单。我看到问题：这是个 MySQL 问题，需要整理数据库，这很简单，跑一下，工单关闭。‘先生，一切已经恢复正常了，请再试一次。’关闭。‘哦，这个其实根本不是问题。’关闭。‘另外，这里需要升级 PHP。’关闭。然后工单队列就清零了，整天都是空的。于是就有人说，嘿，Kelsey 没登录电话队列，他根本没在接电话。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: my manager pulls me in the office like hey Kelsey why are you not on the phone
text: 我的经理把我叫进办公室，说，嘿 Kelsey，你为什么不接电话

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: queue I said because we don't all need to be on the phone queue we can just have one person making the ticket queue stay at zero. And then I would tell my colleagues, hey, look, if you can't figure it out fast, just open the ticket super quickly. I will take care of it. So some people specialize in Windows. They got a Linux call. They didn't know what to do. I said, don't worry about it. Just put it in the queue quickly. Move on to the next call. I got you. And so I became super efficient. So I explained this process to the manager. He thought about it. His name is Mike. And Mike was like, yeah, I like that. We're going to change it. We're going to have a couple people stay out of the queue. But the problem is, that queue has to stay zero. And it's the first time I learned the difference between activities and impact. Activity is you being an all-star answering a bunch of calls, but the ticket queue for the team is still high. You making this jump and saying, look, maybe I stay out of the queue, and my promise to my colleagues is impact the ticket queue is zero so on Wednesday when we do the team turnover we're handing off an empty queue now of course I didn't teach them that because I didn't think about it and when we would come in the queue would be high again it's like yo whoa this is not you guys keep handing off a bunch of burden to our team. And of course, we would clean it up. But then the management team was like, yo, everybody's going to do it. And then I was like, man, you can change the process. So that was like the huge inflection point. I think the next one was more about being a mature person. Stop job hopping so much. Because again, after maybe a few promotions and some impact, I'm now off to the next thing. and I didn't have to necessarily live a long time with some of those decisions or stay long enough to really impact more of the culture. So when I got to financial services, it was the first time I got restrictions put on me, right? Because in these companies, everything is about moving fast. Google, we got to move fast. We have to compete with the big guys. We're going to do a cheap hardware, smart people, any means necessary. Web hosting, we're not charging a lot. Margins are thin. You got to move quick. Financial service is like, no, we making money over here.
text: 队列？我说，因为我们没必要所有人都待在电话队列里。只要有一个人专门把工单队列维持在零就行了。然后我会跟同事们说，嘿，你们如果没法很快搞定，就赶紧开工单，我来处理。有些人专精 Windows，结果接到 Linux 的电话，不知道怎么办。我就说，别担心，赶紧把它放进队列里，继续接下一个电话，我来兜底。于是我变得超级高效。我把这个流程解释给经理听。他想了想，他叫 Mike。Mike 说，嗯，我喜欢这个思路。我们要改流程。我们会安排几个人不进电话队列。但问题是，那个工单队列必须保持为零。那也是我第一次真正理解“活动”和“影响”的区别。活动，是你像个明星员工一样接了很多电话，但团队的工单队列还是很高。影响，是你做出这个跳跃，说，听着，也许我不进电话队列，但我对同事们的承诺是：结果就是工单队列清零，这样到周三我们团队交接班的时候，交出去的是一个空队列。当然，我当时并没有把这套东西教给他们，因为我自己也没认真想过这个层面。结果等我们再来上班时，队列又高起来了。我就想，喂，等等，这不对啊，你们一直把一堆负担甩给我们团队。当然，我们还是会把它清掉。但后来管理层就说，行，所有人都要这么做。那时候我就意识到，原来流程是可以改变的。所以那是一个巨大的转折点。我觉得下一个转折点更多是关于人成熟了。别再那么频繁跳槽了。因为说到底，在经历了几次晋升、做出一些影响之后，我又跑去做下一件事了。我不一定会长期承受自己某些决定带来的后果，也没有待得够久，去真正影响更深层的文化。所以当我进入金融服务行业时，那是我第一次真正被加上各种限制，对吧？因为在之前那些公司里，一切都围绕着快速行动。Google，我们得快，我们得跟大公司竞争，我们要用便宜硬件、聪明的人，不惜一切手段。虚拟主机行业也是，我们收费不高，利润薄，你就得快。金融服务行业则是：不，我们这边是在赚钱的。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: You joined the financial services company?
text: 你后来加入了那家金融服务公司？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah so that was the first time my salary doubled so I went on my lunch break for a job interview That must have felt awesome Oh my god I remember my manager was so upset I went on a lunch break.
text: 对，那是我第一次工资直接翻倍，所以我午休的时候跑去参加了个面试。那感觉肯定爽爆了。天哪，我记得我经理当时特别不高兴，因为我午休跑出去了。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: The previous manager, the one you left.
text: 就是你离开的那位前任经理。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yes, I met Pier One. He was a good friend of mine named Joe Rodriguez. He's the first person to make me a software developer. So I showed him some ideas I had on like modernizing our virtualization, optimizing our pixie boot process. And he was like, man, you have a lot of good ideas. So in the interview, he's like, show me what you built. I'm so excited. I'm still that entrepreneur thinking. And I got the job. So now I'm a software engineer working on this automation stack. And I remember seeing jobs that I used to be afraid of five years ago, the same jobs that made me want to just go get an A-plus certification and open a computer store instead of even trying. And so I looked at those job descriptions again, and it was like, you need Linux. I was like, I got that. Even at that job, I got Red Hat certified, right? So I was like, I got all the qualifications now. And I remember the job. I didn't know how much it paid before I went, but you had to wear a shirt and tie. It was the first time in my whole life and wear a shirt and tie. So you have to go and buy one.
text: 对，我是在 Pier One 遇到他的。他是我的一个好朋友，叫 Joe Rodriguez。他是第一个真正让我成为软件开发者的人。所以我给他看了我一些想法，比如怎么把我们的虚拟化做现代化改造，怎么优化我们的 PXE 启动流程。他就说，哥们，你这些想法真不错。所以面试的时候，他就说，给我看看你做过什么。我当时特别兴奋，我脑子里还是那种创业者思维。然后我拿到了这份工作。于是我就成了一名软件工程师，开始做这套自动化技术栈。我记得那时候又看到了那些五年前我还会害怕去投的岗位，就是那些曾经让我只想去考个 A+ 认证、开家电脑店、连试都不敢试的工作。后来我再看那些职位描述，就写着，你得会 Linux。我心想，这我会啊。甚至在那份工作里，我还拿了 Red Hat 认证，对吧？所以我就想，我现在资格都齐了。我还记得那次面试，我去之前都不知道薪资多少，但你得穿衬衫打领带。这是我人生第一次要穿衬衫打领带，所以还得专门去买一套。

speaker: Kelsey

I'm driving up on my lunch break and I get there and this is enterprise. I'm like, wow, this is the big league. I didn't even think Google was big league. I thought financial services bigly obviously if you have to wear a tie to do your job you must be doing something so serious that you need a shirt and tie to do it and so I get to the interview and I'm sweating like they're going to ask me stuff that I've probably never heard or seen before and they started asking Linux questions I'm like No, you gotta use this flag. That's not the right flag. No, look it up. That is not the right flag. You can't do that with grip. No, you gotta pipe it this way. The PS table doesn't show you that. They're on that version of Linux. Not with that kernel. And I'm like, it's too easy. And part of me is like, either I'm really good or something else. And so I'm thinking like, yo, that was, maybe there's another round. And so I'm driving home and I'm loosening up the tie and I'm calling my wife like hey I think I did a really great job like you know and this has been a pattern you know I get good at something and I find a better job I got good at something I found a better job but now it's like this is like a career and so I'm talking to my wife and I was like hey I gotta call you back and it's the recruiter calling hey you have a second it's like 100% they want to make you an offer I'm like Make your offer is different. It's usually this is how much the job pays. That's how it was until then. How much were you making?
text: 我还记得我家政课老师还帮我把裤脚给缝好了。我当时还问她，嘿，我有个工作面试，这样穿对吗？我午休时开车过去，到了那儿一看，这可是企业级环境。我心想，哇，这才是大联盟。我那时候甚至都不觉得 Google 算大联盟，我觉得金融服务行业才是真的高端。很明显嘛，如果你上班都得打领带，那你做的事肯定严肃到必须穿衬衫打领带才行。然后我到了面试现场，紧张得直冒汗，心想他们肯定会问一些我从没听过、没见过的东西。结果他们开始问 Linux 问题，我心想，不对，这里得用这个 flag。不是，那个 flag 不对。不是，你查一下，那真不是对的 flag。你不能用 grep 那么搞。不，你得这样 pipe 过去。PS 表里看不到那个。你说的是那个 Linux 版本？那个 kernel 下可不行。然后我就想，这也太简单了吧。我心里一部分在想，要么是我真的很强，要么就是别的什么情况。所以我还在想，哟，这可能后面还有下一轮。然后我开车回家，一边松领带，一边给我老婆打电话，说，嘿，我觉得我这次表现特别好。你知道，这已经成了一个模式了：我把一件事做熟了，就找到一份更好的工作；再把一件事做熟了，又找到一份更好的工作。但这次不一样了，这次感觉像是真正的职业生涯了。我正跟我老婆说着，突然说，嘿，我得先挂一下，是猎头打来的。喂，你现在方便说两句吗？这基本就是 100% 要给你发 offer 了。我当时想，给 offer 可不一样。以前通常都是直接告诉你，这份工作薪资是多少。一直到那时候都是这样。你那时候赚多少来着？

text_en: 45k. So you were making 45k at that point. $90,000. I almost drove off the road.
text: 4万5。所以你那时候年薪是 4万5。9万美元。我差点把车开出路。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Like double. This is insane. I'm thinking 90 and you're doing the retirement calculations. We can buy a house. You're thinking of all of these things that you could do. and so I'm on the way back to the job and I remember and they were like when can you start it's like Thursday I'm telling them we can start Monday like uh two week notice I'm like I don't know man like it's double for this I have to so I remember I called Joe and Joe's based in San Antonio where the headquarters of rec space was I was like hey Joe man hey man I gotta I gotta I gotta quit he's like what man you
text: 直接翻倍了。这太离谱了。你脑子里想着 9 万，然后已经开始算退休金了。我们可以买房了。你会开始想所有这些能做到的事。所以我当时正在回公司的路上，我记得他们还问我，你什么时候能入职？那天是周四，我直接跟他们说我周一就能开始。什么提前两周通知？我当时想，我也不知道啊，哥们，这可是翻倍，我必须得去。所以我记得我给 Joe 打了电话，Joe 当时在 San Antonio，那边是 Rackspace 总部。我说，嘿 Joe，哥们，那个，我得，我得，我得辞职了。他说，什么，哥们，你

text_en: And so I got to that company, Total Systems T-Sys. And it did feel a bit slow. They had run books, changed windows. everything was regulated this is a financial institution we're processing credit card payments we're doing work for the government we're doing all of these things and I remember the team was just everybody just moving at this pace but isn't it
text: 后来我去了那家公司，Total Systems，T-Sys。确实感觉节奏有点慢。他们有操作手册、变更窗口，一切都被严格规范着。这是一家金融机构，我们在处理信用卡支付，也在给政府做项目，做的都是这类事情。我记得那个团队里的人，大家做事的节奏都很慢，但这不就是——

speaker: Gergely

text: 很离谱吗？就是你进去之后，我也有过类似经历。我搬去 London 的时候，工资比在 Edinburgh 的时候翻了一倍多。你会有很多期待，因为人家给你高那么多薪水。对我来说也是一样，还得打领带。结果进去之后反而有点失望。我在想，你当时会不会也有点那种感觉：是不是我漏掉了什么？这里本来应该更——你懂的——更高级一点？面试还行，可能没那么难，但就是——

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: something I mean I did because I was naive because I didn't understand the consequences of a mistake And so, yeah, I was like, oh, these people are just moving slow. And I looked at what they were doing and everything was like a risk. If you made a mistake, you remember, you only get seven milliseconds, seven seconds to give Visa a response. If you don't, then it's a default decline. Now everybody's losing money. And so the cost of getting a good change, it was just worth waiting. So I didn't know that in the beginning. So I'm just like, oh, everybody's moving a little too slow. I was seeing how they were doing deployments. I'm watching how they're provisioning servers. I'm like, you know, you can automate this whole thing because I've done it multiple times. And then I learned to be a little bit patient. So I went, hold on. I learned how to deal with the nose. I learned how to deal with the executives. I learned how to talk not just to engineers, but to the senior leaders and get their trust. And I won't tick about everything that I did there, but I was there for about three years. So the job had me stops. And I remember there was a task. where we were using Apache and some Java plugin to talk to our JBoss instances. And so they were using a lot of memory per connection on the load balancer. And so we got a new, we're moving off the mainframe. We're moving into this new, you know, Java world and the servers just kept falling over. We can handle all the transactions. And I'm just watching the team go into these change windows and then we fail, we would fail, we would fail. And the CTO at the time was like, you know, this is costing us money. We're getting chargebacks. We're having to pay out penalties. And I was like, I had a dev environment when I was using Nginx. I got rid of all this Java stuff. Like, it's just HTTP. You don't need a Java connector thing. I'm reading the spec. You don't need it. And my memory usage is a fraction.
text: 总归是有那种感觉。我当时确实这么想，因为我那时候很天真，不理解犯错会带来什么后果。所以对，我当时就觉得，哦，这些人动作也太慢了。我看他们做的事情，发现每一步其实都是风险。你想，你只有 7 毫秒，不对，7 秒钟给 Visa 返回响应。如果没返回，那就会默认拒绝。这样一来大家都在亏钱。所以，确保一次变更是正确的，这件事本身就值得你多等一等。我一开始并不知道这些，所以我就觉得，哦，大家是不是都太慢了。我看他们怎么做部署，看他们怎么配置服务器，我心想，这整套东西其实都可以自动化，因为我以前做过很多次。后来我学会了更有耐心一点。所以我就停下来想了想。我学会了怎么面对别人说不，学会了怎么和高管打交道，也学会了不只是跟工程师沟通，还要跟高级领导沟通并赢得他们的信任。那段时间我做了很多事，我就不一一细讲了，但我在那里大概待了三年。那份工作确实让我成长了很多。我记得当时有个任务，我们在用 Apache 和某个 Java 插件去跟 JBoss 实例通信。所以他们在负载均衡器上，每个连接都会占用很多内存。后来我们拿到了一套新的系统，我们要从 mainframe 迁出来，进入这个新的 Java 世界，结果服务器一直在宕，根本扛不住所有交易量。我就看着团队一次次进变更窗口，然后失败、失败、还是失败。当时的 CTO 就说，这事正在让我们赔钱，我们在被收拒付费用，还得支付罚金。我就说，我在开发环境里用的是 Nginx，我把这些 Java 的东西全去掉了。说到底这就是 HTTP，你根本不需要什么 Java 连接器。我在看规范，真的不需要。而且我的内存占用只有原来的一小部分。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: By getting rid of it.
text: 就是把它去掉之后？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, you don't need it. And Nginx had a better threading model, all of these things. And I was like, I got a perfect config. I've ported the Apache config to this one. I even handle all of our redirects, all of our routes, all the legacy cruft. This thing will work. And it's like, I don't know, Kels, this is not certified stuff. It's like, no, no, no, I got it from Red Hat. RPM install Nginx. It's in Red Hat. And they're like, okay, it's in Red Hat? Okay, sure. I said, just give me one chance.
text: 对，根本不需要。而且 Nginx 的线程模型更好，诸如此类的优势都有。我就说，我这边已经有一份完美配置了。我把 Apache 的配置迁到这边来了，连我们所有的重定向、所有路由、所有历史遗留的烂摊子我都处理好了。这东西肯定能跑。结果他们说，我不知道啊，Kels，这不是经过认证的东西。我说，不不不，我这是从 Red Hat 来的，RPM 安装 Nginx。它就在 Red Hat 里。然后他们就说，哦，在 Red Hat 里？那行吧。我说，你们就给我一次机会。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: And about after a week, they gave me a shot.
text: 大概过了一周，他们给了你一次机会。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: and they let me be in the change window. And what is the change window? So change window typically in the financial institution is like, we can start changes at midnight and we have to be finished by 6 a.m. And you have to notify every customer. We're going to change something in the environment. Things may get a little weird. We would like your permission. And if it's something that can be detrimental to your business, now's your time to speak up. So once we got permission, we had a window. that's the only window you got and if you can't finish it on time you gotta shut it down and get us back to where we were yeah roll back roll forward or if you could roll back and I remember I put Nginx in place so at this point it's all you no one at this company has Nginx experience also most people don't want to do this they're like hey we think this is a bad idea and so now it's your reputation on the line There is no hiding behind the team. It's almost like everyone's sitting back like, you got it. Now, luckily the leadership was like, we are supporting you and we're putting our careers on the line as well. I mean, they probably would have been safe. But after about two hours, I got everything working and we just watched your memory pressure just drop. So if we were at 90% on utilizing, let's call it 32 gigs and we're just blowing stack every time everything's crashing, this thing is hovering at like, Two gigs of RAM. And everyone's like, yo, like, are we getting peak load? Is this legit? And we're all just sitting there like not sure. And the way we used to test the platform is we would, someone would drive to the gas station, get a credit card, buy some gas. We want to see the transaction land in the Oracle database. Yeah, you could track it. So we can track the whole thing with like, yo, it works. And so the thing is, you can't really be comfortable until about 10 o'clock the next day. So now it's 3 a.m. We all go home. I don't go to sleep. I'm like, yo, we've changed a big part of the infrastructure in production. Let's see. So 10 o'clock goes by and we're looking around and there's no Nagios alerts going off. I'm like, man, we might be good. You know me, I got top going. I'm looking like memory usage is holding steady.
text: 他们还让我进了变更窗口。那什么叫变更窗口呢？一般在金融机构里，变更窗口通常就是：我们可以从午夜开始做变更，必须在早上 6 点前全部完成。而且你得通知每一位客户：我们要对环境做一些改动，期间可能会有点异常，我们希望得到你们的许可。如果这件事可能会对你们的业务造成影响，现在就是你们提出异议的时候。所以一旦拿到许可，我们就有了一个窗口期。那也是你唯一的窗口，如果你没法按时做完，就得立刻停掉，把系统恢复到原来的状态。对，要么回滚，要么往前修；前提是你还能回滚。我记得我当时把 Nginx 部署上去了，所以到了那个阶段，就全靠你自己了。公司里没有任何人有 Nginx 经验，而且大多数人其实也不想做这件事，他们会说，嘿，我们觉得这是个馊主意。于是现在就变成你的个人声誉押在上面了。你没法躲在团队后面。几乎就像所有人都往后一靠，说，行，你来吧。好在管理层当时是支持我的，他们说，我们支持你，我们也把自己的职业生涯押上了。我的意思是，他们大概率其实不会有事。但大概两个小时后，我把一切都跑通了，然后我们就眼看着内存压力一路往下掉。比如说，原来我们 32G 内存的利用率大概在 90%，每次一上负载就直接爆栈，整个系统都在崩。结果这个东西跑起来以后，内存占用大概稳定在 2G RAM 左右。大家都在说，哇，我们现在拿到的是峰值负载吗？这是真的吗？而我们所有人就坐在那里，也不太确定。我们以前测试平台的方式是这样的：有人开车去加油站，拿信用卡加个油。我们想看到这笔交易落到 Oracle 数据库里。对，你可以一路追踪。所以我们就能把整个流程都跟下来，然后说，哟，能跑通。问题是，真正放心下来得等到第二天大概 10 点。所以那时候是凌晨 3 点，我们都回家了。我没睡，我心想，哟，我们刚刚在生产环境里改了基础设施里很大的一块，看看情况吧。等到 10 点过去，我们四处看了看，Nagios 也没有任何告警。我就想，哥们，可能真成了。你也知道我，我一直开着 top，看着内存使用情况，发现它一直很稳。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Yeah, top is... For those who don't know that, that's when you chose the CPU. You chose the PS tree in a loop, right? So just like you ran the PSAUX command, you see all the processes running.
text: 对，top……给不了解的人解释一下，这就是你用来看 CPU 的那个工具。你相当于是在循环看 PS 树，对吧？就像你跑了 ps aux 命令，能看到所有正在运行的进程。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: And so you just run top and then it's basically that every second being refreshed or whatever timer you give it. But one of the bets I made was if I get this to work, you have to buy pizza for the whole floor for a week. Every day we get a new order. and because he promised like Kelsey if you get this to work I'll buy you a steak dinner and at the time I still am I was vegetarian so I don't eat steak but here's what I would like instead you got to buy pizza for the whole floor now I don't know how strategic I was thinking but I figured that I could score some points with the whole team from turning into a I succeeded to a we succeeded and then people eating together is one good way of doing it and I remember after it worked he was like what's the order and I just ordered pizza from one place and just put it in the middle. Everyone's like, what's the pizza for? Because usually you only get this for special events, like for the thing we did. And the next day, some more pizza and some more pizza. And so when I got that accomplishment, I was like, okay, this is what maturity looks like. It's not about just having the right solution. You literally have to get consensus, buy-in, make sure and then your reputation's on the line. And I got that change and it just kind of changed the way I thought about what success looked like. And so that was the inflection point. And then of course I brought Puppet into that organization, repeated a similar process of automating everything with this tool.
text: 对，所以你就跑 top，它基本上就是每秒刷新一次，或者按你设定的时间间隔刷新。不过我当时下了个赌注：如果我把这事做成了，你就得给整层楼的人买一周披萨。每天都得来一单。因为他当时承诺说，Kelsey，如果你把这事搞定了，我请你吃牛排大餐。可那时候我不吃牛排——其实我现在也还是素食主义者——所以我说，那我想要别的：你得给整层楼买披萨。现在回头看，我也不知道自己当时是不是想得很有策略，但我觉得这样可以帮我在整个团队里刷点好感，把“我成功了”变成“我们成功了”。而且大家一起吃东西，确实是个不错的办法。我记得事情成了之后，他问我，点什么？我就从一家店订了披萨，直接摆在中间。大家都在问，这披萨是干嘛的？因为一般只有特殊场合才会有这种待遇，比如庆祝我们做成了什么事。结果第二天又有披萨，再下一天还有披萨。所以当我拿下这个成果的时候，我就在想，好吧，这才叫成熟。不是说你有正确的解决方案就够了。你真的得拿到共识、拿到支持、把各方面都确认好，然后再把自己的声誉押上去。我完成了那次变更，这也彻底改变了我对“成功是什么样子”的理解。所以那就是我的转折点。后来当然我又把 Puppet 引进了那家公司，用类似的方式把所有东西都自动化了。

text_en: Kelsey just talked about what CICD looked like in the 2010s and how difficult deploying to prod was, which leads us nicely to our episode sponsor, Buildkite. This type of manual deploy era was the time when Buildkite was created. Airbnb, Canva, Uber and OpenAI have all been using Buildkite for 7 to 12 years. The same architecture that observed Shopify's traffic at scale five to ten years ago now absorbed the share push from Entropic, Cursor, Meta, Mistral, Cohere, VLM, XAI, Lambda and more who are all BuildKite customers. Where other CIs are cracking under the weight, BuildKite is running about 1.4 billion job minutes a week. Agents run on your infrastructure or on BuildKite's. Every artifact and log is captured. And when something fails, you see the why immediately. If you're hitting ceilings on your existing CI or downtime is becoming more problematic for your delivery, head to buildkat.com slash pragmatic. 30-day all-access trial, no credit card, and an actual human engineer on the standby. His name is Ola and he is very helpful. I'd also like to mention our season sponsor, Sentry. AI agents don't have a feedback loop from production observability logs. But should they have it? I mean, we probably don't want to build a system where after each production error, AI agents automatically push out a fix into production without supervision. That would be a recipe for disaster. But what if we did something more modest? When a production error fires, an agent investigates this with context from Sentry. Sentry already has all the context in the error after all. Sentry MCP is one way to plug in Sentry to agents that support the model context protocol. Cloud Code, Cursor, Codex, VS Code and Copilot, they all do. After you hook up the MCP server, you can do some very useful things. For example, you could do this. When an already resolved Sentry issue resurfaces, you can kick off a cursor agent to investigate their regression, read the relevant code, and open a PR with a suggested fix. There's a little work involved to get all of this going. You need to connect Sentry to your code repository, add Sentry MCP to cursor, Define the instruction for cursor's agent to investigate Configure the trigger that launches the automation and test that it all works. But once you have it up and running, you can get regressions fixed faster whilst they're reviewing every and all fixes. This feels like a sensible and healthy use of AI, NCP, and Sentry to me. Check out Sentry at sentry.io slash pragmatic and start monitoring and fixing regressions today. And with this, let's get back to Kelsey and when he introduced Puppet into his organization and automated DevOps processes.
text: Kelsey 刚刚讲的是 2010 年代的 CI/CD 是什么样子，以及把东西部署到生产环境有多难。这也正好把我们带到本期节目的赞助商 Buildkite。那种手工部署的时代，也正是 Buildkite 诞生的时候。各个工程组织当时都在想办法提高发布频率，不想再停留在一周发一次，而最先撞上天花板的那些团队，就转向了 Buildkite。Shopify 从 2015 年开始就在用 Buildkite。Airbnb、Canva、Uber 和 OpenAI 也都已经用了 7 到 12 年。整个 2010 年代，全球软件行业领导者遇到的那些最难的 CI 问题，很多都是在 Buildkite 的流水线上解决的。这也正是为什么 Buildkite 这个 CI 平台，对当下正在发生的事情最有感知。GenAI 吞吐量正在把所有人的构建队列推到极限。编码 agent 提交 commit 的频率，已经是你默认配置原本设计承载量的 5 倍、10 倍、甚至 50 倍。五到十年前，正是同样的架构承接了 Shopify 大规模流量；而现在，它又吸收了 Anthropic、Cursor、Meta、Mistral、Cohere、VLM、XAI、Lambda 等客户带来的推送洪峰，这些也全都是 Buildkite 的客户。当别的 CI 在重压之下开始崩的时候，Buildkite 每周仍然在跑大约 14 亿分钟的 job。Agent 可以跑在你的基础设施上，也可以跑在 Buildkite 的基础设施上。每一个 artifact 和日志都会被完整捕获。一旦出问题，你马上就能看到原因。如果你现有的 CI 已经碰到上限，或者宕机越来越影响交付，那就去 buildkite.com/pragmatic 看看。30 天全功能试用，不需要信用卡，还有真人工程师随时待命。他叫 Ola，人非常给力。我还想顺便提一下我们这一季的赞助商 Sentry。AI agent 现在还没有从生产可观测性日志里获得反馈闭环。但它们应该有吗？我的意思是，我们大概不想构建这样一个系统：每次生产环境报错后，AI agent 都在没人监督的情况下自动把修复推到生产环境。那简直就是灾难配方。但如果我们做得克制一点呢？比如说，当生产错误触发时，让一个 agent 带着来自 Sentry 的上下文去调查。毕竟 Sentry 里本来就已经有错误相关的全部上下文。Sentry MCP 就是一种把 Sentry 接到支持 Model Context Protocol 的 agent 上的方法。Cloud Code、Cursor、Codex、VS Code 和 Copilot 都支持。接上 MCP server 之后，你可以做很多很有用的事。比如说这样：当一个已经解决过的 Sentry issue 再次出现时，你可以触发一个 Cursor agent 去调查这次回归，读取相关代码，然后开一个带建议修复的 PR。要把这一整套跑起来，还是需要做一点工作。你得把 Sentry 连到你的代码仓库，把 Sentry MCP 加到 Cursor 里，定义好让 Cursor 的 agent 去调查的指令，配置触发自动化的条件，再测试整套流程是否正常。但一旦跑起来，你就能更快修复回归问题，同时仍然由人来审核每一个修复。我觉得这才是 AI、MCP 和 Sentry 一种合理又健康的用法。去 sentry.io/pragmatic 看看吧，今天就开始监控并修复回归问题。好了，说到这里，我们回到 Kelsey，继续聊他是怎么把 Puppet 引进组织里，并把 DevOps 流程自动化的。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: And I remember we had someone from Puppet come by.
text: 我记得当时还有 Puppet 的人来过我们公司。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: And then just for those who don't know Puppet, Puppet is this tool?
text: 顺便给不了解 Puppet 的人解释一下，Puppet 是个什么工具？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Configuration management tool. So Puppet is, we went from, you know, lots of shell scripts, you know, doing random things and pseudo automation. But most of the time, you just did a manual thing. You had a ticket come in. Someone wanted a new SSH key on a server. Someone wants something installed. You did it manually, copied and pasted the output in the ticket, and you closed it. and you did that every day over and over again and that's where the saying that I say sometimes some people have 20 years of one year experience some people in IT have been doing that 20 years in a row they never made the leap to automation or learning new tools or skills and so I saw like man maybe I can bring in a tool and I brought this tool called Puppet at the time 0248 and this is right before DevOps becomes a word but it's like all right I'm starting to learn how to write code like the for the Java production stuff I'm writing Puppet you know manifest using the Puppet DSL sometimes you have to write a little bit of Ruby to build a new resource type and I'm contributing and also I get introduced to open source like hey there's something there that doesn't work I'm going to contribute it upstream so I'm doing all this behind the scenes getting ready for act two And I remember my manager went to a conference and he came back and was like, hey, Kelsey, I finally know what you're doing. I was like, what's that? He was like, you're doing DevOps. And part of me was like upset. How do you get to name what I'm doing? And so, you know, everyone's talking about DevOps. And he's like, I met a guy named James Turnbull. And James is an Australian dude. He worked at Puppet Labs and he wrote the first puppet book that I bought and read. and so I'm like James Turnbaugh that name sounds familiar oh I'm looking at my desk James Turnbaugh and Jeff McCune so he's one of the co-authors of this book and it turns out James wrote a lot of tech books back then and he's coming to the office and my manager wanted him to check out how we were using Puppet and I was like okay so you know this is a world class expert and by that time I had Puppet hidden behind Jira tickets and so you could just open a Jira ticket I had RPMs for everything. You picked the RPM you wanted from a dropdown. You picked your environment from a dropdown. And magic happened. And you got results. Most people didn't even know Puppet existed. Most people never touched it. They just knew they can get anything on demand. You could go to Jira and it does it.
text: 配置管理工具。Puppet 这个东西吧，我们当时是从一堆 shell 脚本起步的，脚本到处都是，做各种零散的事，算是半自动化。但大多数时候，你还是在手工操作。来了一个工单，有人想在服务器上加一个新的 SSH key，或者有人想装点什么东西。你就手动去做，把输出复制粘贴到工单里，然后关单。每天都这么干，一遍又一遍。所以我有时候会说一句话：有些人是 20 年经验，有些人其实只是把 1 年经验重复了 20 次。IT 行业里确实有些人就是这样，连续 20 年都在做同样的事，从来没有跨到自动化，也没有去学新工具、新技能。所以我当时就在想，也许我可以引进一个工具。于是我带来了 Puppet，当时的版本是 0.24.8。那还是在 DevOps 这个词流行起来之前，但我已经开始学着写代码了。Java 生产环境那边的东西我在写，Puppet 这边我也在写 manifest，用 Puppet DSL；有时候还得写一点 Ruby，去做新的 resource type。我也开始做贡献。同时我也接触到了开源世界：比如有个东西不好用，我就把修复贡献回上游。我在幕后做着这一切，其实是在为第二幕做准备。我记得我经理去参加了一个大会，回来后跟我说，嘿，Kelsey，我终于知道你在干什么了。我说，什么？他说，你在做 DevOps。我当时有一部分情绪其实是不爽的：你凭什么来给我做的事命名？后来大家都开始谈 DevOps。他又说，我认识了一个叫 James Turnbull 的人。James 是个澳大利亚人，在 Puppet Labs 工作过，还写了我买来读的第一本 Puppet 书。所以我就想，James Turnbull，这名字听着很耳熟啊。然后我低头一看桌上，James Turnbull 和 Jeff McCune——对，他就是那本书的合著者之一。后来我才知道，James 那时候写了很多技术书。而且他要来我们办公室，我经理想让他看看我们是怎么用 Puppet 的。我就想，好吧，这可是世界级专家。到那个时候，我已经把 Puppet 藏在 Jira 工单后面了。你只要开一个 Jira 工单就行。我把所有东西都做成了 RPM。你从下拉框里选你想要的 RPM，再从下拉框里选环境。然后魔法就发生了，你就能拿到结果。大多数人甚至都不知道 Puppet 的存在，大多数人也从来没碰过它。他们只知道，自己想要什么都可以按需拿到。你去 Jira 提个单，它就会帮你搞定。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: So for example, you could like provision a new machine or a whole environment. I want database.
text: 比如说，你可以新建一台机器，或者直接拉起一整套环境。比如我想要一个数据库。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: I want IBM MQ Series Message Server I want these three apps And I need this firewall set up No problem Open the right jar of tickets Get them approved When they're approved We had this little I called it Mr. Resetti Alright One of my buddies gave me a name from some video game Once it got approved Mr. Resetti would own the ticket And we would just extract the custom fields And we would just call the right puppet manifest And we would take the output and update the ticket And so PMs, developers They just got what they want damn near instantaneously So we built all the systems. They didn't know nights and weekends. I'm contributing to Puppet because, you know, it wasn't allowed at work at the time. This is 2007, 2008.
text: 我想要 IBM MQ Series Message Server，我想要这三个应用，还需要把这个防火墙配好。没问题，开对对应的工单，把审批走完。审批一通过，我们有个小东西——我管它叫 Mr. Resetti。好吧，这名字是我一个哥们从某个电子游戏里拿来的。只要审批通过，Mr. Resetti 就会接管这个工单。然后我们把自定义字段提取出来，调用对应的 Puppet manifest，再把输出结果回写到工单里。这样一来，PM、开发基本上几乎是立刻就能拿到他们想要的东西。所以这些系统都是我们搭起来的。他们根本不知道我晚上和周末都在忙这些。我还在给 Puppet 做贡献，因为你知道，当时公司里还不允许。这是 2007、2008 年那会儿。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: They probably didn't even really understand what open source was anyway. So they were just like, hey, you got to do that on your own time.
text: 他们可能压根也不真正明白开源到底是什么。所以他们的态度就是，嘿，这种事你得用你自己的时间去做。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So I would just do it nights and weekends. So James shows up. And I remember we all get in the elevator. We're going to the third floor. My manager's introducing James to me, to our team. Like, hey, this is this person, this is this person. This is Kelsey. And James turns to me, he's like, Kelsey Hightower? Oh, we know you. We love your contributions. And my manager's just like, what contributions? We're not doing contributions. And James is like shaking my hand and he's just talking to me like we're friends because the thing is, I have been working with the Puppet Labs team through like open source contributing to Puppet. And so we get up there and, you know, my manager's like, hey, let's show him our setup. I was like, all right, James, we have a lot of Puppet manifests over here. I'm using external node classifiers. I'm setting a brand and config from data. And I read Mark Burris' promise theory and got it all dialed in. But from an interface perspective, I just didn't want everyone to have to learn Puppet. So we hooked it up to Jira. I'm showing him this entire workflow. And he's just sitting there like... Yeah I have no recommendations at all like this is this is amazing and here's the thing that kind of the game changer so he came all the way to Atlanta we're 45 minutes from the airport he spent time with our team and it's the end of the day and then he's on the way out and he's about to call a cab to go to the airport. I'm like, no, you're in my hometown. I cannot let you go to the airport. I will give you a ride to the airport. So I get in my car and he's in the front seat. My colleague's in the back seat. He takes off his tie and rolls up his sleeves. I can see all the tattoos. I'm like, oh, so you're like a legit tattoo. He's like, yeah, I'll do all of this. I just did this for you all. This is your dress code. I was like, oh, James is cool. He was like, man, yeah, you guys are doing great work. And now I'm talking to the real James Turnbull. And he invited me to give a talk at Puppet Cough. and I went to go give that talk. It was my first, like, real conference talk. And I talked about some low-level kind of puppet integration stuff and I had a little comedy in there. It was like the first time I felt like I could just be myself on stage. Before I came home, Lucanese, the founder of Puppet, we sat down in a little coffee shop in Portland, Oregon. And he was like, you know, would you like to work here? And I was like, yes! Of course, 100%. And of course, there was a nice salary increase and I could work remote. I'd have to leave Atlanta immediately. I remember coming back and my manager really appreciated all the progress I was making over the years. I kind of made a name for myself. And I remember he gave me a nice little raise, just out of the blue. And he slid this envelope to me and I opened it and it was a nice number in there. I was like, oh, that's great. I was like, but here's the thing. I'm resigning. I'm going to go work for Puppet. And then he said something that was dope because we had a kind of, you know, difficult relationship depending on how it went. But overall, he helped me mature. That's one thing I will always say. He helped me really be mature. And he was like, I'm surprised you stayed here this long. Right? It's like this ultimate compliment that you stayed here beyond the just getting better or making an impact. You literally changed this culture. Right? and the full circle moment happened like seven years later when Kubernetes came out I remember coming back to that office and they had already been running etcd in production Kubernetes in production and even some of the old tooling I had was still running and to me that was like a really important feedback that sometimes if you make an impact lasting impact is way different that even when I wasn't there they still had the culture to say there's new technology and we know how to bring it into the stack So at this point
