text: 所以我就晚上和周末做。后来 James 来了。我记得我们一起进电梯，要去三楼。我经理在给 James 介绍我和我们团队，说，嘿，这是这个人，这是那个人，这是 Kelsey。James 转过来看着我，说，Kelsey Hightower？哦，我们认识你。我们很喜欢你的那些贡献。我经理当场就愣了：什么贡献？我们可没在做什么贡献。James 一边跟我握手，一边像老朋友一样跟我聊天，因为实际上，我一直在通过开源的方式跟 Puppet Labs 团队一起做 Puppet 相关的事情。后来我们到了楼上，我经理说，来，给他看看我们的整套方案。我说，好，James，我们这边有很多 Puppet manifest，我在用 external node classifiers，我从数据里设置品牌和配置，我还读了 Mark Burris 的 promise theory，把整套东西都调得很顺。但从界面角度来说，我不想让所有人都得学 Puppet，所以我们把它接到了 Jira 上。我就把整个工作流都演示给他看。他就坐在那里，一副……对，我完全没什么建议，这太厉害了。然后真正改变局面的事是这样的：他专门飞到 Atlanta 来，我们离机场有 45 分钟车程，他花时间跟我们团队待了一整天。到了傍晚，他准备走了，正要叫出租车去机场。我说，不行，你在我老家，我不能让你自己去机场，我送你去。于是我开车，他坐副驾，我同事坐后排。他把领带摘了，袖子卷起来，我看到他满手臂的纹身。我心想，哦，所以你真的是那种很有范儿的纹身哥们。他说，嗯，我平时就是这样，今天这些都是为了你们才穿的，这是你们这边的着装要求。我当时就想，哦，James 挺酷的。他说，哥们，你们做得真的很棒。然后我就这样和真正的 James Turnbull 聊上了。他还邀请我去 PuppetConf 做分享。我后来真的去了，那是我第一次真正意义上的大会演讲。我讲的是一些比较底层的 Puppet 集成相关内容，还加了点喜剧元素。那是我第一次觉得，自己在台上可以完全做自己。回家之前，Puppet 的创始人 Luke Kanies 跟我在 Oregon 州 Portland 的一家小咖啡馆里坐下来聊。他问我，你愿意来这里工作吗？我说，愿意！当然愿意，百分之百愿意。而且薪资涨幅也很不错，还可以远程办公。我得立刻离开 Atlanta。我记得我回来以后，我经理其实一直都很认可我这些年的进步。我算是慢慢闯出了点名气。我记得有一次他突然给我涨了点薪水，完全没预告。他把一个信封推给我，我打开一看，里面的数字还挺不错。我说，哦，这很好。但问题是，我要辞职了，我要去 Puppet 工作。然后他说了一句特别酷的话。因为我们之间的关系吧，得看具体时候，有时挺难搞的。但总体来说，他帮助我成熟了很多。这点我一直都会承认，他真的让我变得更成熟。他说，我很惊讶你居然在这儿待了这么久。对吧？这简直是最高级别的夸奖了。意思就是，你留在这里已经不只是为了变得更强，或者做出点影响。你是真的改变了这里的文化。然后大概七年后，Kubernetes 出来时，那个完整闭环的时刻发生了。我记得我又回到那间办公室，他们已经在生产环境里跑 etcd、跑 Kubernetes，甚至我当年留下的一些老工具还在运行。对我来说，那是一个特别重要的反馈：有时候，如果你真的做出了影响，持久的影响和短期影响完全不是一回事。哪怕我已经不在那里了，他们依然保留着那种文化——看到新技术，就知道该怎么把它引入现有技术栈。所以到了这个阶段——

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Containers were starting to start, right? How did you first come across containers? And very early on, you realized this is going to be big and important.
text: 那时候容器也开始起来了，对吧？你最早是怎么接触到容器的？而且你很早就意识到，这东西会变得很大、很重要。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: I didn't. Oh, you didn't? No, because I'm at Puppet Labs. You know, before that, I was contributing to core Python stuff like VirtualInt, PyPy. I was on the team that was trying to integrate some of the Python. Python had a package manager problem even back then. and so I'm all in on Python and then when I'm learning Puppet I'm all in on Ruby at that time I'm thinking DevOps plus configuration management is the end all be all for everything and really at that time we're talking 2011 2012 in my mind the competition was only between Puppet, Chef and Ansible that was it and so I'm all in and I'm working at Puppet Labs and so we're kind of in some ways I felt like we were dictating the future we were going to companies and getting them to understand configuration management We were talking about the benefits of speeding everything up and doing all these compliance. We were moving people from SharePoint to executable code to implement these things. So we thought we were just getting started. There was no world where we didn't think that we needed 10 more years of that. We even got money from VMware to integrate Puppet into that. Chef was being integrated into AWS at that time. So this was like the thing that finally, we're finally getting there. We just got to teach system administrators to become engineers via DevOps and start embracing these tools. And then the first thing that I saw come out was Golang.
text: 其实没有。哦，你没有？没有，因为那时候我在 Puppet Labs。你知道，在那之前，我在给 Python 核心相关的东西做贡献，比如 VirtualInt、PyPy。我当时在一个团队里，想把 Python 的一些东西整合起来。那时候 Python 就已经有包管理的问题了。所以我当时是全身心投入 Python。后来学 Puppet 的时候，我又全身心投入 Ruby。那时候我觉得，DevOps 加上配置管理就是一切问题的终极答案。说的是 2011、2012 年那会儿。在我脑子里，竞争对手只有 Puppet、Chef 和 Ansible，就这几个。所以我是完全押注在这上面的，而且我还在 Puppet Labs 工作。某种程度上，我觉得我们当时是在定义未来。我们去各家公司，让他们理解配置管理；我们讲怎么把一切提速、怎么做各种合规；我们把人们从 SharePoint 文档迁移到可执行代码上，用代码来落地这些东西。所以我们觉得自己才刚刚开始。我们完全不觉得这件事还不需要再干 10 年。我们甚至还拿到了 VMware 的资金，把 Puppet 集成进去。那时候 Chef 也在和 AWS 做集成。所以这看起来就像是：终于，终于走到这一步了。我们只需要教系统管理员通过 DevOps 变成工程师，开始拥抱这些工具。然后我最先看到冒出来的东西，其实是 Golang。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: And I remember sitting at my desk and I was like,
text: 我记得我当时坐在办公桌前，我就想——

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: you know, at that time we were hitting performance issues with Ruby. The global lock interpreter, you can't do more than one thing at a time, not easily. And so we moved to things like JRuby We even did Clojure for some part of the Puppet stack And I remember we were thinking about Should we start doing stuff in C++? Should we start doing stuff in C? But the problem is contributors What would we do with all the Ruby contributors? We were relying on things like RubyGems And it was going to be a hard sell And I remember downloading Go for the first time And the thing that sold me on it I remember one of the prototypes that I built was Factor. So Factor is one of the agents in Puppet that gives you facts about a machine. So this is Red Hat, this kernel version, this is what the users look like, and it would give that to Puppet so that your configuration code had context on what to do. And that was written in Ruby, and sometimes it will run slow because you're reading all these files serially. And so I remember it's like, all right, let's try this Golang out. And I remember I wrote the code on my Mac, compiled it, SCPed it to a Linux box. I was like, oh, this is crazy. And I remember running all the facts in parallel. And it came back in a fraction of the time. I was like, yo, we got to use Golang for stuff. And I remember the team was like, nah, it doesn't work for Solaris or AIX because Puppet is now moving into the enterprise. I was like, that's the criteria? No way. But I got it, right? I've gone through this maturity thing before. and then Terraform comes out. But before Docker, Terraform is starting to challenge things a little bit, right? And it's starting to use things like Go. At least that was on my radar. So Terraform is like, who cares about the node? It's all about the APIs. And all of us come from the server world and everything's about an agent on a node versus the clouds starting to take over at this point.
text: 你知道，那时候我们已经碰到 Ruby 的性能问题了。它那个全局解释器锁，让你没法轻松地同时做多件事。所以我们转向了 JRuby 之类的东西，甚至在 Puppet 技术栈的某些部分还用了 Clojure。我记得当时我们在想，要不要开始用 C++ 写点东西？要不要上 C？但问题在于贡献者。那些 Ruby 贡献者怎么办？我们还依赖 RubyGems 这样的东西，所以这会非常难推。我记得我第一次下载 Go 的时候，真正打动我的是这个。我当时做过一个原型，叫 Factor。Factor 是 Puppet 里的一个 agent，它会收集机器的各种 facts。比如这是 Red Hat、这是内核版本、这是用户情况，然后把这些信息交给 Puppet，这样你的配置代码就知道该怎么做。那东西原来是 Ruby 写的，有时候会跑得很慢，因为你是在串行读取一堆文件。我记得当时就想，好，来试试这个 Golang 吧。我记得我在 Mac 上写完代码，编译好，用 SCP 传到一台 Linux 机器上。我当时就想，哇，这也太离谱了。然后我把所有 facts 并行跑起来，结果只用了原来一小部分时间就跑完了。我当时就说，兄弟们，我们得用 Golang 来做东西。结果团队说，不行，它不支持 Solaris 或 AIX，因为 Puppet 现在正在往企业市场走。我当时想，这就是你们的判断标准？不至于吧。但我也能理解，对吧？这种成熟阶段我以前也经历过。然后 Terraform 出来了。不过是在 Docker 之前，Terraform 就已经开始有点挑战原来的思路了，对吧？而且它开始用 Go 这种东西。至少这是我当时关注到的。Terraform 的思路是：谁在乎那个节点？关键是 API。可我们这些人全都是服务器世界出来的，什么都围绕着节点上的 agent 转，而这时候云已经开始逐渐接管一切了。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: So Terraform was built with the cloud in mind, right? You could configure your cloud environment infrastructure as a code.
text: 所以 Terraform 从一开始就是围绕云来构建的，对吧？你可以把云环境基础设施当成代码来配置。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah. And we were trying to do this from a node. We were trying to teach Puppet how to talk through by having this indirection where you have to go through a node to configure a server in the cloud. It's so weird. but then Terraform Mitchell Hashimoto right like he was a big part of the DevOps movement early too then he was like Vagrant was written in Ruby so he was part of that same ecosystem and then he splits off and there's this goling I'm like look at that they're using go for this and I remember when Docker came out Puppet at that time was the one pushing innovation we're asking people to think a little different get out of their comfort zone and then Docker comes out and most of the office was dismissing this as like nope this is a fad They don't even have config management in this thing. They don't really understand enterprise. This is just kind of, not like a toy. They were not being disrespectful, but no one saw this as a challenger, what we were doing. And I looked at it and I didn't get it immediately until I left. When I left Puppet, I built this tool called CompD and went to go be a VP of engineering and I got to write Go code. So we started, rightfully so, we lifted all the Java heavy usage. And I earned the trust of the team. We started rewriting some of the microservices in Go, shrinking our cloud footprint. And we sunset it and got it all into production, the Go code. And I was like, we don't really need Puppet anymore. And I open sourced this project called ConfD, and it would pull variables from etcd and generate just enough config, just the parts of Puppet that I thought made sense. And then Docker was out. And then I was like, wow, we can probably stop moving Python files around. We can probably package them up, not in RPMs.
text: 对。我们当时还想从节点这个层面来做这件事。我们试图教 Puppet 怎么通过一种间接层去通信，也就是你得先经过一个节点，才能去配置云里的服务器。特别奇怪。后来 Terraform 出来了，Mitchell Hashimoto，对吧，他也是早期 DevOps 运动里很重要的人物。再后来他搞了 Vagrant，Vagrant 是用 Ruby 写的，所以他本来就在同一个生态里。然后他又分出来，搞了这个 goling，我当时就想，哇，他们居然在用 Go 做这个。我还记得 Docker 刚出来的时候，那会儿 Puppet 其实还是那个推动创新的人，我们在让大家换个思路，走出舒适区。结果 Docker 一出来，办公室里大多数人都很不屑，觉得，不，这就是个一时热潮。他们这玩意儿连配置管理都没有，根本不懂企业级需求，这东西也就，倒不算玩具吧。他们不是故意不尊重，只是没人把它当成我们所做事情的挑战者。我当时看着它，也没立刻看懂，直到我离开之后。离开 Puppet 后，我做了个叫 CompD 的工具，然后去当了工程 VP，也终于能写 Go 代码了。所以我们开始，理所当然地，把那些重度依赖 Java 的东西都搬掉。我也赢得了团队的信任。我们开始用 Go 重写一些微服务，缩小云资源占用。后来我们把旧的东西下线，把 Go 代码全都跑进了生产环境。我当时就想，我们其实已经不太需要 Puppet 了。然后我把一个叫 ConfD 的项目开源了，它会从 etcd 里拉变量，然后生成刚刚够用的配置，也就是只保留我觉得 Puppet 里真正有意义的那部分。那时候 Docker 也已经出来了。我就想，哇，我们大概也不用再到处搬 Python 文件了。我们可以把它们打包起来，而且不是打成 RPM。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: The idea with Docker was, right, that it would define a virtual machine or a container, right?
text: Docker 的思路是，它可以定义一个虚拟机或者一个容器，对吧？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So to me, the big value of Docker at the time was previously I had definitely did the work to make RPMs for every app, even the custom apps. RPMs? RPMs are the Red Hat package management.
text: 对我来说，Docker 当时最大的价值在于，以前我确实会认真给每个应用都做 RPM，连那些内部自定义应用也是。RPM？RPM 就是 Red Hat 的包管理格式。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So if you're in a Red Hat system, you can do yum install Nginx, yum install Postgres, but most people... Even today, don't package their third-party apps like the apps that a development team would write. You're like, no, we just put CICD. We'll copy them over there, maybe put them in a tarball. But we usually never went to making official Debian packages or RBM packages. That puppet meant you didn't have to go through all of that work and you could still end up with a package, something that was repeatable. So all the stuff we used to do with Python and Virtual Ant, all the things we used to do with RubyGems and the virtual environments we have for that, we got rid of it. You squished it all into a Docker container and you got rid of a lot of dev tooling. So this is why I think it resonated so much with developers because we cleaned up the mess of working on multiple projects. And so I was attracted to that. Comp D was compatible with that. And then CoreOS was the thing that I was like, you know what? I think I know what I want to do. But I didn't understand distributed systems, not to the degree what CoreOS was doing. So when I moved to CoreOS.
text: 所以如果你在 Red Hat 系统里，可以直接 yum install Nginx，yum install Postgres。但大多数人……哪怕到今天，也不会把第三方应用，或者说开发团队自己写的那些应用，正经打成包。大家通常会说，不，我们有 CICD。我们把文件拷过去，可能再打个 tarball。但一般不会真的去做正式的 Debian 包或者 RBM 包。Puppet 的意义就在于，你不用走完那一整套流程，最后也还是能得到一种“像包一样”的、可重复的交付方式。所以我们以前用 Python 和 Virtual Ant 做的那些事，用 RubyGems 和各种虚拟环境做的那些事，后来都不要了。你把这些东西全都塞进一个 Docker 容器里，就能省掉大量开发工具链。所以我觉得这也是它为什么那么打动开发者，因为它把大家在多个项目之间切换时留下的一堆烂摊子都清理掉了。所以我很被这个吸引。Comp D 也和这套思路很兼容。然后 CoreOS 出现了，我当时就想，你知道吗？我大概知道自己想做什么了。只是我那时还不懂分布式系统，至少没懂到 CoreOS 那种程度。所以后来我去了 CoreOS。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: What was the idea behind CoreOS?
text: CoreOS 背后的想法是什么？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So CoreOS was like Google's infrastructure for everyone else. And so at that time we did, there was a tool called Mesos. The Borg paper had already been published. I tried to read it when I was at Puppet Labs. I don't understand any of this stuff. Mesos is hard to install. I couldn't justify it. But we would see kind of the rumblings of the Twitter folks talking about the distributed system and all this maybe big data stuff is going around. But I was like, I just don't understand it. It seems incompatible with the stuff that I think about. Even as someone who's worked at Google, that had a system like this, right? They had Google cluster file system at this time. It didn't seem as complicated as this Meso's thing. So I was like, ah, I dismissed it. But what CoreOS did was build on top of Docker. CoreOS was like, what if we had an operating system that only had Docker on it? Everything is written in Go. We can have a little key value store where you can put your config and we would just synchronize it to all the machines. And that was so compatible with the ConfD way. And so I'm looking at this CoreOS thing, I'm like, yo, this looks a little bit more like the future. Because that's what we used to do. You know, I had, at this time, I was a software developer at Puppet. I was a software developer, VP of engineering at the other company. And I was like, you know what? Ops can learn a lot from the Docker way of thinking about the world. Because as a system administrator, we always try to make the OS small, remove things you don't need so it can be secure and repeatable. Docker was like, for the things that need to change, just put it here. and isolate it. And Coroes is like, what if you had an operating system designed only to do that? And I met the Coroes team at Go4Con, a Go language conference, and they saw me present. And what I learned from the puppet days, every presentation is an interview. I don't think a lot of people think about it that way because people are looking at you on stage and They get to see what you're about. And so at GopherCon, I remember also Rob Pike and the original creators of Golinger and I was just like the first GopherCon, right? And so the original creators, Russ Cox, all of these people I look up to, Brad Fitzpatrick, and they're all in the audience. And I remember I had a talk two things I'm sitting in the audience waiting for my turn I'm number four on a list or something like this and the two people who started GopherCon they're just from the community Brian Kettleston and Eric St. Martin and they're new to this conference scene like you could tell right you know they weren't the best MC's in the world but they were like hey welcome to GopherCon and at the point in time Ruby is still dominating and down the street there was the Ruby conference going on and we were just in this other building And I remember the first talk they introduced Rob Pike and he gave this amazing keynote around Hello World. He went up all the way down to the compiler. He went all the way up and he described how the language took shape. It felt simple on the surface, but it went super deep. And it's like Rob Pike had also come full circle from his AT&T days through Unicode, through all the stuff that he's ever done. And now we get to see one of his best works, Golang. and my talk was titled Golang for System Administrators. I wanted them to see that there's a better way. The way they introduced Rob Pike, I was like, oh man, this is Rob Pike. You gotta have more energy than that for Rob Pike. So I'm sitting in my chair and I don't know them, but I'm sitting with a buddy of mine from that company where we rewrote everything in Golang. Billy Click, we're sitting next to him and say, hey, Bill, I'm gonna go ask them, can I be the emcee? He's like, what? Okay, so I'm whispering. And I walked to the backstage just like, hey, Eric, Brian, can I try my hand at being the emcee? And they were like, sure, right? And they knew at least who I was because they accepted the talk. And so I don't know if it was after Rob, but I came out next. So like, hey, I'm Kelsey Hightower. You may not know me, but I'm going to be your emcee for the rest of the day. And I don't know what happened because it was my first time doing that. I'm cracking jokes. I'm having fun. And I said, hey, from here on out, when anyone comes to the stage, it needs to be loud in here to the point where we can get kicked out. And so I was, all right, so our next speaker, and I think it might have been Derek Colston, you know, he's like a Googler, he's talking about some ghost stuff. And I remember it got really loud. and then they come on stage so if you were a speaker ever before and it's like you walk up to basically a standing ovation you're energized everybody is excited I don't care what you do the energy level is high and so he comes out he's having a good time the audience is having a good time and then eventually it was my turn and I realized like who introduces me so I go onto stage I was like I'm next. So we're going to try it like this. I'm going to go back and I'm going to come out and you guys are going to make a lot of noise for my talk. And then I remember just like sliding slowly behind the curtain. So everyone's now laughing. And then the music comes on and I walk out surprised. Like, hey!
text: CoreOS 的定位，有点像是“把 Google 的基础设施带给其他所有人”。当时有个工具叫 Mesos。Borg 那篇论文也已经发表了。我在 Puppet Labs 的时候试着读过，但完全看不懂。Mesos 也很难装，我没法为它找到合理的使用理由。不过我们能感觉到一些风向，比如 Twitter 那帮人在聊分布式系统，还有各种所谓的大数据。我当时就想，我就是不理解这些东西，它看起来和我平时思考问题的方式完全不兼容。哪怕我自己在 Google 工作过，知道他们内部也有类似系统，对吧？他们那时候已经有 Google cluster file system 了。但看起来也没像 Mesos 这套东西这么复杂。所以我当时就把它否掉了。但 CoreOS 做的事情，是建立在 Docker 之上。CoreOS 的想法是：如果我们有一个操作系统，上面基本只有 Docker 呢？所有东西都用 Go 写。再加一个小型 key value store，你把配置放进去，我们就把它同步到所有机器上。这和 ConfD 的思路太契合了。所以我看着 CoreOS，就想，哟，这看起来更像未来。因为这本来就是我们以前在做的事。你知道，那时候我在 Puppet 是软件开发工程师，后来在另一家公司当工程 VP，本质上也还是软件开发。我就想，运维其实可以从 Docker 这种看世界的方式里学到很多。因为作为系统管理员，我们一直都在努力让操作系统尽量精简，删掉不需要的东西，这样才更安全、更可重复。Docker 的思路是，那些需要变化的东西，就放在这里，把它隔离起来。而 CoreOS 则是在说，如果有一个操作系统，天生就是为了做这件事而设计的呢？我是在 Go4Con，一个 Go 语言大会上认识 CoreOS 团队的。他们看了我的演讲。而我从 Puppet 那段经历里学到的一件事就是：每一场演讲其实都是一次面试。我觉得很多人没这么想过，因为台下的人在看你站在台上，他们会借这个机会看清你是个什么样的人。所以在 GopherCon 上，我记得 Rob Pike 和 goling 的原始作者们也都在。那是第一届 GopherCon，对吧？Russ Cox，这些我一直很敬佩的人，Brad Fitzpatrick，他们全都坐在台下。我记得我也有个演讲。我坐在观众席里等轮到自己，大概排在第四个之类的。GopherCon 的两位发起人其实就是社区里的人，Brian Kettleston 和 Eric St. Martin。他们刚开始办这种大会，你能看出来，对吧？他们不是世界上最会控场的主持人，但他们还是会说，嘿，欢迎来到 GopherCon。那时候 Ruby 依然占据主导地位，街对面还在办 Ruby 大会，而我们只是在另一栋楼里。我记得第一场他们介绍 Rob Pike 上台，他做了一个特别精彩的 keynote，主题居然是 Hello World。他一路从编译器层面讲起，再一路往上，讲这门语言是怎么成形的。表面上看很简单，但其实非常深。感觉 Rob Pike 也是走了一个完整的轮回，从他在 AT&T 的年代，到 Unicode，到他做过的所有事情，最后我们看到的就是他最好的作品之一，Golang。我的演讲题目叫《Golang for System Administrators》。我想让大家看到，其实有一种更好的方式。他们介绍 Rob Pike 的那个方式让我心想，天啊，这可是 Rob Pike 啊，你们得更有点激情才行。所以我坐在椅子上，虽然不认识他们，但我旁边坐着我在那家用 Golang 重写一切的公司里的一个朋友，Billy Click。我跟他说，嘿，Bill，我要去问问他们，能不能让我来当主持。他说，啥？行吧。于是我就小声说着，然后走到后台，直接说，嘿，Eric，Brian，我能不能试试当主持？他们说，当然可以，对吧？至少他们知道我是谁，因为他们已经收了我的演讲。所以我不确定是不是在 Rob 之后，但反正接下来就是我出来。我说，嘿，我是 Kelsey Hightower。你们可能不认识我，但今天剩下的时间我来给大家当主持。我也不知道发生了什么，因为那是我第一次干这个。我开始讲笑话，开始玩起来。我还说，从现在开始，每一个人上台的时候，这里都得吵到像是我们会被赶出去一样。所以我就说，好，接下来我们的下一位讲者，我记得可能是 Derek Colston，一个 Googler，讲一些 ghost 相关的东西。我记得现场一下子就特别热闹。然后讲者一上台，如果你以前当过讲者你就知道，那种感觉就像你一走上去就已经接近全场起立鼓掌了，整个人会被点燃，所有人都很兴奋。不管你讲什么，现场能量都已经拉满了。所以他上来之后也玩得很开心，观众也很开心。后来终于轮到我自己了，我突然意识到，那谁来介绍我？于是我走到台上说，下一个就是我。我们这么试一下：我先退回去，然后再走出来，你们得为我的演讲制造巨大的声浪。我记得我当时还慢慢地滑到幕布后面。全场这时候都笑了。然后音乐响起，我装作很惊讶地走出来，说，嘿！

speaker: Gergely
text_en: And everyone's clapping.
text: 然后所有人都在鼓掌。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: And I do this talk. And at that time it was live demo or nothing. And at the time they had this thing called GoPresent. So Rob Pike team, they wanted to make sure they could use Go for everything. even generating a slide deck presentation. It was all in Go. It was all in Go. So you had this nice HTML representation of your slides. You just open up your browser. Hopefully it won't crash. Hopefully it doesn't crash. And you can run executable code in your examples. So any code snippet you put there, it was formatted nicely. And you had the little run button so people could see the output of the Go code. And so I was like, hmm, what would a system administrator value you can get from Go? How can I prove it to them? And so I wrote an iPixie server in Go. And I remember in VMware Fusion, that runs on your laptop. So if you want virtual machines on your laptop, you can use Desktop Parallels or VMware Fusion. The thing about VMware Fusion on your Mac, you can create virtual machines, but you can also swap out the network card. And I did one where you can have a network card that talks to iPixie. So I wanted to show them how I would boot up multiple machines from a GoPixie server running on my laptop. So part of that demo, I had this kind of snippet of my Pixie server. and I hit the run button. So now my Pixie server is running in the background. It's like, so what can you do with it? And I remember looking in the front row and Rob Pike and team were just intimately looking at this thing like, wow, he just started a Pixie server from his laptop from GoPresent, the slide deck tool. And so this thing is running and I'm like, all right, let's bring up VMware. I got to make sure we switch the adapter to the one that supports iPixie. You got to do some firmware thing. And then I remember I booted the VM and You can see the logs in the Go present of like HTTP handing off the image, giving an IP, and the virtual machine is booting up. And you can just see the amazement on the audience face like, did you just do that? And then I booted another thing in the thing, and I'm going through why I think that this is a game changer for the craft that we have. We finally have a tool where we write high-performing things with the simplicity of imperative programming, and we can go beyond just scripts. We can actually build systems. The audience was dialed in and it got loud. Like after, you know, things were working, people were clapping. I look in the back and there's a whole bunch of people now standing. And I recognize some of the names because they're from the Ruby community. These are people that are writing the Ruby books. And then, you know, after I'm done, there's a break and I walk back there. They're like, hey, we saw on Twitter that you guys are just like going crazy over here. It's out of control. It's electrifying. We left the Ruby event. to come here. And so I felt like, man, it had arrived. But guess who else was there? The CoreOS team. The CoreOS team was watching me because I was pixiebooting CoreOS. This is what I mean is every job is an interview. The team was like, all right, we can see that, yeah, you should join the CoreOS team. So that's how I ended up at CoreOS. This is how I really felt like the container movement had legs. And this is what maybe a year or two before Kubernetes comes out. And then Kubernetes came out. and you were starting to contribute to that as well. Yeah, so I'm at CoreOS and I think the thing that's very important, a lot of software engineers, sometimes they look at the people on stage and they ask questions like,
text: 然后我开始讲这个 talk。那时候的风气就是，要么 live demo，要么别讲。而且当时他们有个东西叫 GoPresent。Rob Pike 那个团队想证明，Go 什么都能做，甚至连幻灯片演示都能用 Go 来生成。全都是 Go。全都是 Go。于是你会得到一个很漂亮的 HTML 版幻灯片，直接在浏览器里打开就行。希望它别崩。希望它别崩。你甚至还能在示例里直接运行可执行代码。所以你放进去的任何代码片段，都会被排版得很好看，而且旁边还有个运行按钮，大家可以直接看到 Go 代码的输出。我当时就在想，系统管理员到底能从 Go 里得到什么价值？我怎么向他们证明？于是我用 Go 写了一个 iPixie server。我记得是在 VMware Fusion 里演示的，那东西是跑在你笔记本上的。所以如果你想在笔记本上跑虚拟机，可以用 Desktop Parallels 或者 VMware Fusion。VMware Fusion 在 Mac 上有个特点，就是你不仅能创建虚拟机，还能替换网卡。我当时就配了一个能和 iPixie 通信的网卡。我想给他们展示，怎么从我笔记本上跑着的一个 GoPixie server 启动多台机器。所以在那个 demo 里，我放了一小段 Pixie server 的代码，然后点了运行按钮。这样我的 Pixie server 就在后台跑起来了。我就说，那你能拿它做什么呢？我记得我看向前排，Rob Pike 和他的团队都特别专注地盯着这个东西，像是在想，哇，他居然直接通过 GoPresent 这个幻灯片工具，在笔记本上启动了一个 Pixie server。然后这东西就在跑着，我说，好，我们来打开 VMware。先得确保把网卡切到支持 iPixie 的那个，还得做一点固件相关的设置。然后我记得我启动了那个 VM，你能在 Go present 里看到日志，比如 HTTP 在交付镜像、分配 IP，然后虚拟机就开始启动了。你能从观众脸上看到那种震惊：你刚刚真的做到了？然后我又启动了另一个东西，同时一路讲我为什么觉得这会改变我们这个行业。我们终于有了一个工具，既能用命令式编程的简单方式写出高性能的东西，又能超越脚本层面。我们真的可以构建系统了。观众完全听进去了，现场也越来越热烈。比如每次一个环节成功了，大家都会鼓掌。我往后面一看，已经有一大群人站着了。我认出其中一些名字，因为他们是 Ruby 社区的人，是那些写 Ruby 书的人。后来我讲完，中间休息，我走到后面，他们跟我说，嘿，我们在 Twitter 上看到你们这边已经疯了，完全失控，太有感染力了。我们直接从 Ruby 那边的活动跑过来的。所以我当时感觉，哇，这门语言真的来了。但你猜还有谁在现场？CoreOS 团队。CoreOS 团队当时就在看我，因为我正在用 pixieboot 启动 CoreOS。这就是我说的，每一份工作其实都是一次面试。那个团队当时就觉得，好，我们看出来了，没错，你应该加入 CoreOS。所以我最后就这么去了 CoreOS。也是从那时候起，我真正感觉到容器这股浪潮是有后劲的。而这大概还是 Kubernetes 出来前一两年的事。后来 Kubernetes 出来了。然后你也开始给它做贡献了。对，我那时候在 CoreOS。我觉得有一点特别重要，很多软件工程师有时候会看着台上的人，然后会问一些这样的问题：

speaker: Gergely
text_en: does that person know what they're doing?
text: 那个人知道自己在干什么吗？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Is this person just like an evangelist? Did someone write this demo for them? Did they give it to them and they just run the code?
text: 这个人是不是就只是个 evangelist？这个 demo 是不是别人给他写的？是不是别人把东西交给他，他就只是跑一下代码？

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Yeah, we think that, don't we?
text: 对，我们都会这么想，不是吗？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Well, I mean, I can see why because sometimes It's hard to value skills you don't have. So a lot of software engineers are terrible. If you put a software engineer, they can't talk. They can't simplify concepts. Maybe they can write code really well, but this is a set of skills that they may or may not have. And so when you see someone like that, you're questioning them. And I remember giving a talk one time at Strange Loop about etcd and CoreOS, and someone was like, Do you understand, is etcd like a CA system or an AP system, like the CAP theorem? And the question was kind of loaded, like, we don't think you even know what that means. I was like, etcd is going to always favor consistency. He was like, that's not correct. The RAF paper, blah, blah, blah. I said, listen, you said etcd. Let me show you. This is the RAF log. This is my three nodes. I'm going to turn off two. And you notice I can't write any keys. So availability has been sacrificed. consistency is being preserved. I'm going to start another node. There's going to be a handshake. There's going to be quorum. I will be able to write keys. Now it works. That's the cap theorem in reality. So it doesn't matter what the RAF paper says. You're talking about a RAF log on a single implementation. RAF doesn't talk about cluster membership, leader election, how it's implemented, and what you should do in the different modes. That choice is yours. And this is how Etsy-D is implemented. And I remember he was like, oh shit, this guy actually knows what he's talking about. Being at CoreOS, we were working on our own fleet management system called Fleet. We were using Systemd and we were trying to synchronize configs through etcd. And remember in a CoreOS cluster, all the nodes communicate via etcd. So imagine using systemd for those that have never used systemd you put a unit file like I want this process to start with these flags you know bind to this port and you put it in a directory and then systemd will start it and if you had a thousand nodes of course you could scp that file to all 1,000 nodes. And then there you have it. So we decided instead of you copying all the files to all the nodes, just put the unit file in etcd and the node that should run those things would then pull from etcd and just run those unit files. And we called it Fleet. So we had our own vision of giving people a distributed system. About a year goes by, Kubernetes comes out. And everyone was like, what's that? and we got like a day notice. So the Google team reached out and was like, hey, tomorrow we're announcing this thing. Here's the GitHub repository. You guys are under embargo. Don't talk about it. And so I'm thinking, we're not part of this story. Our names are not in here. It just says Google and Red Hat and they're going to announce it at DockerCon. There's no CoreOS in here. So what can we do? And I remember I stayed up all night. I got access to the GitHub repository. I'm reading all the Go code, trying to figure out what all these binaries mean because there's no docs. And so I got everything working on CoreOS. So when they did the official Google announcement, of course, there's a famous Docker keynote where they unveiled it to the surprise of even Docker to some degree. And I remember they post their number one on Hacker News. And then we post, hey, what they just said, But here's how you run it on CoreOS and some examples and commands. And I had to do a few patches to get it to actually work and I had to build some binaries to get things glued together. You do that overnight. Overnight. I didn't go to sleep. I'm just like, hey guys, I finally figured out how to compile everything. I think the kubelet does this. You have to put this there. They had like a kube up SSH script, but I had to reverse it because I'm not using Google Cloud. We got CoreOS, we're on bare metal So I have to pixie-boot some things But I think if you put all these things in the right place There's this etcd thing, that's ours, we know that So we know how to use that But I think the API server connects to that There's no volumes, there's no config maps So all you can do is get this thing stood up And then you can submit a config And then it will just basically use Docker in the background Okay, I can document that So we get everything to work And I write this nice little guide Someone on our team, they publish it to the official CoreOS website So then we relaunched that on Hacker News and then we go to number one. And everyone is like, Google just announced this thing. We don't know what it is. And then Kelsey launches this thing. We now know what it is. We know how to install it. We know how to run it. So now people are downloading CoreOS just so they can play with Kubernetes. And I had a keynote probably in a week I don't know what I submitted to the conference because usually you submit like months ahead of time. I was like, hey, I know this talk was supposed to be about this, but it's not today. It's going to be about Kubernetes. And people are like, what's that? It's like, yeah, I just got announced. I'm going to show you. And I started giving people live demos of Kubernetes and how to make it work, you know, using CoreOS and all of these things. But the team still wasn't sold because we had our own roadmap.
text: 嗯，我能理解为什么会这样，因为有时候你很难去评价自己不具备的技能。所以很多软件工程师其实很不擅长这些。你让一个软件工程师上来，他可能不会讲，没法把概念讲简单。也许他代码写得特别好，但这是一套他未必具备的技能。所以当你看到一个这样的人时，你就会质疑他。我记得有一次我在 Strange Loop 做一个关于 etcd 和 CoreOS 的演讲，有人问我：你真的理解吗，etcd 到底是 CA 系统还是 AP 系统，就是 CAP theorem 里的那个？这个问题本身就有点带刺，潜台词像是在说，我们觉得你连这是什么意思都不知道。我当时说，etcd 永远会优先保证 consistency。他说，这不对，Raft 论文里怎么怎么说。我就说，听着，你问的是 etcd。那我直接给你看。这是 Raft log，这是我的三个节点。我现在关掉两个。你会发现我没法写任何 key。所以 availability 被牺牲了，consistency 被保住了。然后我再启动一个节点，会有握手，会形成 quorum，我就又能写 key 了。现在又能用了。这才是现实里的 CAP theorem。所以 Raft 论文怎么写其实不重要。你说的是某个具体实现里的 Raft log。Raft 并不讨论 cluster membership、leader election、具体怎么实现，以及在不同模式下你该怎么做。那些选择是你自己定的。而 etcd 就是这么实现的。我记得那个人当时的反应就是，哦靠，这家伙是真的懂自己在说什么。在 CoreOS 的时候，我们在做自己的 fleet management system，叫 Fleet。我们用的是 Systemd，还想通过 etcd 来同步配置。要知道在 CoreOS 集群里，所有节点都是通过 etcd 通信的。所以你可以想象一下，用 systemd 的话——对那些没用过 systemd 的人来说——你会放一个 unit file，内容类似于我希望这个进程带着这些参数启动，比如绑定这个端口，然后你把它放进一个目录里，systemd 就会把它启动起来。如果你有一千个节点，当然你也可以把这个文件 scp 到全部 1000 个节点上。这样也能搞定。所以我们当时决定，不要把所有文件复制到所有节点上了，直接把 unit file 放进 etcd，应该运行这些东西的节点再从 etcd 拉下来，然后执行这些 unit file。我们把这个东西叫 Fleet。所以我们当时也有自己的愿景，想给大家一套分布式系统。大概过了一年，Kubernetes 出来了。大家都在问，那是什么？而且我们只提前一天收到消息。Google 团队联系过来说，嘿，我们明天要发布这个东西。这是 GitHub 仓库。你们现在在 embargo 期内，别往外说。所以我当时想，这个故事里根本没有我们。里面没有我们的名字，只写了 Google 和 Red Hat，他们要在 DockerCon 上发布。这里面没有 CoreOS。那我们能做什么？我记得那天晚上我整夜没睡。我拿到了 GitHub 仓库权限，就开始读所有 Go 代码，想搞清楚这些二进制到底都是干嘛的，因为根本没有文档。然后我把整套东西在 CoreOS 上跑起来了。所以当 Google 正式发布的时候，当然有那个很有名的 Docker keynote，他们把它亮出来了，甚至某种程度上连 Docker 自己都有点意外。我记得他们在 Hacker News 上发帖冲到了第一。然后我们也发了一帖：嘿，他们刚刚讲了这个东西，但这里是怎么在 CoreOS 上跑它，还有一些示例和命令。我还得打几个 patch 才能让它真的跑起来，还得编一些二进制把各个部分粘起来。这些全是一夜之间做完的。一夜。完全没睡。我就跟大家说，嘿各位，我终于搞明白怎么把这些东西编译出来了。我觉得 kubelet 是干这个的，你得把那个放到那里。他们当时有个 kube up 的 SSH 脚本，但我得反着把它拆开，因为我又不用 Google Cloud。我们用的是 CoreOS，跑在 bare metal 上，所以我还得去做一些 pixie-boot 的东西。不过我觉得如果你把这些东西都放到正确的位置，有个 etcd 的部分，那是我们的，我们知道怎么用它。但我觉得 API server 会连到它。那时候还没有 volumes，也没有 config maps。所以你能做的，就是先把这套东西搭起来，然后提交一个配置，它基本上就在后台用 Docker 跑起来。好，这些我都能写成文档。于是我们把一切都跑通了，我写了一份很不错的指南。我们团队里有人把它发到了 CoreOS 官方网站上。然后我们又把这个发到 Hacker News，上了第一。大家都在说，Google 刚发布了这个东西，我们还不知道它是什么。然后 Kelsey 就把这东西跑起来了。现在我们知道它是什么了，知道怎么安装，也知道怎么运行了。所以大家开始下载 CoreOS，就只是为了玩 Kubernetes。我记得大概一周后我还有个 keynote。我都不记得自己当初给那个会议投的是什么题目了，因为通常你都是提前几个月提交。我就说，嘿，我知道这场演讲本来应该讲那个，但今天不讲了，今天讲 Kubernetes。大家都在问，那是什么？我说，对，它刚刚发布，我给你们演示。我就开始给大家做 Kubernetes 的 live demo，演示怎么让它跑起来，怎么结合 CoreOS 和这些东西一起用。但团队当时还是没有完全买账，因为我们自己有自己的 roadmap。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: and also at the time and you had your own fleet management software yeah we had our own fleet management and Kubernetes was now competing with it with fleet
text: 而且当时你们自己也有一套 fleet management software。对吧，你们有自己的 fleet management，而 Kubernetes 现在等于是在跟 Fleet 竞争。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: specifically yeah and also we didn't know we can trust it right remember Docker is the king Docker is number one there's Docker Swarm right and we're with fleet and right now we're like Google also launched years prior a thing called let me contain that for you it was a container runtime written in C++ to compete with Docker and no one cared yeah and so we didn't know like maybe no one's gonna care about this either it's only when I started going home and starting doing small contributions and starting to read everything and getting a feel for it I was like no there's something here and so maybe two or three months and luckily the founders of course were really nice Alex Povey and Brandon they were just like they didn't get mad or anything I was contributing nights and weekends kind of like I was at the other company all of my keynotes were more like Kubernetes plus CoreOS and I remember at some point it was inevitable we all got in a room I became the product manager of CoreOS at the time and Alex was like I think you got the vision here and we got everybody in the basement in San Francisco in the office we were like hey guys all in on Kubernetes fleet deprecate it. All these things that we're building, deprecate it. We're going to go all in. And I'm glad we did because Alex Povey came up with the name Operators, which is like a core idea in the Kubernetes community. We put all this effort in there, meeting another guy working on a thing called CNI, which is the networking layer for Kubernetes. And what Kubernetes really meant for me was that previous 15 years of experience as a practitioner in the data center, learning promise theory, learning Puppet where it works well where it doesn't and then understanding that Puppet wasn't the only way and then making going through all of these loops and so when I ended at Kubernetes it felt like this would be the thing I would build if I only knew how and that's the way I explained it to the rest of the world and at that GopherCon maybe the next one there were people from the now early Kubernetes community it's a small company called Kismatic they were kind of a Coral West competitive to some degree but they came up with the idea like we should have a conference just like GopherCon. We called it CoopCon. Joseph got the logo going and the Kismatic team kind of put up the money for the first event and we really welcomed the entire community and now it's been 12 years later and the CNCF has done a fabulous job of keeping it going. Now there's like what 13,000 people here in Amsterdam keeping that thing going. So I asked this from Kat Cosgrove as well who was on the podcast.
text: 对，就是直接竞争。而且我们当时也不知道该不该信它。你想想，当时 Docker 是王者，Docker 排第一，还有 Docker Swarm。我们这边有 Fleet。再说，Google 早几年也发布过一个东西，差不多像是“让我替你把它容器化”那种，是个用 C++ 写的 container runtime，用来跟 Docker 竞争，但根本没人关心。所以我们也不知道，也许这次也一样，根本不会有人在意。直到我开始回家以后自己做一些小贡献，开始把所有东西都读一遍，慢慢摸出感觉，我才意识到，不，这里面有东西。大概两三个月后，幸运的是，创始人们人都特别好，Alex Polvi 和 Brandon 他们完全没有生气之类的。我那时候晚上和周末都在贡献代码，有点像我在上一家公司时那样。我所有的 keynote 基本都变成了 Kubernetes 加 CoreOS。我记得到了某个时候，这已经是不可避免的了。我们所有人进了一个会议室，那时候我成了 CoreOS 的产品经理。Alex 说，我觉得你看到了这里面的愿景。于是我们把大家都叫到旧金山办公室的地下室里，跟大家说，伙计们，全面押注 Kubernetes，Fleet 弃用。我们现在在做的这些东西，统统弃用。我们要 all in。我很庆幸我们这么做了，因为 Alex Polvi 提出了 Operators 这个名字，这后来成了 Kubernetes 社区里的一个核心概念。我们把大量精力都投了进去，还认识了另一个人在做一个叫 CNI 的东西，也就是 Kubernetes 的网络层。对我来说，Kubernetes 真正意味着的是，我前面 15 年作为一线实践者在数据中心积累的经验——学习 promise theory，学习 Puppet 在什么场景好用、什么场景不好用，然后理解 Puppet 并不是唯一的路，再一路经历这些反复迭代——最后走到 Kubernetes 的时候，我感觉这就是那个“如果我早知道怎么做，我自己也会想造出来”的东西。我也是这样向外界解释它的。后来在那届 GopherCon，或者可能是再下一届，已经有了早期 Kubernetes 社区的人。有一家很小的公司叫 Kismatic，某种程度上算是 CoreOS 的竞争对手，但他们提出了一个想法：我们也该办一个像 GopherCon 那样的大会。我们把它叫做 KubeCon。Joseph 把 logo 做出来了，Kismatic 团队也为第一届活动出了钱，我们真的把整个社区都欢迎进来了。现在 12 年过去了，CNCF 在持续推动这件事上做得非常棒。现在你看，阿姆斯特丹这里都有大概 13000 人在让这件事继续往前走。所以我也把这个问题问过 Kat Cosgrove，她之前也上过播客。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: What do you think really made Kubernetes break through and then just become the de facto way of orchestrating nodes and just winning again? There was, at CoreOS, you were building Fleet, Docker had Swarm. Like, as you said in the beginning, it didn't seem like this would be this big. I think the number one success criteria was Docker.
text: 你觉得真正让 Kubernetes 突破出来、然后变成节点编排的事实标准、最终胜出的原因是什么？当时在 CoreOS，你们在做 Fleet，Docker 有 Swarm。就像你一开始说的，那时候看起来它并不像会变得这么大。我觉得排第一的成功因素其实是 Docker。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So remember, there was Mesos and Mesosphere. and they had their own runtime. HashiCorp had come out with Nomad and they had their own runtime. But the biggest runtime that had already got global consensus was Docker. So by that time, there were so many Docker containers and Docker workflows and Docker Swarm, maybe the Achilles heel to Docker Swarm was its design. They tried to take the Docker API, which worked really well for one node and expand it across multiple systems. And it was not the right API to scale to another type of thing that we needed. And so they kept trying. They tried to add storage. They tried to add networking. But the Docker API was never meant for that. And so the Kubernetes team was smart. Instead of trying to say Google's better than everyone and everything, they did a couple of things correct. Let's just use etcd. Let's just use Docker. So you take those two things and you take the experience of the people who wrote the Omega paper, which is kind of thinking about what would come after Borg and at least the things like Mesos.
text: 你要记得，当时还有 Mesos 和 Mesosphere，它们有自己的 runtime。HashiCorp 也推出了 Nomad，也有自己的 runtime。但当时已经获得全球共识、规模最大的 runtime 是 Docker。所以到那个时候，已经有海量的 Docker 容器、Docker 工作流，还有 Docker Swarm。Docker Swarm 的阿喀琉斯之踵，可能就是它的设计。他们试图把 Docker API——这个在单节点上表现非常好的 API——扩展到多个系统上去。但对于我们真正需要扩展到的那种场景来说，它并不是正确的 API。所以他们一直在尝试。他们试着加 storage，试着加 networking。但 Docker API 从一开始就不是为这些设计的。所以 Kubernetes 团队很聪明，他们没有试图说 Google 比所有人都强、什么都要自己来。他们有几件事做对了。先直接用 etcd。再直接用 Docker。所以你把这两样东西，再加上那些写过 Omega 论文的人积累的经验——那篇论文本质上是在思考 Borg 之后会是什么，至少也包括了像 Mesos 这类东西——合在一起。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: So Omega was a system where Google would have built after Borg, but they never built it, right?
text: 所以 Omega 本来是 Google 在 Borg 之后会做的一个系统，但他们其实一直没真正把它做出来，对吧？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, so they had elements of it. So like the omelet, you know, this like agent that would like be more declarative. A lot of hints from the Kubernetes world that will come later.
text: 对，他们当时有一些相关的组成部分。比如说 omelet，你知道吧，就是那种更偏声明式的 agent。后来 Kubernetes 世界里的很多思路，其实那时候已经有些苗头了。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: And then just to be clear, Borg was and is still Google's way of managing their back then hundreds of thousands now probably millions or tens of millions of servers and they were best in the world. Yeah,
text: 那我再确认一下，Borg 当时、甚至现在，都是 Google 用来管理他们服务器的方式——那时候是几十万台，现在可能已经是几百万甚至上千万台了，而且他们当时就是全球最强。对吧。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: I think Borg was one of these things where you integrate the hardware, the software, the package management, the configuration management, MapReduce, right? Borg is this thing that just expands and grows. In some ways, I guess it's extensible, but all of that insight and knowledge. But then they get so much experience with that. If you were to do it again, what would you do? And you read the Omega paper. and it's like here's how we what we learn from scheduling it doesn't need to be that complex especially for certain workloads you don't need this like high performance over engineered thing there's a simple way to do scheduling especially if you can get the scheduler a bit more metadata about the workload the also big game changes now instead of talking about Java versus Python versus Ruby you only have to talk about scheduling Docker containers And so I think that's the number one success criteria that we were already off to a running start because you could just reuse the same Docker containers. You didn't have to rebuild a new image thing. So given that What they tried to do in the early beginning was just fill in the gaps. And in many ways, yes, it's a new system, but it fills in the gaps. The one gap that they filled in was Docker had an entry point. So if you needed a Ruby app that needed Nginx and your process, you used to have to write a little shell script, an entry point script that would do all of this magic, almost imitating an init system. Kubernetes is like, no, no, no, you don't need to do that. You can just make separate containers and then Kubernetes will run them as a process tree. And so for many people, it's like, finally, now we can have a clear way of thinking about application architecture. Like blocks. Like blocks. Now, instead of like, you have to open the entry point to see what we're going to do versus full lifecycle management independent. So it solves that number one problem. The other big one that I think that they solve, number one, we went from infrastructure as code to infrastructure as data. And infrastructure as code is like, if this, do that. Bring in this module, for loops, all this stuff. And Kubernetes is like, no, no, no. You have to specify exactly the containers you want, how much memory that they need, and then we have the status field to tell you if they were running or not. And you would take this data object that you could write by hand, give it to an API, and then the control loops would operate on this state. So that means it didn't matter if you had Ruby, Python, or anything. You can just take your IDE, write some YAML, give it to another tool, manipulate the YAML, and then pass it down to the API servers. You can build any combination that you want it without having to be a compiler first. That to me was a fundamental game changer that I don't know if a lot of people understood why it felt very easy to onboard the Kubernetes. Kube CTL apply object. Off you go. And the last thing I think, credit to Brendan Burns, the ability to extend Kubernetes in a first class way. OpenStack didn't have it Mesos didn't really have it in Mesos you have a scheduler and you built the other part of the scheduler so you can have Spark, Hadoop, Marathon but you had all these other tools sitting on top of a thing so an extension in Mesos was heavy almost like a whole nother system the thing that makes Kubernetes powerful there's a data model we gave infrastructure a type system So instead of imperative shell scripts, you finally had types. So if anyone's ever come from like Python to a type language, types do a lot for you in terms of cognitive overhead. Like you really know what goes into this function. If you put the wrong thing into this function, it doesn't even work. Like you can't pass a string where integer needs to be. Kubernetes brings the same semantics to infrastructure. And finally, now it's much safer to automate things because you're gluing together things that actually have structure and types.
text: 我觉得 Borg 属于那种把硬件、软件、包管理、配置管理、MapReduce 全都整合在一起的系统，对吧？Borg 就是这样一个不断扩张、不断生长的东西。从某种意义上说，它当然是可扩展的，但里面沉淀了大量洞察和经验。不过当他们积累了这么多经验之后，就会开始想：如果再做一遍，你会怎么做？然后你去看 Omega 那篇论文，就会发现他们在说：这是我们从调度里学到的东西。它其实没必要那么复杂，尤其是对某些工作负载来说，你根本不需要那种高性能、过度设计的东西。调度其实可以有更简单的做法，特别是如果你能给调度器更多关于工作负载的元数据。另一个巨大的变化是，现在你不用再讨论 Java、Python、Ruby 之间的差别了，你只需要讨论 Docker 容器的调度。 所以我觉得，这就是最核心的成功条件之一：我们一开始就已经占了先机，因为你可以直接复用同样的 Docker 容器，不需要重新搞一套新的镜像体系。基于这一点，他们早期想做的，其实就是把缺口补上。从很多方面看，没错，它是个新系统，但它本质上是在补缺口。 其中一个补上的缺口是，Docker 有 entry point。所以如果你有个 Ruby 应用，需要 Nginx 再加上你自己的进程，以前你得写个小 shell 脚本，写个 entry point script，把这些魔法都串起来，几乎是在模拟一个 init system。Kubernetes 则说，不不不，你不用这么干。你可以直接拆成多个独立容器，然后 Kubernetes 会把它们当成一个进程树来运行。对很多人来说，这就像终于有了一种清晰的方式去理解应用架构。像积木一样。对，像积木一样。现在你不用再打开 entry point 才知道它到底要干嘛，而是可以做完整生命周期的独立管理。所以它先解决了第一个大问题。 另一个我觉得他们解决的大问题是：我们从 infrastructure as code 走向了 infrastructure as data。infrastructure as code 的思路是：如果这样，就做那样；引入这个模块；写 for 循环；各种逻辑。Kubernetes 则说，不不不。你要精确描述你想要哪些容器、它们需要多少内存，然后我们用 status 字段告诉你它们是不是在运行。你把这个数据对象——甚至可以手写——交给一个 API，然后控制循环就会围绕这个状态去运作。 这意味着，不管你用的是 Ruby、Python 还是别的什么语言，都无所谓。你只要打开 IDE，写点 YAML，交给另一个工具，操作这些 YAML，然后再把它传给 API server。你可以构建任何你想要的组合，而且不需要先变成一个编译器作者。对我来说，这才是根本性的游戏规则改变。我觉得很多人未必真正理解，为什么 Kubernetes 的上手会感觉那么容易。kubectl apply 一个对象，然后就可以开始了。 最后一点，我觉得 Brendan Burns 值得被记一功，就是他让 Kubernetes 能以一等公民的方式被扩展。OpenStack 没有这个能力，Mesos 其实也没有。在 Mesos 里，你有一个 scheduler，然后你还得把 scheduler 的另一部分自己做出来，所以你可以有 Spark、Hadoop、Marathon，但这些其实都是一堆额外工具叠在某个底层之上。所以在 Mesos 里，扩展是很重的，几乎像再造一个完整系统。 Kubernetes 真正强大的地方在于：它有一个数据模型，我们给基础设施引入了类型系统。也就是说，不再是命令式 shell 脚本了，你终于有了类型。任何从 Python 转到强类型语言的人都知道，类型能大幅降低认知负担。你会非常清楚这个函数到底接受什么参数；如果你传错了，它根本就跑不起来。比如该传整数的地方，你不能塞个字符串进去。Kubernetes 把同样的语义带到了基础设施领域。于是终于，自动化变得安全得多，因为你拼接起来的是那些真正有结构、有类型的东西。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: I was about to say, the reason we love types and every language just goes towards them is safety and it eliminates a whole class of errors.
text: 我刚刚正想说，我们之所以喜欢类型，而且几乎所有语言最后都在往这个方向走，就是因为它更安全，而且能消灭整整一大类错误。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, you can do things like static analysis. You can have other tools compile different things and ensure that they have the exact same thing. And you have this value that tells you that's not the right object. That's not the right field. And so once you have all of those things, you can build a really nice deployment system. So kubectl deploy these containers, no problem. What about everything else? So instead of trying to evolve Kubernetes to do everything, Brendan Burns, I remember sitting next to him, he's like, Kelsey, let me show you this thing. You can extend Kubernetes just by giving it a description of what you would like your object to be. So if you're thinking about this from the rest world, hey, I need a user. Here's the credit operations and just give it to the thing and it does everything else for you. And so when that came out, it's like, so if I want to manage, let's say a firewall. Yeah, you can describe a firewall. and you give that to Kubernetes and all the tooling works. You can now say kubectl apply a firewall. And so now we got tools like cert management where if you want a certificate from Let's Encrypt, you can just say what domain you want, where it should live, give it to it, and now you had a first-class extension. You didn't have to uninstall it. You didn't have to make some magical binary and it really didn't matter what language you wanted. because once you put the data model in place, you got the machinery, and if you like Python, you can have Python running the loop, grab the data, and then make it so. If Bash was your thing, you can literally pull the config using a Bash script and make it so and just update the status field. That opened up the entire ecosystem. So Cisco could come in, do what they wanted to do. Red Hat can come in, OpenShift, and do what they wanted to do. To me, that was the game changer that brought the rest of the community in.
text: 对，你就可以做静态分析。你可以让别的工具去编译不同的东西，并确保它们完全一致。你还有这种反馈机制告诉你：这不是对的对象，这不是对的字段。所以一旦你把这些都具备了，就能构建出一个非常漂亮的部署系统。比如 kubectl 部署这些容器，没问题。那其他东西怎么办？ 所以 Brendan Burns 当时的思路不是把 Kubernetes 一路演化成什么都做，我记得我当时就坐在他旁边，他说，Kelsey，我给你看个东西。你只要告诉 Kubernetes 你希望对象长什么样，就能扩展它。你如果从 REST 世界来理解的话，就是：嘿，我需要一个 user，这是相关操作，把它交给系统，剩下的它都会帮你处理。 这个东西一出来，我就想，那如果我想管理一个 firewall 呢？他说，可以啊，你描述一个 firewall，然后交给 Kubernetes，整套工具链就都能工作。你现在甚至可以说 kubectl apply 一个 firewall。于是后来我们就有了 cert management 这样的工具：如果你想从 Let's Encrypt 拿一个证书，你只要说清楚你要哪个域名、它应该放在哪儿，交给系统，它就成了一个一等公民的扩展。你不需要卸载什么东西，也不需要搞一个神秘的二进制程序，而且你想用什么语言其实都无所谓。 因为一旦数据模型到位，整套机制就都有了。如果你喜欢 Python，那就让 Python 去跑控制循环，拿到数据，然后把事情做成。如果你偏爱 Bash，你甚至真的可以用 Bash 脚本把配置拉下来，完成操作，然后更新 status 字段。这个能力一下子把整个生态都打开了。于是 Cisco 可以进来做他们想做的事，Red Hat 也可以进来，通过 OpenShift 做他们想做的事。对我来说，这才是真正把整个社区都带进来的那个转折点。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: And then you joined Google, not very surprisingly at this point, I guess from the outside, of course, but how did that go? You've been contributing to Kubernetes as well. The team was there. Did you join the Kubernetes team?
text: 然后你就加入了 Google，这到这一步从外部看其实也不算太意外了。不过当时具体是怎么发生的？你那时候也已经在给 Kubernetes 做贡献了，团队也在那里。你是直接加入 Kubernetes 团队了吗？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: No, so by that time, I met CoreOS. Kubernetes has definitely taken off. I'm giving lots of keynotes. Now everyone wants to know my opinions. I'm making all these prototypes. I'm kind of moving things forward. There's a Kubernetes book now, right? I'm a co-author with Brendan Burns, Joe Beda at the time. I'm like, you know what? I'm thinking about my exit. So in our careers, we do a lot of work to get into this field. All the certifications, the boot camps, the studying, some of us, college. And then once we get in, we're thinking about career progression. Dev, senior engineer, principal engineer, distinguished engineer. and we spend almost our entire lives on that trajectory and our field is so young that some of our pioneers are finally like no longer here for the first time we're not used to that and we're not really used to people retiring like Rob Pike just retired the concept of an exit for an individual contributor or a leader in the tech space is we don't we didn't have a lot of those Linus is still at it. He's not retired, right? So we don't spend a lot of time thinking about the exit plan in our field. If you're a professional athlete, your body will tell you when it's time to go. And so at CoreOS, we reached this peak. I felt like I've done everything I've ever wanted to do. In the tech industry. In the tech industry from 1999 to being unsure of myself, to seeing myself on the side of museums, buildings, full landscape view because people were coming to see what I think about where technology is actually going. And so it comes full circle. You get a bit of a taste of the fame. You can look at GitHub and you meet people. We use your libraries. We use your command line tools. I started my career, like some people were not even born when I graduated high school and they started their careers from those books. And so I felt at the time that I had come full circle and I was starting to think about the exit. Part of that journey, I remember spending time with Jet Propulsion Labs, JPL, part of NASA. And I remember being there and I was so excited because the movie The Martian had just wrapped up filming there. And they gave me a tour of the facility, like the Mars rover, the new one before they launched it to Mars. They were QAing, it was just going in a circle around the track. And I'm like, had a little laser on there so it can split rocks and they were showing me how they improved the wheels over time. I went to another lab and and there were a bunch of scientists working in there and it looked like a fish tank and I described it that was like yeah we determined that if you want to replicate parts of Mars surface and I might be getting this wrong that the rocks that you find in like a fish tank we can replicate some of this stuff and maybe it's slightly different than that so I'm watching these people work and they show me like how spacecraft has evolved over the last 20 or 30 years and I'm like wow you all seem to have an actual purpose For the first time I've seen people using technology not to just make more apps, not to add numbers in a database, but to actually have humanity do something. And so they were not all about Kubernetes and Docker and Python and Go. They were like, we're just trying to get a person to Mars and back again. I remember part of the interview process, their interview questions are, if you had to deflect a meteor, how would you do it? But it has to hit one state. So now you're in the leadership position, what would you do? And you're just explaining the answer like, you know, I would kind of bring a bunch of experts and then, you know, you got to think about the ability to evacuate people and you have to explain yourself. And the core part of my answer was you would have to explain yourself almost 24 hour live stream. Here's the trajectory, giving everyone the countdown, explain every decision you're making. I chose transparency. I chose truth. I chose like, look, we have to deflect it. There's no way to make it zero. So we've chosen this state. And this is the evacuation plan. And we estimate that this number of people won't make it. We're just being honest with you. No need for conspiracy theories. It's live. And I was like, wow, what an interview. A 20-year career at that point. Never had an interview that made me feel that way. And so I actually was going to go to NASA after CoreOS. I even signed the employment agreement. I was going to move to Pasadena, California. and work at NASA on the Mars mission and lead up the infrastructure and the infrastructure teams. And of course Google called and was like, hey, come to Google. And at that point I was like, for what? You have hundreds of thousands of employees I admire Google. I've been there before, but not in that capacity. I was going to go to the headquarters. Not to the data center, but to the headquarters. And I've always admired people like Brian Grant, Eric Toon, Don Chin, all of these wonderful people that I got to work with through the Kubernetes community. In many ways, I felt like I was already working on the team because by that time, I had commit access to Kubernetes. So I kind of felt like All the things I wanted to achieve in that regard was there. And so I was like, why would I come work there? I'm just going to be a cog in the wheel. I'm going to go there. I'm just going to disappear. They're going to just make me work on Google stuff all day long. What's the value in that? I've seen the peak of this. And they were like, we won't do that. I was given the opportunity to do DevRel. And it's the first time I ever did it. But DevRel represented freedom. No tickets, no right code measured against SWE benchmarks. I was like, I don't want that. I wanted to be able to make impact. And so the team was smart. They were like, look, we got this area called DevRel and we'll let you define what you do.
text: 没有，到那个时候，我已经认识了 CoreOS。Kubernetes 也确实已经彻底起飞了。我在做很多 keynote，现在每个人都想听听我的看法。我做了很多原型，某种程度上也在推动整个事情往前走。那时候甚至已经有一本 Kubernetes 的书了，对吧？我是和 Brendan Burns、Joe Beda 一起合著的。然后我就想，你知道吗，我开始考虑退出这件事了。 我们的职业生涯里，前半段会做很多努力才能进入这个行业。各种认证、训练营、学习，有些人还上大学。等真正进来了，我们想的就是职业发展路径：Dev、资深工程师、principal engineer、distinguished engineer。我们几乎把整个人生都花在这条轨道上。而我们的行业又太年轻了，以至于一些先驱人物第一次开始不在了，我们其实并不习惯这个。我们也不太习惯看到人退休。像 Rob Pike 才刚退休。对于技术领域里的 individual contributor 或领导者来说，“退出”这个概念，我们过去其实没什么现成样本。Linus 现在还在干呢，他也没退休，对吧？所以我们这个行业并不会花很多时间去思考退出计划。 如果你是职业运动员，你的身体会告诉你什么时候该离开了。但在 CoreOS 的时候，我们已经到了一个高点。我感觉自己已经完成了所有曾经想在科技行业里做到的事。是的，就是在科技行业里。从 1999 年那个对自己没什么把握的人，到后来我能看到自己的形象出现在博物馆、建筑外墙上，整面大幅展示，因为人们会专门来听我怎么看技术的未来到底会走向哪里。那种感觉就像一个完整的闭环。你会稍微尝到一点名气的滋味。你会去看 GitHub，会遇到别人对你说：我们在用你的库，我们在用你的命令行工具。我刚开始职业生涯的时候，有些人甚至都还没出生；而他们后来是看着那些书开始自己的职业生涯的。 所以那时候我觉得，自己已经走完了一个完整循环，也开始认真思考退出这件事。那段旅程里，有一部分经历我记得特别清楚：我去过 Jet Propulsion Labs，也就是 JPL，NASA 的一部分。我当时特别兴奋，因为电影《The Martian》刚刚在那里结束拍摄。他们带我参观了整个设施，比如 Mars rover，新的那台，在发射去火星之前我就看到了。他们当时还在做 QA，它就在跑道上绕圈。我心想，哇，上面还有个小激光器，可以打碎岩石。他们还给我讲这些年他们是怎么不断改进轮子的。 我又去了另一个实验室，里面有一群科学家在工作，看起来有点像鱼缸。我当时是这么形容的。他们说，是的，我们发现如果你想复现火星表面的某些部分——我这里细节可能记得不完全对——像鱼缸里那种石头，就能模拟其中一些情况，也许实际说法和这个略有不同。总之我就在看这些人工作，他们还给我展示了过去二三十年航天器是怎么演进的。我当时就想，哇，你们这些人看起来是真的有一个明确的使命。 这是我第一次看到，人们使用技术不是为了再多做几个 app，不是为了往数据库里再加几行数字，而是真的在推动整个人类去做点什么。所以他们关注的根本不是 Kubernetes、Docker、Python、Go 这些。他们想的是：我们只是想把人送上火星，再安全带回来。 我记得面试流程里有一道题是：如果你必须偏转一颗陨石，你会怎么做？但它一定会撞上美国某一个州。那现在你在领导岗位上，你会怎么办？你就得把答案讲出来，比如说，我会先召集一批专家，然后你还得考虑人员疏散能力之类的，你必须把自己的思路解释清楚。而我回答里最核心的一点是：你必须几乎用 24 小时直播的方式把一切解释清楚。告诉大家轨迹是什么，实时倒计时，解释你做的每一个决定。我选择的是透明，我选择的是说真话。我会说，听着，我们必须让它偏转，不可能做到零风险。所以我们选了这个州。这是疏散计划。我们估计会有这么多人来不及撤离。我们就是坦诚告诉你们。没必要让阴谋论滋生。全程直播。 我当时就想，哇，这面试也太不一样了。那时候我已经有 20 年职业生涯了，却从来没有一次面试让我有这种感觉。所以其实在 CoreOS 之后，我本来是要去 NASA 的。我连 employment agreement 都签了。我本来要搬去 California 的 Pasadena，去 NASA 参与火星任务，负责基础设施以及基础设施团队。 结果当然，Google 打电话来了，说，嘿，来 Google 吧。那时候我的反应是：去干嘛？你们有几十万员工。我当然很敬佩 Google。我以前也去过，但不是以那种身份。我这次要去的是总部，不是数据中心，而是总部。我一直都很欣赏 Brian Grant、Eric Toon、Don Chin，还有这些我通过 Kubernetes 社区合作过的优秀的人。从很多方面来说，我那时甚至觉得自己已经像是在那个团队里工作了，因为到那时候我已经有 Kubernetes 的 commit 权限了。所以我感觉，在那方面我想达成的目标，其实已经差不多都实现了。 所以我当时想，我为什么还要去那儿上班？我去了不就是个大机器里的一个小齿轮吗？我去了就会消失掉。他们只会让我整天做 Google 内部的事。那样的价值在哪？我已经见过这件事的高峰了。然后他们说，我们不会那样做。 他们给了我一个做 DevRel 的机会。这也是我第一次做这个角色。但 DevRel 对我来说代表的是自由。没有工单，不用写代码然后按 SWE 的基准来衡量。我当时就想，我不要那种。我想要的是能真正产生影响。所以那个团队很聪明，他们说，你看，我们有这么一个叫 DevRel 的领域，我们可以让你自己来定义你要做什么。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: So you got to write your job description pretty much.
text: 所以基本上你的岗位说明书都得自己来写。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, but as an entrepreneur, I know how this goes. How does it go? If you come in and you really do DevRel stuff, in my mind, you're going to get fired. Because if you limit yourself to the external perception of DevRel, you go to conferences and you become more of an evangelist, you do tutorials and guides. For me, those are activities. I'm a person of impact. And so the first thing I did when I got to Google was like, where's the customers? How do we make money on cloud? So I need to figure out how to talk to the customers. So hey, where's the sales reps? Hey, if you ever need anyone with Kubernetes expertise, call me. I will fly to Disney. I will go to Walmart on site and I will whiteboard for six hours because that's the revenue component. So globally, went to Australia, Canada, doesn't matter. We don't even have a region there yet. I'm going to bootstrap it. So now I'm growing my impact on the revenue side.
text: 对，但作为创业者，我知道这事会怎么发展。怎么发展呢？如果你进去之后真的只做那种 DevRel 的活，在我看来你最后会被开掉。因为如果你把自己限制在外界对 DevRel 的那种理解里——去参加大会，变成更像 evangelist，做教程、写指南——对我来说，那些都只是活动。我是看重影响力的人。所以我到 Google 之后做的第一件事就是：客户在哪？Cloud 怎么赚钱？所以我得搞清楚怎么跟客户交流。于是我就说，销售在哪？你们谁要是需要 Kubernetes 专家，叫我。我可以飞去 Disney，我可以去 Walmart 现场，我可以站在白板前讲六个小时，因为那才是跟营收直接相关的部分。所以我在全球各地都这么干，Australia、Canada，都无所谓。那边甚至还没有正式区域，我也要先把它从零搭起来。这样一来，我在营收这一侧的影响力就开始扩大了。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: And the reason you said if you would have gone as devil, you would have been fired because you would have not been generating any revenue. I just felt like I was going to be fired. But this is just like thinking as an entrepreneur as like you want I want to make money.
text: 你刚才说如果你真按那种 DevRel 的方式去做，就会被开掉，原因是你创造不了任何营收，对吧？我就是觉得自己会被开掉。但这其实就是创业者思维：你就是想赚钱。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: I want to make sure that I'm impacting the business. And for most businesses, revenue is the criteria. And no one ever made me do that, by the way. It wasn't like, they're real, we're going to hit you with the metrics. It's like, no, no, no. To me, I understood the value of revenue. So I would go out and I was able to do it in an authentic way. I'm just talking about the same things I was talking about before. And then product impact. This is cloud. Why limit yourself to Kubernetes? There's serverless, there's databases, there's metrics, there's so many things here. So now I'm like, I need to learn everything. And I want to employ all of my skills. So it turns out my time in financial services means I could be an exec sponsor. I knew how to go from hello world to hello revenue. So if I got into an exec briefing, I didn't waste everyone's time showing them the latest feature of Kubernetes. It doesn't make sense. They want to know how these tools come together to lead to actual impact and outcomes. And so I matured there. And I also got smart. You got to go to other teams and you read the OKRs. So another team might say, hey Kelsey, we're really trying to get more adoption on our metric stack. And I remember the first thing that I started implementing at Google was a thing called empathetic engineering. So you have a lot of smart Googlers. These people are brilliant. I mean... extremely brilliant to the point where the hardest problem Google had, in my opinion, was what to build. Not how to build it, what to build. They could do that. And in some cases, you end up with like five messaging systems. But the thing is, what to build seemed to be the most pressing problem. And so the one thing that I tried to do was like, how do you convince other engineers, you know, their manager, How do you give them to trust you? And I started these empathetic engineering sessions where the first one was like, get the Kubernetes team in one room, all of them, engineering offsite. I want you all to install Kubernetes, but you can't use any scripts. And remember, some of them are distinguished engineers and principal engineers. Some of them worked on board. Some of them are just the original creators of Kubernetes itself. And it was so fun to watch them struggle because it's like, do we install Docker first? What version of Docker? Can we put this on Ubuntu? Or does it need to be Red Hat? And so an hour goes by, teams of four are like, nah, man, this doesn't work. I was like, great, you all can stop. I'm going to show you how I would do it. And of course, I know how to do this because I get to prepare, right? So not a knock against them. I'm just like, all right, Debian, tune the kernel this way, put Docker on there, put etcd, put the API server, put all these things. There you go. That's how you do it. And I was like, yeah, I mean, of course you had to prepare, of course. And so The question then was, from an engineering perspective, how will we make this better? And then people were like, well, if we had OS packages, this could have been avgit install, and we could have just used local machiners. Like, that's a good idea. And someone was like, we will make that happen. Another person was like, even if you had those packages, though, you still need to know what order and where the config files go. So kubadmin was born, which was a command line tool that gave you a procedural thing. But the other thing that I remember from my career was, I don't want just a tool that abstracts everything from me. I want to know how it works. So I wrote the guide, Kubernetes The Hard Way. And that guide is what I used to help teach people at GitHub in the early days, pre-Microsoft, how to like run Kubernetes on bare metal and walk them through that guide. And so it was that empathetic engineering that helped me make a huge impact on cloud because I can go to every team, every org, and instead of guessing what their roadmap should be, giving someone who would spend time in the field, Given someone that had this enterprise background, hands-on experience across lots of tooling, I knew where people were coming from, I would say, based on where Google sits in the landscape and in the competitive landscape, given what our abilities are and what our customers need, I think this is it. But I never said it that way.
text: 我想确保自己是在真正影响业务。而对大多数公司来说，衡量标准就是营收。顺便说一句，从来没人要求我这么做。不是说“你是 DevRel，我们要拿指标来考核你”。不是这样。是我自己理解营收的价值。所以我会主动出去做，而且我能用一种很真实的方式去做。我讲的其实还是我之前一直在讲的那些东西。然后是产品影响力。这是 cloud，为什么要把自己限制在 Kubernetes 上？还有 serverless、数据库、metrics，这里有太多东西了。所以我当时就想，我得把这些都学会，而且我要把自己所有技能都用上。后来发现，我在金融服务行业的经历让我可以去做 exec sponsor。我知道怎么从 hello world 走到 hello revenue。所以如果我去参加 exec briefing，我不会浪费大家时间给他们演示 Kubernetes 的最新功能，那没有意义。他们想知道的是，这些工具怎么组合起来，最终带来真正的影响和结果。于是我在那段时间也成长了很多。还有一点，我也变聪明了。你得去看别的团队在做什么，去读他们的 OKR。比如另一个团队可能会说：“Kelsey，我们现在特别想提升我们 metric stack 的采用率。”我记得我在 Google 最早开始推动的一件事，叫 empathetic engineering。Google 有很多非常聪明的工程师，这些人真的太厉害了，厉害到在我看来，Google 最难的问题不是“怎么造”，而是“该造什么”。怎么造他们完全能搞定。有时候甚至会搞出五套 messaging system。但问题在于，“该造什么”似乎才是最紧迫的。所以我当时一直在想，怎么说服其他工程师，怎么让他们的经理信任你，怎么让他们愿意相信你。我就开始组织这种 empathetic engineering 的 session。第一个 session 是这样的：把 Kubernetes 团队所有人都拉到一个房间里，做一次 engineering offsite。我说，我要你们所有人都来安装 Kubernetes，但不许用任何脚本。要知道，这里面有 distinguished engineer、principal engineer，有些人参与过 board 层面的工作，还有些人就是 Kubernetes 的原始创建者。看他们卡壳特别有意思，因为他们会开始纠结：先装 Docker 吗？装哪个版本？能不能跑在 Ubuntu 上？还是必须用 Red Hat？一个小时过去了，四人小组都在说：“不行，这搞不定。”我说，太好了，你们可以停了。现在我来给你们演示，如果是我，我会怎么做。当然，我知道怎么做，因为我提前准备过，对吧？这不是在贬低他们。我只是说，好，Debian，内核这样调，装 Docker，再装 etcd、API server，把这些东西都配起来。好了，就是这么做。然后他们就说，对啊，你当然准备过。没错，当然准备过。接下来问题就变成了：从工程角度看，我们怎么把这件事做得更好？于是有人说，如果我们有 OS package，这事就可以直接 apt install 了，我们甚至可以直接用本地机器。很好，这是个好主意。然后有人说，这事我们来做。另一个人又说，就算你有这些 package，你还是得知道安装顺序，也得知道配置文件该放哪。于是 kubadmin 就诞生了，它是一个命令行工具，给你一套按步骤执行的流程。不过我职业生涯里还有一个一直很明确的想法：我不想只要一个把所有东西都抽象掉的工具，我还想知道它到底是怎么工作的。所以我写了那份指南，Kubernetes The Hard Way。早期在 GitHub、也就是 Microsoft 收购之前，我就是用这份指南教大家怎么在 bare metal 上跑 Kubernetes，带着他们一步一步走完整个过程。正是这种 empathetic engineering，帮我在 cloud 上产生了巨大的影响。因为我可以去每个团队、每个组织，不是靠猜他们的 roadmap 应该怎么做，而是给他们一个真正花时间待在一线、又有 enterprise 背景、还亲手用过大量工具的人提供判断。我知道大家的问题从哪里来，所以我会想：基于 Google 在整个行业和竞争格局中的位置，结合我们的能力以及客户真正需要的东西，我觉得方向应该是这个。但我从来不会直接这么说。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: I would get everyone in the room you would have them discover it have them discover it and nudge them so you knew what you think the key the most important problem areas were for example and then you orchestrated a session to help people I tried
text: 你会把大家都拉到一个房间里，让他们自己发现问题，再稍微推他们一把。也就是说，你其实心里知道最关键、最重要的问题区域大概在哪，比如说哪些地方最值得解决，然后你会设计一个 session 来帮助大家自己意识到这些。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: to always know the two things in multiple product areas that would make the most impact aka revenue that would get adoption and so and then launches versus landings you would put all these things in motion They would land at different times. So launches is we ship the thing, people have a big celebration. I know that doesn't matter as much as the landing. People are actually paying for it. So when I got good at that cycle, the promotions were a little predictable because I would be able to make impact. Sometimes you build prototypes and sometimes you would contribute to things like Cloud Functions. But the overall goal though was to use every skill you had. So working with PMs, we got to add Go support to Cloud Functions. We're the team that made Go. Amazon has support in Lambda for Go. We need to do this. And also, I have a keynote at the next GopherCon and I'm talking about serverless. And I have two choices. I have to use Lambda or we can use Go functions. And so we sprint. We get it done. We get all the Googlers reality. We get it checked in. It's in alpha. And I tell the PM, hey, you want to go to GopherCon? We'll launch it on stage. We launch it on stage. We tell people how we designed our worker, how the internals work. and then they can just come sign up that was the trifecta you do the work you have the vision you execute you launch and you land land and the landings compiled over seven years is how you go from like I think maybe L5 to Distinguished Engineer L9 that's like four promotions yeah four promotions seven years
text: 我会尽量始终清楚，在多个产品领域里，哪两件事最能带来最大影响，也就是营收，也最能带来采用率。然后还有一个区别，就是 launches 和 landings。你把这些事情都推动起来，它们会在不同时间点落地。launch 是我们把东西发出去了，大家热热闹闹庆祝一番。但我知道，那远没有 landing 重要。landing 是真的有人开始为它付钱了。当我把这个循环跑顺之后，晋升基本就变得有点可预测了，因为我能持续做出影响。有时候你会做 prototype，有时候你会参与像 Cloud Functions 这样的项目。但总体目标一直是：把你拥有的每一项技能都用起来。比如和 PM 一起合作，我们推动 Cloud Functions 增加 Go 支持。Go 都是我们这个团队做出来的，Amazon 的 Lambda 都支持 Go 了，我们必须得做。而且我接下来还要去 GopherCon 做 keynote，主题就是 serverless。我只有两个选择：要么用 Lambda，要么我们就用 Go functions。于是我们就冲刺，把它做出来，把所有 Googlers 都拉进现实节奏里，把代码提交进去，做到 alpha。然后我跟 PM 说，嘿，你想去 GopherCon 吗？我们直接在台上发布。于是我们就在台上发布，告诉大家我们的 worker 是怎么设计的，内部机制是怎么运作的，然后大家就可以直接来注册使用。这就是三连击：你亲自做事，你有愿景，你能执行；你 launch，还得能 landing。七年里这些 landing 累积起来，就是你从大概 L5 一路走到 Distinguished Engineer L9 的原因。差不多四次晋升吧，对，七年四次晋升。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: But it's interesting because a lot of times when you ask someone on, you know, how they got promoted, let's say, yeah, four times in seven years at a place like Google, I would expect just being naive that they will tell you like, okay, this is how I planned or like, this is what you need for each level. But it sounds like it's a very different, you just had landings that created impact. And you were focusing, do I read it correctly, that you were not focusing on your promotions or the next level. You just wanted to, you know, do the best work that you could.
text: 但这很有意思，因为很多时候你问一个人，比如说他在 Google 这种地方七年晋升了四次，他通常会告诉你，哦，我是怎么规划的，或者每个级别需要什么。以我这种比较天真的预期，我本来以为你也会这么说。但听起来完全不是这样。你更像是不断做出真正落地的成果，创造影响力。我的理解对吗？也就是说，你关注的并不是晋升本身，或者下一个级别，而是单纯想把自己能做的工作做到最好。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Oh no no I was focused on the next levels because that was the goal because think about it if my whole career I've always tried to acquire the skills and make the impact so I can move to the next level and so I googled the levels were expressed as promotions and there was a point in time in the org that I was in there was no level 7 for an individual contributor so now we have to make a level 7 And then once you get to a level 7, now you kind of pave the way for the other people that want to come up through that icy path. And then level 8 isn't the same as level 7. Level 8 isn't the same, or level 9 isn't the same as level 8. And yes, there is a formula to some of the promotions early. So from 3 to 4, 4 to 5 is a little formulaic. You have a ladder, there are things that are expected of you, and the decision-making on those type of promotions are localized, meaning your manager, maybe a director. But then outside of that, though, when you start to go higher, it's now expanded where there's now other teams that have to understand your impact in a way that can't be biased by a local team. So if you think you've made a lot of impact and then people across the org do not agree, that allows you to really throttle what impact means, right? Because if it's just your team, I really like this person. Now everyone's a distinguished engineer. And so I think Google did a really good job of saying, look, it was okay to be like L5 or 6 for your entire career.
text: 哦不不，我其实就是在盯着下一个级别，因为那本来就是目标。你想想看，我整个职业生涯一直都在努力获取技能、做出影响，这样我才能去到下一个层级。所以到了 Google，这些层级就是通过晋升来体现的。而且我所在的那个组织在某个阶段，individual contributor 根本没有 level 7，那我们就得先把 level 7 这条路做出来。等你到了 level 7，你其实也就在为后面那些想走 IC 路线的人铺路。然后 level 8 跟 level 7 不一样，level 9 跟 level 8 也不一样。没错，前期有些晋升确实是有公式可循的。比如 3 升 4、4 升 5，这些会更程式化一些。你有一套 ladder，里面写着对你的预期，这类晋升的决策也更本地化，通常就是你的经理，也许再加一个总监。但再往上走就不一样了，范围会扩大，会有其他团队也需要理解你的影响力，而且这种理解不能只被本地团队的偏见左右。如果你觉得自己影响力很大，但整个组织里其他人并不这么认为，那这其实就能很好地校准“影响力”到底意味着什么，对吧？因为如果只看你自己的团队，那很容易变成“我很喜欢这个人”，最后人人都是 distinguished engineer 了。所以我觉得 Google 在这点上做得很好：它明确告诉你，哪怕你整个职业生涯都停留在 L5 或 L6，也完全没问题。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Yeah, that's their terminal level. I think it used to be L5, now it's L4, actually. Oh, okay. But they moved it back because I think L5 has gotten a bit tricky and they don't want to fire pretty good L4s.
text: 对，那就是他们的终点级别。我记得以前是 L5，现在其实是 L4。哦，明白了。不过他们后来又调回去了，因为我觉得 L5 变得有点棘手，他们也不想把那些其实挺不错的 L4 给裁掉。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Exactly. And I think for a lot of people, you can follow the formula and get to where you need to be. But I was like, at the time, I think there was like a couple hundred distinguished engineers. So as an entrepreneur, if you show me the top of the mountain...
text: 没错。而且我觉得对很多人来说，你照着那套公式走，就能到你该到的位置。但我当时想的是，那时候 distinguished engineer 大概也就几百个人。所以作为一个创业者，如果你把山顶指给我看……

speaker: Gergely
text_en: I want to get there. So then you were targeting, you were like, all right, how can I get to the next one, to the next one? And impact was the name of the game, right?
text: 我就想爬上去。所以你当时的目标就是，好，那我怎么到下一级、再下一级？而且关键就在于影响力，对吧？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, and I guess the only thing I would probably add to the moment or to the situation was I wanted to do it authentically. And so there was one part of the time, I remember I didn't get one of the promotions, but I was getting promoted pretty fast. So, of course, people were like, dude, you need to actually make an impact before you get promoted again. Stop it. And that was good feedback. But I remember I took a chance. So there's a pattern to doing a promotion package. structure. There's examples and you go through all of this stuff and you get feedback. I remember one year I was like, I'm not doing that. This year, I'm just going to talk in like the first person. Hey, I'm Kelsey. I work on these things, not these things. These things are important to Google. So here's exactly what I did. But more importantly, here's the people I brought along. Here's the teams I've impacted. And here's the results of this. So I did this project and here are the results of that. And I'm talking in this way like I'm ignoring the process. I don't really care about the template and as someone who at that point in time was on some of the promotion communities where we're looking at the promotion packets and making a decision as a team I decided to write my packet for those people so that when they got it, because look, if you're having to do a read a lot of these, it's hard. You're like, oh man, they're all so dry. Everyone's being very safe. Everyone's only telling me what I want to hear. I don't even know the person after reading this whole packet. I said, I'm not going to do that. I want them to see me as if I was in the room advocating for myself and I remember getting feedback on that packet like Kelsey this is like come on bro this is you know things I was taking it seriously I wasn't making a mockery of it I just wanted to make sure that they understood what I was doing and I was aware that I wasn't just trying to play the game I was trying to approach this process authentically and I got promoted off of that packet and look it could just be sometimes the work sometimes speaks for itself But a lot of times people can't see the work if it's not presented correctly. And so that kind of slingshoted me. And the reason why I tell this story is that every distinguished engineer doesn't get there the same way. Everyone thinks like, oh, how much code did you write? What complex thing did you build? And for me, I think it was the impact on Google Cloud's culture. The empathetic engineering thing became an official thing that they used to onboard other engineers. It became things that had a whole team behind it. They used to go give them in a product manager that would evolve the program and integrate into HR. The philosophy around other engineers saying, Kelsey, we just did an empathy session. I want to show you the results of it because we're about to ship it. and then engineers started really thinking about the customer. I mean, Amazon was always known for that to some degree, but to help Google get on the same page I mean, I'm pretty sure other people had an impact, but there was a direct line of impact from those type of programs and also me diversifying, moving away from Kubernetes into the serverless realm, moving to the world where you're helping out the Postgres or the Spanner team at a Postgres interface, the Go team, getting a little bit better with cloud, just making other people successful around you is one of those things that helps you become distinguished. It's the impact, the ability to influence.
text: 对，不过我觉得对当时那个阶段、那个处境，我唯一想补充的一点是，我想用一种真实的方式去做到这件事。我记得有段时间，我有一次没拿到晋升，不过我当时晋升速度其实已经很快了。所以当然就有人说，哥们，你得先真正做出影响力，再想着下一次晋升，先别急。这反馈其实挺好的。但我记得我后来冒了一次险。因为写晋升材料其实是有套路的，有固定结构，有范例，你要把这些东西都走一遍，还会拿到反馈。我记得有一年我就想，我不这么写了。今年我就直接用第一人称来讲。嗨，我是 Kelsey，我在做这些事，不是在做那些事。这些事对 Google 很重要。所以我具体做了什么，我就直接讲清楚。但更重要的是，我带动了哪些人，我影响了哪些团队，以及这些事情最后带来了什么结果。所以我做了这个项目，结果是什么，都摆在这里。我当时就是用这种方式在写，等于是在无视那套流程。我其实不太在乎模板。而且那时候我自己也已经在一些晋升评审委员会里了，我们会一起看晋升材料、一起做决定。所以我决定把我的材料写给那些评审的人看。因为你想，如果他们要读很多这种材料，其实很累。你会觉得，天啊，怎么都这么干巴巴的。每个人都特别保守，每个人都只是在说我想听的话。整份材料看完，我甚至都不知道这个人到底是什么样。我就说，我不要那样写。我想让他们看到我，就像我本人坐在房间里为自己争取一样。我记得那份材料也收到过一些反馈，大概是说，Kelsey，这也太……拜托，兄弟，这个有点太那个了。但我不是在开玩笑，也不是不认真对待这件事。我只是想确保他们真正理解我在做什么，也让他们知道，我不是单纯在玩这套游戏，我是在用一种真实的方式来面对这个流程。最后我就是靠那份材料拿到了晋升。当然了，有时候也可能只是因为工作成果本身就足够说明问题。但很多时候，如果呈现方式不对，别人其实看不到你的工作价值。那次经历某种程度上也把我一下子推了上去。我之所以讲这个故事，是因为每个 distinguished engineer 走到那个位置的路径都不一样。大家总会想，哦，你写了多少代码？你做了多复杂的系统？但对我来说，我觉得更关键的是我对 Google Cloud 文化的影响。empathetic engineering 这件事后来变成了一个正式项目，他们会拿它去给其他工程师做入职培训。后来它发展成了背后有完整团队支持的东西。他们甚至会给它配 product manager，持续演进这个项目，并把它整合进 HR。后来也有其他工程师会跟我说，Kelsey，我们刚做完一场 empathy session，我想给你看看结果，因为我们马上就要上线了。然后工程师们开始真的去思考客户。我的意思是，Amazon 在这方面本来就一直有点那个意思，但要帮助 Google 也在这件事上达成一致，我觉得当然肯定也有其他人起了作用，不过从这些项目到最后产生的效果之间，确实有一条很直接的影响链路。还有就是我自己也在拓宽方向，从 Kubernetes 转向 serverless 领域，进入那种你会去帮助 Postgres 或者 Spanner 团队做 Postgres 接口的世界，也会去帮助 Go 团队，让他们在 cloud 这块做得更好。说到底，就是让你身边其他人也变得更成功，这种能力正是帮助你成为 distinguished 的关键之一。核心就是影响力，是你影响别人的能力。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Before you got to the distinguished level, you shared a story about Microsoft and an offer. Can you tell us about that?
text: 在你到 distinguished 级别之前，你讲过一个关于 Microsoft 和 offer 的故事。能跟我们说说吗？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, so I'm one of those people. I don't like ultimatums. It's hard. An ultimatum would be you're working at a company for a couple of years. You're doing a really good job. I'm just going to make up numbers here just to protect everybody. But let's just say you're making $100,000. And for a lot of times, you could be happy with $100,000. And so a company says, hey, we're hiring and you go there, you do an interview and they say, look, we're paying $120,000. At that moment, you're actually worth $120,000 because you now have evidence from the market. The $100,000 you currently make doesn't look so good anymore. You can't unsee a job offer for $120,000. And so now you have to make a decision. You can commit to that. Leave your job and go make $120,000 and start over. or you can do the ultimatum thing. If you don't pay me 120, then I'm leaving. And that puts everybody in a weird predicament because sometimes it doesn't have to be that adversarial. Sometimes it's just, this is evidence that, hey, look, I want to advocate for myself. I know we're not in promotion cycle, but I believe I'm worth 120 and I would like to have that conversation. And someone would say, hey, well, you need to go prove it. It's like, well, I have an offer if you want to see it, but I would rather be here. and that can turn into a you went to go look for another job it's like oh my god so
text: 可以。我就是那种人，我不喜欢最后通牒。那种感觉很难受。所谓最后通牒就是，你在一家公司干了几年，表现也很好。我这里随便编个数字，主要是为了不牵扯到任何人。比如说你现在年薪是 10 万美元。很多时候，你对这 10 万其实也挺满意的。然后有家公司说，嘿，我们在招人。你就去面试了，对方说，听着，我们给 12 万美元。到了那个瞬间，你其实就值 12 万了，因为你已经从市场上拿到了证据。那你现在拿的 10 万，看起来就没那么香了。你没法假装自己没看过那份 12 万的 offer。所以这时候你就得做决定。你可以接受它，离开现在的工作，去拿 12 万，从头开始。或者你也可以走最后通牒那条路：如果你不给我 12 万，我就走。这样就会把所有人都放到一个很尴尬的处境里，因为很多时候事情其实不一定非得这么对立。有时候只是，这就是一个证据，说明，嘿，你看，我想为自己争取一下。我知道现在不是晋升周期，但我相信我值 12 万，我希望能聊聊这件事。然后可能有人会说，哦，那你得先证明自己。你就会想，我手上其实有个 offer，你要是想看我可以给你看，但我其实更想留在这里。然后这件事又很容易变成，哦，所以你跑去找别的工作了？天啊，那就……

speaker: Gergely
text_en: I'm stuck here It can have weird dynamics right especially with your manager or
text: 我就卡在这儿了。这个过程确实会带来一些很奇怪的关系变化，对吧，尤其是和你的经理或者……

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: your management chain Exactly so I was always weary of that but I also knew that in business that's just how it goes and so I'm really at the peak now I'm thinking like early retirement I probably can get out of here at what 55, 60 if I continue on this pace and then Microsoft is like hey come through And look, I never had an executive recruitment process. So by this time, I'm probably considered an executive at Google. Once you hit like L7, you're kind of considered like an executive.
text: 和你的管理链条之间。没错。所以我一直都很警惕这种事。但我也知道，在商业世界里，事情很多时候就是这么运作的。所以那时候我其实已经到了一个高点了，我甚至开始想提前退休。我当时想，照这个节奏下去，我大概 55、60 岁就能退出了吧。然后 Microsoft 就来找我了，说，嘿，来聊聊吧。而且说实话，我以前从来没经历过高管级别的招聘流程。到那个时候，我在 Google 大概已经算高管了。基本上你到了 L7，大家就会把你视作高管。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: What is an executive?
text: 什么叫 executive？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So an executive just means that you probably have an admin to help you with some of the tasks you're doing. You're probably going to be asked to be an executive sponsor of things and programs. So if a team wants to have a program for something and There's gonna be a budget for that. You may have to help oversee that program, so they need executive sponsorship. Another engineering team knows that they're gonna need budget for something, and they need someone that has a little bit of political capital, a little bit of weight in the organization to help endorse them.
