That can be executive sponsorship, or you might be assigned to one of the largest customers the company has. But that executive set of duties means that you're gonna be making impact above and beyond yourself, typically to support other parts of the organization. So I grew into that at Google. Microsoft was like, we want you to start there. And here's the thing, I didn't think about my role that way there. I'm still the old Kelsey from CoreOS days, right? I'm just doing well here. And I wasn't going to interview at Microsoft. I'm like, for what? I'm at Google. I mean, I had a lot of freedom at Google. I was making impact at Google. I had a good reputation at Google. And so I'm like, no, my trajectory is fine. I'm not doing that. And to be honest, I don't like Windows. I didn't like Azure. I don't like .NET. I like GitHub. VS Code is nice. But my whole career, the majority of it, has been rooted in authenticity. I've been working on the things I actually like and care about. This would be one of the first times, maybe outside of some of the enterprise roles, that I'm going to go work on a set of technology that I wouldn't use on purpose. And so I went there and I met the Microsoft team and the weird thing though is I had a recruiter and they swapped the recruiter out and it's like hey our mistake I'm like what do you mean mistake this person is super nice and they asked for a resume and I didn't have a resume because it's been seven years Well it's been actually 15 years at that point since I ever had to show someone a resume so I'm like resume oh man I haven't made one of those in a long time I'm looking online for a template like what's the style of resumes these days and so I'm like trying to figure out a resume I'm like see this is why I don't waste time interviewing I don't got time for this anymore and they swap the recruiter out and say hey sorry about all that we don't need no resume sorry that's not this kind of process what we want to do is make sure that you meet the right people who represent Microsoft I was like, okay, that's different. So I get outside, I'm like, okay, what kind of quiz questions are we going to be prepared for? Because I'm not doing quiz, bro. You had committed access to Kubernetes. You wrote the book on Kubernetes. You're leading these things. You helped start Kubernetes. Come on, like you have a Wikipedia page. Like there's, we don't need to try to figure out whether you make the impact. We can just look at GitHub. There's evidence there. And so I'm meeting all of these leaders. they're bringing all these people who are behind the scenes I remember me and Scott Gunthry from the first time and I didn't really know who he was because I just didn't know how I didn't know the lineage and the history of Microsoft and you know of course I'm looking up who these people are and and I was like wow this is they're courting me oh this is what that feels like I mean Google did it a little bit too but not like this was like I'm looking I'm looking at the these are the executive directors this is like reverse interview
text: 所谓 executive，基本上就是你大概率会有一个行政助理，帮你处理一部分日常事务。你也很可能会被要求去担任一些项目和计划的 executive sponsor。比如某个团队想推动一个项目，而且这个项目会有预算，那你可能就得帮忙做一些统筹和监督，所以他们需要 executive sponsorship。又或者另一个工程团队知道自己要为某件事申请预算，他们就需要一个在组织里有点政治资本、有点分量的人来帮他们背书，这也算 executive sponsorship。再或者，公司最大的几个客户里，可能会分配一个给你负责。总之，这类 executive 职责意味着，你产生的影响会超出你个人本身，通常是去支持组织里的其他部分。我是在 Google 慢慢成长到这个阶段的。而 Microsoft 的意思是，我们希望你一进来就是这个级别。问题是，我当时并没有那样看待自己在那边的角色。我还是 CoreOS 时代那个老 Kelsey，对吧？我只是现在做得还不错而已。而且我本来根本没打算去 Microsoft 面试。我心想，图啥？我在 Google 啊。我在 Google 有很大的自由度，也能做出影响力，在 Google 名声也不错。所以我就觉得，不，我的发展轨迹挺好的，我不折腾这个。说实话，我也不喜欢 Windows。我以前不喜欢 Azure，也不喜欢 .NET。我喜欢 GitHub，VS Code 也挺好。但我整个职业生涯，大部分时候都建立在真实感上。我一直都在做那些我自己真的喜欢、真的在乎的东西。这可能会是我第一次——也许除了一些企业方向的岗位之外——去做一整套我自己平时根本不会主动用的技术。所以我还是去了，也见了 Microsoft 的团队。不过有个很奇怪的事：一开始有个 recruiter，后来他们把那个 recruiter 换掉了，还说，哎呀这是我们的失误。我心想，失误是什么意思？这人明明特别好啊。然后他们还跟我要简历，可我根本没有简历，因为已经七年——其实到那个时候是整整 15 年——我都没再给别人看过简历了。所以我就想，简历？天啊，我好久没做过这玩意了。我还上网找模板，想看看现在简历都流行什么风格。我就在那儿折腾简历，心想，你看，这就是我为什么不浪费时间去面试，我现在真没空搞这些。结果他们把 recruiter 换掉之后又说，抱歉，前面那些都不用了，我们不需要简历，不好意思，这不是那种流程。我们真正想做的是，确保你能见到那些真正代表 Microsoft 的合适的人。我当时想，哦，这就不一样了。于是我就开始想，好，那会不会有什么答题式面试要准备？因为那种我可不干，兄弟。你对 Kubernetes 有提交权限，你写过 Kubernetes 的书，你在带这些事情，你还参与创立了 Kubernetes。拜托，你连 Wikipedia 页面都有。我们根本不需要再去判断你有没有影响力，直接看 GitHub 就知道了，证据都在那里。所以我就开始见这些领导，他们把很多幕后的人也带来。我记得第一次见 Scott Guthrie 的时候，我其实并不知道他是谁，因为我不了解 Microsoft 的传承和历史。当然我后来也去查这些人是谁。然后我就想，哇，他们这是在追我啊。哦，原来这就是被招揽的感觉。Google 其实也有一点这种意思，但没到这个程度。这次真的是，我看着这些人，心想，这些可都是 executive directors，这简直像是反向面试。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: they're not interviewing you they're trying to sell themselves you know what and
text: 他们不是在面试你，而是在推销自己，你知道吧，而且——

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: that's the thing that I appreciated a lot because they were like This is your career. You've built a fantastic career over there. We can't ask you to throw that away without understanding what you would be doing here. And so it was like you can, if you want to understand the business, if there's someone you haven't gotten a chance to talk to, just give us a name. And I'm talking to them and the one thing that I really respected about Microsoft was I think by that time they had also acquired GitHub. And so they had a big vision for themselves, a lot of diversity, And I was like, okay, there's a lot of opportunity here. They're also all in on Kubernetes. They had just acquired people like Deas and Brendan Burns is there. So I was like, all right, Kelsey, you can come and make an impact.
text: 对，这正是我特别欣赏的一点，因为他们的态度是：这是你的职业生涯。你已经在那边建立了非常出色的职业发展，我们不能在不让你充分了解你来这里会做什么的情况下，就要求你把这些都丢掉。所以他们的意思是，如果你想了解业务，或者还有谁你没机会聊到，你只要给我们一个名字就行。我在和他们交流的时候，最让我尊重 Microsoft 的一点是，我觉得那时候他们也已经收购了 GitHub。所以他们对自己有很大的愿景，也有很强的多样性。我当时就想，嗯，这里机会很多。而且他们也在全力投入 Kubernetes。他们刚刚把 Deis 那样的团队收进来，Brendan Burns 也在那边。所以我就想，好吧，Kelsey，你可以过来，做出一些影响。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: There's room to grow.
text: 有成长空间。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: I'm like, all right, I might do it. All right, so I'm glad I did the interview. And I get home and it felt like that time I doubled my salary I told you on the way home. And I remember I get this email from Satya, the CEO of Microsoft. I'm like, man, he wrote this nice email, Kelsey, that heard you had a good experience with the team. Remember, I did the interview at the Microsoft headquarters, right? So, hey, heard really good things from the team. Just wanted to let you know, you know, you're going to be respected here. We're going to support you as a team. I'm like, damn, support as a team coming from the CEO? And the offer was like a PDF. It's an attachment. So I read this thing. So number one, what an honor. This is the CEO of Microsoft. He has so many more important things to be doing than to be emailing me about a role. And I open a PDF and very often in your career, there's a zero get added to the equation. And so you're looking at this like, I didn't even know that they do that. We know that it happens, but the person that graduated from high school in 1999 that chose the A-plus certification didn't know that was available. even while I was at Google having all the success and Google paid me pretty well too but I know you can add another zero still and so I'm like whoa this is this is crazy and I'm like wow so I um I showed my wife and she was the one that said you should just go interview like put your ego to the side and let's go see what's out there so shout out to my wife And so I get the PDF and I'm like, okay, this number is perfect. Honestly, I don't know what to say, but just find out. Like, is this really the only number? So I remember giving a counter. Like, you know what? I think it should be this. And the funny thing is Microsoft countered back higher. So we're not playing around. I'm like, oh, now I understand that I don't understand this part of the game. And so I have this offer and I knew that I wasn't going to go interview there if I wasn't serious about taking it. so I was serious about going to Microsoft in almost six years at that time at Google and I went to my manager I had the same manager which is legendary at Google I had the same manager for six years straight even with all the reorgs same manager the whole time he was a director so even when I got leveled up they allowed at least someone one level above still to report to a director and I had such a good relationship with them I told them what happened I said I wasn't looking and they asked me to come in so I went in and I'm going to take it. Number one, it will be financially irresponsible to not do this. So that will be the driving force. And also, I get to stretch myself in another way and see if I can make an impact again. He's like, okay. But there's no ultimatum. I'm leaving. And he's like, I'm just curious what Google would say.
text: 我当时想，好吧，我可能会去。总之，我很高兴自己去面了。然后我回到家，那感觉就像我之前跟你说过的那次——回家路上发现自己薪资翻倍的时候。我记得我收到了一封来自 Satya，也就是 Microsoft CEO 的邮件。我当时想，天啊，他给我写了这么一封很真诚的邮件。Kelsey，听说你和团队聊得很愉快。别忘了，我那次可是在 Microsoft 总部面试的，对吧？所以他说，团队对你的反馈非常好。我只是想让你知道，你在这里会被尊重，我们整个团队都会支持你。我当时想，哇，连 CEO 都来说“团队会支持你”？然后 offer 是一个 PDF 附件。我就把它打开来看。职业生涯里经常会有那么一个时刻，数字后面突然多了一个零。你看着那个数字会想，原来还能这样。我们都知道这种事会发生，但 1999 年高中毕业、当年去考 A+ 认证的那个我，根本不知道人生里还有这种可能。哪怕我当时已经在 Google，也很成功，Google 给我的薪酬也相当不错，但我都不知道原来后面还能再多加一个零。所以我当时就想，哇，这也太夸张了。我把那个 PDF 给我老婆看了。其实也是她当时跟我说，你就去面试吧，把自尊先放一边，我们去看看外面到底有什么机会。所以必须感谢我老婆。我拿到 PDF 之后就想，好，这个数字已经非常好了。说真的，我都不知道该说什么，但还是去确认一下吧：这真的是唯一的数字吗？我记得我还给了一个 counter，我说，你知道吗，我觉得应该是这个数。好笑的是，Microsoft 回过来的数字更高。意思就是，我们不是在闹着玩的。我当时想，哦，我明白了，原来是我根本不懂这套游戏怎么玩。于是我手上有了这个 offer。而且我很清楚，如果我不是认真考虑要去，我根本不会去那边面试。所以我是认真在考虑去 Microsoft 的。那时候我在 Google 差不多已经快六年了。我去找了我的经理。这个经理在 Google 也算传奇了——整整六年，我一直是同一个经理，哪怕经历了各种重组，还是同一个人，一直没变。他是 director，所以即便后来我的级别提升了，他们还是允许像我这种只高一层的人继续向 director 汇报。我和他的关系一直特别好。我就把事情经过告诉了他。我说，我本来没在找机会，是他们主动找我过去聊的，所以我去了。然后我说，我准备接受。第一，从财务角度来说，不接受这个机会都算不负责任。所以这会是最主要的驱动因素。第二，我也想换一种方式拉伸一下自己，看看我能不能再次做出影响。他说，好吧。不过这不是在下 ultimatum，我就是要走了。然后他说，我只是很好奇 Google 会怎么回应。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: I said, no, not great. I don't want to do ultimatum. Yeah, you don't believe in us.
text: 我说，不，不太好。我不想搞 ultimatum。对，就是那种“你们不相信我们吗”的感觉。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: I mean I do believe in it but I didn't want to do it you don't want to do it yes I
text: 我的意思是，我当然相信，只是我不想那么做。你就是不想那么做。对，我——

speaker: Gergely
text_en: didn't want to do it because you understand the dynamics the dynamics especially I
text: 不想这么做，因为你懂这里面的门道，尤其是我——

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: in many ways Google had been really really good to me in every facet so it wasn't ultimatum time it was more like I've earned it and he said that too I gave him the PDF and he looked at it he just started smiling and like oh wow whoa and then he said something that was really dope he says I want you to know you're worth every penny of this That was a game changer because he knows me like no other person I've ever worked with. No one knows me as well as Greg does. He knows my strengths. He knows my weaknesses. He knows my ambitions and my motivations. He knows all of them. For him to say that was important. And so I'm thinking... If Google wanted me to be at that level, they would have done it already. But also, pragmatically speaking, no one really knows your situation. All these people have thousands of people they manage. They're not targeting you or being mean to you, at least in my case. And so maybe they thought I was making that kind of money already. Maybe they thought that I was already a distinguished engineer. How would everyone in the org know? They got other things to worry about. So he presents the thing to them. and I think within hours they're like this is no problem like hey here's the you know don't worry about that number here's a bunch of stock and all these things and I'm looking like whoa whoa so now I have the money and I'm at the company I want to be and you didn't do an ultimatum I didn't do the ultimatum so I felt good about the relationship I didn't feel like there was going to be some retaliation I had no fear about that and I continued to be successful eventually got promoted to the distinguished engineer at Google told the Microsoft team no but the one thing that people saw from this I didn't talk about this at that time but I did do a tweet and the tweet was different company same team
text: 很多方面上，Google 真的对我非常非常好，方方面面都是，所以这不是那种“最后通牒”的时刻，更像是我已经挣到这个待遇了，他自己也这么说。我把那个 PDF 给他，他看了一眼，就开始笑了，像是“哦，哇，哇哦”。然后他说了一句特别酷的话，他说：我想让你知道，你值这里面的每一分钱。这句话彻底改变了局面，因为他比我合作过的任何人都更了解我。没有人比 Greg 更了解我。他知道我的长处，也知道我的短处。他知道我的野心，也知道我的动力来源。他全都知道。所以他说出这句话，非常重要。于是我就在想……如果 Google 真的想让我到那个级别，他们早就会做了。但另一方面，现实一点说，其实没有人真正了解你的具体情况。这些人手下都管着成千上万的人，他们不是专门针对你，也不是故意对你不好，至少我这边不是这样。所以也许他们以为我已经拿着那个级别的薪水了。也许他们以为我已经是 distinguished engineer 了。组织里怎么可能每个人都知道？他们还有别的事要操心。于是他把这件事拿去跟他们谈，我记得好像几个小时之内，他们就说这根本不是问题，像是“没事，这个数字你别担心，这里再给你一大笔股票，还有这些那些”。我当时看着都惊了，哇，等等等等。所以现在我既拿到了钱，也还留在我想待的公司，而且你也没有搞什么最后通牒，我也没有下最后通牒。所以我对这段关系感觉很好，我不觉得之后会有什么报复，我完全不担心这个。后来我也一直很顺，最后在 Google 晋升成了 distinguished engineer，然后我跟 Microsoft 团队说了不。但大家从这件事里看到的一点是——我当时没公开讲这些，不过我确实发了一条推文，那条推文写的是：different company same team。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: a lot of people was like what does that even mean different companies same team but
text: 很多人都在说，这到底什么意思，different companies same team 是什么意思？不过——

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: people were you know retweeting it and they liked it and people were just like oh it's because of the community stuff and how the different people in the community work together and that was part of it But a big part of it was that moment that Microsoft got me the biggest raise in my career at Google. And about three to six months later, I was in San Jose and Satya was there. And his admin's like, hey, Satya would like to just, you know, meet me with you. I'm like, the CEO of Microsoft got time to do anything? So I'm in San Jose and then I go to this hotel and the admin meets me downstairs like hey Satya's kind of ready to see you now and I'm going up and now he knows I'm coming right like let's just say the meeting's at one o'clock he knows I'm coming for like days and then you go into the hotel room and like the the doors are open overlooking the mountain range. And Satya's sitting there overlooking the mountain range like a Vanity Fair photo shoot. And I remember before on the plane ride there, I'm reading his book, Hit Refresh. And I remember the opening chapter, he talks about starting Microsoft as a developer advocate and now being CEO of Microsoft, advocating for the soul of Microsoft. because he had been there so long until the birth of the cloud and all these things. And the book was actually a pretty good read about his trajectory in the industry and his time at Microsoft. So now I'm done with the book and I'm about to meet him. So I have all of this context in my head. So when I walked through, I felt like I had this rapport with him for some reason because I read the book. And then he's looking out over the mountain range and I walked through the door, I was like, Satay, why are you sitting here like this?
text: 大家当时就在转发，也很喜欢这句话，很多人会觉得，哦，这是在说社区里的那些事，说不同公司的人在社区里一起协作，那确实也是一部分意思。但更大的一部分，是那个时刻：Microsoft 让我在 Google 拿到了我职业生涯里最大的一次加薪。大概三到六个月之后，我人在 San Jose，Satya 也在那儿。他的助理就说，嘿，Satya 想跟你见个面。我当时想，Microsoft 的 CEO 还有时间干这种事？所以我人在 San Jose，然后去了那家酒店，助理在楼下接我，说嘿，Satya 现在差不多可以见你了。然后我就上楼了。他知道我要来，对吧？比如说，假设会议是一点开始，那他肯定提前好几天就知道我要来。然后你走进那个酒店房间，门是开着的，外面能看到山景。Satya 就坐在那里，看着山脉，简直像在拍 Vanity Fair 的大片。我记得在飞过去的飞机上，我还在看他的书《Hit Refresh》。我记得开篇那一章里，他讲自己是怎么以 developer advocate 的身份加入 Microsoft，后来又成了 Microsoft 的 CEO，为 Microsoft 的灵魂发声。因为他在那儿待了很久，经历了云计算诞生以及后面这些事。那本书其实挺好看的，讲了他在这个行业里的成长轨迹，还有他在 Microsoft 的经历。所以这时候我书也看完了，马上又要见到他。于是我脑子里已经有了很多背景信息。所以当我走进去的时候，不知道为什么，我会觉得自己跟他已经有点熟悉感了，可能就是因为我读了那本书。然后他正望着外面的山景，我一进门就说，Satya，你为什么要这样坐着？

speaker: Gergely
text_en: You knew I was coming, so you're just posing like we're doing a photo shoot. And he just started laughing.
text: 你明知道我要来，所以你就是故意摆个 pose，像我们在拍写真一样。然后他就笑了。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: And so the tension, at least for me, was way down. And we had a discussion, I won't repeat everything here, but he said something dope. He said we were sitting around at the table And we asked ourselves, what executive did we want that got away? That means that you still were in the minds of at least some people. It's like you were on that list. And we had a discussion and he was very transparent. And he said, we gave you a good offer. We think we gave you a good offer. And at that time, Thomas Caring had just come from Oracle. My personal opinion, I like this leadership at Google. But I can understand why some people were afraid of the Oracle DNA being brought to Google. And I think maybe some people in the industry felt like, oh, this is a moment to go and maybe poach a few people that didn't want to make that transition. And he said something like, we gave you an offer as if you were running away from something and we should have gave you something to run towards. And I was like, damn, that's poetic. And so we wrapped up that meeting and I really felt like, man, I actually belong to the industry. It wasn't like I'm a Googler or a Kubernetes person. I really felt after that moment that I was an industry person. So I was very comfortable at that point retiring within the next year or so.
text: 所以至少对我来说，那个紧张感一下就降下来了。然后我们聊了一会儿，具体内容我就不全复述了，但他说了一句特别厉害的话。他说，我们当时坐在桌边讨论，问自己：哪个 executive 是我们错失掉、后来还一直惦记的人？这意味着，至少在一些人的脑海里，你一直都还在。就像你一直在那张名单上。我们聊得很坦诚，他也非常直接。他说，我们给了你一个不错的 offer，我们觉得那是个不错的 offer。那时候 Thomas Caring 刚从 Oracle 过来。就我个人看法，我挺喜欢 Google 当时的这套领导层。但我也能理解，为什么有些人会担心 Oracle 的 DNA 被带进 Google。我觉得行业里可能也有人会觉得，哦，这是个机会，可以趁这个时候挖走一些不想经历这种转变的人。他还说了一句大概是这样的话：我们给你 offer 的方式，好像你是在逃离什么，而我们本来应该给你一个值得奔赴的东西。我当时心想，哇，这话太有诗意了。那次会面结束之后，我真的有一种感觉：我其实是属于整个行业的。不只是说我是个 Googler，或者是个 Kubernetes 圈子里的人。那一刻之后，我真的觉得自己是个行业里的人。所以到那个时候，我已经非常坦然地接受，自己可能会在接下来一年左右退休。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: You talk with Satya Nadell and about a year later, you retired. How did that thinking come up? Because you were mentioning that You kind of had your retirement, you were thinking about your kind of end game or exit game.
text: 你跟 Satya Nadell 聊完，大概一年后你就退休了。这个想法是怎么形成的？因为你刚才提到，你其实一直在想自己的退休计划，或者说最后的收尾、退出方案。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So before joining Google, I kind of felt it was going to be possible, right? Because now I'm making money, I'm saving, I really practice a whole life of minimalism. You know, I live way below my means. So even when the money changed, the lifestyle did it. I was very conservative in terms of what I was spending. I didn't care about jewelry. I didn't care about buying cars to impress other people. That was gone. I felt free from it. that kind of thing in society. So the money started to become like freedom tokens. This money means I can get out of the game. And so everyone, at least the people I know or what I attempt to do, you set this number. and then when you blow past the number and if you're still a little young you're like well maybe I can change the number but the thing I was careful not to do was to change the lifestyle because I watched people around me change the lifestyle so you can make a lot of money and still be broke and I was like I want to avoid that because at that point I used to ask questions why am I doing this and for me personally I felt like I was lying to myself I love this job and it's like no you learn to love it and being someone who was good at it You tried to figure out working around the people you like helps you love it a little more. Working on the things that you're curious in helps you love it a little more. But you can't deny the pressures of just enterprise, stock price. You know, this person wants it now. There's a debate on whether we should do this or do that. There's personalities involved. There's parts of it you didn't love. And then the other one I thought about was time. Everyone thinks they're going to probably live to 90. or a hundred, or we don't even think about it at all. I think a lot of us just don't think about it. Right, and maybe it's not healthy to think about it, but at some point, I think around maybe 37-ish, I'm thinking like, what's the point of doing all of this work? Why are we doing this? And I used to ask that question in my job, why am I doing this? Why am I writing Python? Well, it's because you're a software developer. It's like, that's not the answer. That's the easy, obvious answer. So I learned to just zoom out. And when I started zooming out on my career, it's like, what are you doing this for? Because once I started having a better answer, when my daughter was born, I'm doing this for her. I'm doing this for my family. I want to make sure that we're all safe and protected. And so I started just changing my attitude. So for example, when my daughter was born, I remember just taking a job where I could work overnight. I'm just gonna go work in a knock I don't really care about this job as much in terms of career progression as long as I can be home with her so we don't have to do daycare I'll take my shift my wife takes her shift but this was the priority so then I started to structure my work life around this now I was never great at work life balance I won't lie My daughter goes to sleep. I'm back on that computer trying to learn new skills. I probably approached a lot of burnout in my career. I'm glad it's not just you. No, yeah, I ain't gonna lie. I did not have that part figured out. And also the thing about burnout, what I think is interesting is if you play professional soccer and you put in a lot of effort and you lose every year, you're gonna feel burnout. I'm doing a lot of work and we never win. But the teams who actually win play more games than everyone else because they have to play the playoffs, semifinals, they got to win World Cup. And if you win it multiple times in a row, you play way more games than everyone else. But for some reason, the champions aren't tired. Yeah, they're not burnt out. Because, I mean, they probably are, but they ignore the feeling because they know what's on the other side. And so my career had a lot of winnings. So as I'm pushing the limits, you're getting the win. You're pushing the limits. So some of the burnout that is psychological, you just dial it back because like, The Pragmatic Engineer
text: 所以在加入 Google 之前，我其实就觉得这件事是有可能实现的，对吧？因为那时候我已经开始赚钱、开始存钱了，而且我这一辈子基本都在践行极简主义。你知道的，我的生活水平一直远低于自己的收入。所以即便收入变了，生活方式并没有变。我在花钱这件事上一直非常保守。我不在乎珠宝，也不在乎买车去让别人觉得我很厉害。那些东西我已经放下了。我觉得自己已经从社会里那套东西中解脱出来了。所以钱开始变成一种“自由代币”。这些钱意味着，我可以退出这场游戏。所以大家——至少我认识的人，或者说我自己会做的——都会先设一个数字。然后当你远远超过那个数字之后，如果你还比较年轻，你就会想，也许我可以把这个数字再改高一点。但我特别小心的一点，就是不要改变生活方式，因为我看到身边很多人一旦生活方式升级了，你赚再多钱也还是会穷。我当时就想，我一定要避免这个。因为到了那个阶段，我会开始问自己：我到底为什么要做这些？对我个人来说，我觉得自己以前是在骗自己，说我热爱这份工作。其实不是，你是学会了去喜欢它。而且当你本身又很擅长这件事时，你会慢慢摸索出一些办法：跟你喜欢的人一起工作，会让你更喜欢一点；做你感兴趣的事情，也会让你更喜欢一点。但你没法否认企业环境本身带来的压力：股价、业务压力、这个人要马上交付、大家在争论该做这个还是做那个、里面还有各种人格和政治因素。这里面总有一些部分是你不喜欢的。另一个我开始思考的是时间。每个人都觉得自己大概会活到 90 岁，或者 100 岁，或者我们干脆根本不去想这件事。我觉得很多人就是不去想。也许老想这个也不健康，但到了某个阶段，我大概 37 岁左右的时候，就开始想：我们做这么多工作到底是为了什么？我们为什么要做这些？我以前在工作里也会问自己这个问题：我为什么在做这个？我为什么在写 Python？然后答案是，因为你是软件开发者。可我会觉得，这不是答案，那只是最简单、最表面的回答。所以我学会了把视角拉远一点。当我开始从更高层看自己的职业生涯时，就会问：你做这些到底是为了什么？后来当我有了一个更好的答案——我女儿出生之后——我是在为她做这些。我是在为我的家人做这些。我想确保我们都安全、都被照顾好。于是我开始调整自己的心态。比如我女儿出生的时候，我记得我特意找了一份可以上夜班的工作。我就想，我去上夜班就行，我没那么在乎这份工作在职业发展上的意义，只要我能在家陪她，这样我们就不用送 daycare。我上我的班，我妻子上她的班，但这才是优先级。于是我开始围绕这个来安排自己的工作和生活。当然我得承认，我从来都不擅长 work-life balance。我不撒谎。我女儿睡着之后，我又会回到电脑前，继续学新技能。我职业生涯里应该很多次都逼近 burnout。很高兴不只是你这样。对，真的，我不骗你，这一块我完全没搞明白。还有一点我觉得关于 burnout 很有意思：如果你踢职业足球，拼了命训练和比赛，但你每年都输，你就会 burnout。因为你会觉得，我做了这么多，结果我们从来没赢过。但真正赢球的队伍，比赛场次其实比所有人都多，因为他们还要打季后赛、半决赛，还要去赢世界杯。如果你连续赢很多次，那你踢的比赛会比别人多得多。但不知道为什么，冠军反而看起来没那么累。对，他们没 burnout。我的意思是，他们可能也累，但他们会忽略那种感觉，因为他们知道终点那边有什么。而我的职业生涯里有很多“赢”的时刻。所以当你不断逼近极限时，你又在赢；再逼近极限，你还在赢。所以有些属于心理层面的 burnout，你就会自己把它调低一点，因为就像《The Pragmatic Engineer》那样。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: How much money do you need to live off a fraction of this money? And I started just making the calculations. And then you start negotiating salary around these particular things. And you start asking yourself, am I making an impact that is worthy and deserving of these things? So then, of course, you get into things like investments, startup stuff, blah, blah, blah. But then I'm starting to say, oh, I'm getting close. I can see it now. Which looked impossible early on. It looked impossible. So like halfway in my career, it looked impossible. Maybe my 401k would do something and how much does Social Security pay and if I do everything just right. And then it was like, oh, I don't need Social Security anymore. I don't care what the stock market is doing. But I have to stay the course and be disciplined. And I have to structure my career in a way. So once I got that light at the end of the tunnel, I started making decisions based on that. I'm going to bet to go to CoreOS instead of something that pays way more money. NASA looked great. that man this changes the trajectory over here at Google plus I'm going to have to step way up to be able to walk on that particular stage at least that's what I told myself but also Google is the type of place that could pay for performance at that level so I was like wow this is a good opportunity and so now that I spent all these years thinking about why do you work I never had a good answer but I never accepted the lie and so I was like I'm working to be me and you become a distinguished engineer, but you realize you're a junior person. You didn't put as much work on learning how to live and the relationships and the things that you do when the computer gets turned off. You didn't put any effort into that.
text: 你到底需要多少钱，才能靠其中一小部分钱生活？于是我就开始认真算这笔账。然后你就会围绕这些具体目标去谈薪资。你也会开始问自己，我创造的影响，值不值得、配不配得上这些回报？接着当然就会涉及投资、创业公司之类的，等等等等。但后来我开始觉得，哦，我快接近了，我现在已经能看见了。可这在早些年看起来根本不可能，真的不可能。比如在我职业生涯走到一半的时候，这事看起来还是不可能。那时候我还在想，也许我的 401k 能起点作用，Social Security 能发多少钱，如果我每一步都做得特别对的话。后来就变成了，哦，我已经不需要 Social Security 了，我也不在乎股市在干什么。但我得坚持路线，得自律。我还得以某种方式来规划自己的职业生涯。所以当我看到隧道尽头那束光之后，我开始按这个目标来做决定。比如我会赌一把，去 CoreOS，而不是去一个薪水高得多的地方。NASA 看起来很棒。Google 那边也让我觉得，哇，这会彻底改变我这条职业轨迹，而且如果我要站上那个舞台，我就必须大幅提升自己——至少我是这么告诉自己的。不过 Google 也是那种在那个级别上真的会按绩效给回报的地方，所以我当时想，哇，这是个很好的机会。于是，当我花了这么多年去想“你为什么工作”这个问题时，我一直没有一个特别好的答案，但我也从来没接受过那个谎言。所以我最后想明白了：我工作，是为了成为我自己。你会成为 distinguished engineer，但你也会意识到，自己在“做人”这件事上其实还是个初级选手。你没有花那么多精力去学习怎么生活、怎么经营关系、以及当电脑关掉之后你要做些什么。你根本没在这些事情上投入多少。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Not a lot, not as much effort as you put into the work. What parts are you talking about? Is this the kind of the friendships? Is this the outside of work? Is this a community? What
text: 投入得不多，至少远没有你投入在工作上的那么多。你具体指的是哪些部分？是友情这类吗？是工作之外的生活？还是某种社区？什么方面？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Just developing myself. So most of my friends I've known for 20 years. We talk on the phone all the time. I see them. I fly. I see them. That stuff is important. I've been married for 20 years. I'm going to celebrate my 20th year anniversary in a couple of months. And I feel like the core parts of my life that I wanted to be healthy and stable, I think I did those things. But there was things where, you know, I remember going to Budapest for the first time and stayed an extra day. So it wasn't leave as soon as the conference is over. it will stay one more day and I remember hanging out with people like Liz Rice and her team and they were like we're going to a bath I'm like what's a bath it's like oh it's like a big swimming pool and we went to one of these huge parks where there's like you walk in circle and you go in the building and there's all these plunges and I was like this is not the kind of thing I do the truth was it was the kind of thing I never did didn't even know so I said sure I'll go So I went there, we rented some shorts because I didn't bring any swim things. It wasn't on the agenda. We were there for like three plus hours. And I was like, man, what an experience. And so when I got back home, I didn't talk about the conference. I didn't talk about the keynote. I talked about the escape room we went to and the three hours we spent at the bathhouse.
text: 就是发展我自己这个人。我的大多数朋友，我都认识 20 年了。我们经常打电话，我也会去见他们，坐飞机去见他们。这些都很重要。我结婚也 20 年了，再过几个月就是我们 20 周年纪念日。我觉得，在我人生里那些我希望它健康、稳定的核心部分上，我做得还不错。但也有一些事情是这样的——我记得我第一次去 Budapest，特意多待了一天。不是那种“会议一结束立刻走人”，而是多留一天。我记得当时和 Liz Rice 还有她的团队一起玩，他们说我们要去泡澡堂，我说澡堂是什么？他们说，哦，有点像个大泳池。然后我们去了一个特别大的公园，里面像是绕着圈走，走进建筑之后有各种冷水池之类的。我当时想，这不是我会做的那种事。可事实是，这其实不是“不是我会做的事”，而是“我从来没做过，甚至都不知道有这回事”。所以我说，行，我去。后来我们去了，还租了短裤，因为我根本没带泳衣之类的东西，这本来就不在行程里。我们在那里待了三个多小时。我当时就想，天啊，这体验太棒了。所以我回家之后，聊的不是会议，不是主题演讲，我聊的是我们去玩的密室逃脱，还有在澡堂里待的那三个小时。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: The escape rooms are all so good, yeah.
text: 那些密室逃脱真的都特别棒，是啊。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: And so I was like, what the hell was I doing? I was moving too fast through this thing. And so then I started dialing back a little bit. So hey, I gotta dial back a little bit. And then little things. Like, as a minimalist, I always tried to live intentional. So it wasn't like the first time. I just realized there were other areas where I wasn't being as intentional. I remember when I was listening to music, I was like, hey, my wife can sing and she knows the lyrics so sometimes I'm singing a song she's like that's not how that song goes and I'm like it was what I remember and now when I listen to music I actually pull up the lyrics and I listen while reading the lyrics so I can really understand what the song is about and it's little nuanced things like that where I was like hey life doesn't need to be so fast and this is why you see me sometimes online like the fact that people are over indexing on productivity doesn't necessarily sit well with me because it's like If you just do productivity, you're gonna miss everything. You're gonna miss the experience. You're gonna miss this part that are hard. You're gonna miss the collaboration with your team. If you just go through too fast, you're gonna move right past it because you're a human. You're not a computer. You're an actual human and you don't work only for productivity. Maybe that's what your job believes you are. And there's that saying, you're not your HR title. And so for me, part of that was like dialing back to like, oh, you're a human. Act like one. So I started to invest in like my relationships, talking to people, being patient, go to the school board meeting, do some of the offsite things, spend more time with your child and her games, teach her how to drive instead of going only to driving school. Cleaning became a big part of my everyday routine. And look, if you have enough money, you can hire someone to clean your house for you. No problem. And there's actually nothing wrong with that. But boy, I enjoyed the parts where it reminded me of all the success you've made. There are some people who don't have a stove. There are some people who don't have a refrigerator. And so when you would clean them thoroughly, like take everything out, look at all the expired food, sort everything back out, clean it back to the condition when you bought it, put it all back together. And what I noticed was for the people who had In my opinion, success, they could afford to do that. It is a luxury to be able to afford to go slow. Because when you're really, really busy, you need an admin because you can't afford to book your own tickets. When you're really busy, you cannot afford to clean your own house. And some people would say, I make way more money having someone else clean my house and I can go make money doing everything. It's like, I promise you money isn't everything. It is not. And so for me, I said, wow, now I have time. Because some people only have money. Now I had both. And I decided that I could actually slow down And the thing that maybe some people noticed Even my outward projection changed I was way more methodical And so the work changed The keynotes changed Because I started to incorporate the philosophy I brought the people into the keynote And I started asking questions like I know what I want to show them But then how do I want them to feel? Because sometimes I wanted people to feel excited Sometimes I wanted people to feel a little bit embarrassed by the state of our industry and the complexity that we added for no reason. And I noticed I was just starting to be, like the full Kelsey was starting to be on display. So then I was like, all right, it's time to retire. What am I going to walk out to? And luckily, I was practicing just enough of like who Kelsey is to start doubling down on that as a retired. And I didn't necessarily do a good job. I'm only three years into this. So I'm a junior retired person. And I make time for lots of things. But I still want to hold on to all of this knowledge, all these parts of our craft. So I'm still doing advisory. I'm doing investing. I'm still doing public speaking. I don't speak as much about low-level technology things. I do try to put a little bit of philosophy in there, but I know that I'm still holding on to that part of my ego.
text: 所以我当时就在想，我之前到底在干嘛？我一路都走得太快了。于是我开始稍微放慢一点。好吧，我得稍微慢下来一点。然后还有一些小事。比如，作为一个极简主义者，我一直都尽量有意识地生活，所以这不算是我第一次这么做。我只是意识到，在其他一些领域里，我其实没那么有意识。我记得有一次我在听音乐，我就想，哎，我老婆会唱歌，而且她记得歌词，所以有时候我唱一首歌，她会说，这歌词不是这样的。我就说，这是我记得的版本。现在我听音乐时，真的会把歌词找出来，一边看一边听，这样我才能真正理解这首歌到底在讲什么。就是这种很细微的小事，让我开始意识到，生活没必要过得这么快。这也是为什么你有时候会看到我在网上说，人们对生产力过度用力这件事，我其实不太认同。因为如果你眼里只有生产力，你会错过一切。你会错过体验，会错过那些困难的部分，会错过和团队协作的过程。如果你走得太快，你就会直接从这些东西旁边冲过去，因为你是人，不是电脑。你是一个活生生的人，而你工作也不只是为了生产力。也许你的工作把你当成那样的人。还有一句话说得很好：你不是你的 HR 职级头衔。对我来说，其中一部分就是把节奏放慢，提醒自己：哦，你是个人，那就活得像个人。所以我开始投入到关系里，去和人交流，学会耐心一点，去参加学区委员会会议，去做一些线下活动，多花时间陪孩子、看她比赛，教她开车，而不是只把她送去驾校。打扫卫生也成了我日常生活里很重要的一部分。你看，如果你钱够多，你当然可以雇人来帮你打扫房子，完全没问题，这本身也没什么不对。但我真的很享受打扫这件事，因为它会提醒我，你已经取得了这么多成功。这个世界上有些人连炉子都没有，有些人连冰箱都没有。所以当你认真去清洁这些东西时——把里面的东西全拿出来，看看哪些食物过期了，重新分类整理，把它擦洗到像刚买回来时那样，再把一切放回去——我注意到，那些在我看来算是“成功”的人，其实是有能力去做这些事的。能够慢下来，本身就是一种奢侈。因为当你真的、真的很忙的时候，你需要一个行政助理，因为你连自己订票的时间都没有。当你真的很忙的时候，你也没法自己打扫房子。有些人会说，我让别人帮我打扫，我自己去赚钱，赚得更多。这么做更划算。可我向你保证，钱不是一切，真的不是。所以对我来说，我当时想，哇，现在我有时间了。因为有些人只有钱，而我那时两样都有了。于是我决定，我真的可以慢下来。而且也许有些人注意到了，连我对外呈现出来的样子都变了。我做事变得更有章法了。于是我的工作变了，我的主题演讲也变了，因为我开始把自己的哲学带进去。我把“人”带进了 keynote 里。我开始问自己这样的问题：我知道我想给他们展示什么，但我希望他们有什么感受？因为有时候我想让大家感到兴奋；有时候我又想让大家对我们这个行业的现状、以及那些毫无必要却被我们加进去的复杂性感到一点羞愧。我注意到，我开始越来越像完整的 Kelsey，开始真正把自己展现出来。然后我就想，好吧，是时候退休了。那我退休之后要走向什么？幸运的是，在退休前，我已经练习得差不多了，开始知道 Kelsey 到底是谁，也开始在退休后继续加码去做这件事。不过我也不敢说自己做得有多好。我退休才三年，所以我是个“初级退休人士”。我会给很多事情留时间，但我还是想抓住这些知识、抓住我们这门手艺里的这些部分。所以我现在还在做 advisory，也在做投资，也还在公开演讲。只是我现在不太会讲那么多底层技术的东西了。我会尽量往里面放一点哲学，不过我也知道，我还是没有完全放下那部分自我认同。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Can you tell me a little bit about the advisory and the investing, both how you got started, what advising means? I think a lot of us software engineers are curious about this and some of us will have opportunity and also the investing part, the good, the bad and the ugly. You can now speak freely.
text: 你能不能多讲一点 advisory 和投资？包括你是怎么开始做的，advising 具体是什么意思？我觉得很多软件工程师都对这个很好奇，其中一些人未来也会有这样的机会。还有投资这部分，好的、坏的、坑人的，你现在都可以畅所欲言。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah so look I think if you're a software engineer as you progress in your career you will be an advisor because if someone wants to build something a junior engineer does exactly what they ask if I get this ticket I want to do a good job I want to get it done on time exactly as you ask bugs and all and then as you get more experience you know that the person's asking may not know what they're asking for so you're going to be an advisor hey if we did that is going to add a lot of complexity and you're not going to get what you want. What I think you want is this, let me show you. And as an advisor, You're not necessarily the person's boss. You're just trying to give them something that they don't have. So you're advising. Sometimes you become an engineer and you get really close to the executive team where they check with you before they make any big decisions. So you're an advisor. When you start to advise at a very high level, then you also share the outcomes, right? A lot of software engineers that work at larger tech companies, they have equity. So if you just don't focus on heads down, do your job, and you get into more of those advisory roles, then you start to realize that maybe you start to have a little bit of effect on the stock price, right? So my actions, if they turn out well, then we get there. So, okay, so how does advisory work in the startup world? When I first started doing it, it feels like an exact waste of time. Number one, VCs have large amounts of money so they can invest in a thousand companies and ideally one of them will return the entire fund. But when you and I do like individual investing or advisory, typically when I started advising, you would take advisory shares. 99.99% of the time, they're worth absolutely nothing. Number one, you're going to get diluted to hell and back. You don't even know how the taxes work on this thing. You may exercise them in the wrong way and you may end up paying money and getting nothing in the end. So then you start having this allergic reaction to like advisory shares, especially if you get the ones that are way low on the totem pole. So then I'm like you know what I'm never going to work for free I used to say this to myself you can't be working for free can't be working for free and the people who respect you they'll never let you work for free and so when someone say hey Kelsey we want to advise I say stop this when I first started it was like I need some equity so advisory shares but I understood something different now I said look I might need a quarter point, half a point, or a whole point, depending on where you are with your funding rounds, how much risk am I taking, giving you this time, and how much impact will I make? Let's say I ask for a quarter point of a company.
text: 可以。你看，我觉得如果你是软件工程师，随着职业发展，你最终都会成为 advisor。因为如果有人想做个东西，初级工程师通常就是别人让做什么就做什么：我拿到这个 ticket，我要把它做好，要按时完成，完全照你的要求来，哪怕里面有 bug 也照做。可随着你经验越来越多，你会知道，提需求的人未必真的知道自己在要什么。所以你就会变成 advisor：嘿，如果我们这么做，会增加很多复杂性，而且最后你也得不到你真正想要的结果。我觉得你真正想要的是这个，我来给你演示一下。作为 advisor，你不一定是对方的老板。你只是想给对方一些他们原本没有的东西，所以你是在提供建议。有时候你做工程师，会和高管团队走得很近，他们在做重大决策之前会先来问你，那你就是 advisor。当你开始在很高的层面上提供建议时，你也会开始分享结果，对吧？很多在大科技公司工作的软件工程师都有 equity。所以如果你不只是埋头干活、只把自己的本职工作做好，而是进入更多这种 advisory 角色，你就会开始意识到，也许你对股价都会有一点影响，对吧？如果我的行动最后效果很好，那我们就能走到那个结果。好，那 startup 世界里的 advisory 是怎么运作的？我刚开始做的时候，感觉这事完全是在浪费时间。第一，VC 手里有大量资金，所以他们可以投一千家公司，理想情况下只要其中一家能把整个基金都赚回来就行。但像你我这种个人投资或者做 advisory，通常我刚开始做 advisory 的时候，拿到的是 advisory shares。99.99% 的情况下，这些东西最后一文不值。第一，你会被稀释得一塌糊涂；第二，你甚至都不知道这玩意儿税务上怎么算；你可能还会用错误的方式去行权，最后变成你自己先掏了钱，结果到头来什么都没得到。于是你就会对 advisory shares 产生一种过敏反应，尤其是那种在股权结构里排得特别靠后的。所以后来我就想，你知道吗，我绝对不要再免费干活了。我以前总这么跟自己说：你不能免费干活，不能免费干活。而真正尊重你的人，也不会让你免费干活。所以当有人说，嘿 Kelsey，我们想请你做 advisory，我就会说，先停一下。刚开始的时候，我会说我需要一些 equity，所以给我 advisory shares。但后来我理解得不一样了。我会说，听着，我可能需要四分之一个点、半个点，或者一个点，具体取决于你现在融资轮次到哪了、我投入时间承担了多大风险、以及我能带来多大影响。假设我开口要公司四分之一个点。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Cool quarter point being 0.25%.
text: 明白，四分之一个点就是 0.25%。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yep, of equity. And I would say, look, you know, I used to think you can be advising for four years. That makes no sense, actually, in my opinion, from experience. Advisory, I think, is really good for like a year at a time. because advisory should have impact. It shouldn't just be like this, hey, let's talk about what we're doing and you just give superficial advice. Like, no, it should really make an impact. So if you're going to make an impact, then you've earned the equity. And so what I started doing, instead of a four-year investing, I would say, look, I need one year, no cliff, 10-year exercise window. Never losing again Not on early access Yeah, so that worked really well So you look at Carta and it's all stacking up But I realized that I wasn't necessarily avoiding the working for free problem Because if they don't have a good exit, you still get nothing So I started adding the retainer component And the retainer component, I used to think about them as dividends So you may give a dollar amount, it could be $1,500, it could be $3,000, it could be $5,000 And you get that every month for that one year and so in your mind it's like alright I get $60,000 plus equity and now it's like alright you should probably call me because it's just a very expensive person sitting there I remember the first time I had a decent exit I advised a company, Pixie Labs. They were doing observability. It was a small twist. On Kubernetes, they were doing this observability. And what they were doing is leveraging EBPF so that way you didn't really have to add any agents or instruments. So the way EBPF works is you almost get to the kernel level. So if two applications are talking to each other, At the lower level, I can actually see that networking traffic. I can see what port is buying to. And I can also see that it's a Go program. I can even walk the tree like a debugger would. And so they were doing observability this way. And they had, you know, almost like, hey, we're going to compete with Datadog or something like that. And as an advisor, I looked at it and said, look, you could try that. But most people are really not interested in changing observability stacks just for a slightly different way of doing things, even if it is eBPF. Now, what do you think it should be? So they're in stealth mode. Their investors are like, yo, it's time to come out of stealth. We got to start, you know, getting some revenue from this thing. I said, hey, we need a few more months. And we need to take another approach. So I'm sitting with the team and I'm like, you know what you have? You have like an agentless thing. And they also have this thing called Pixie Scripts that allows you to kind of make your own dashboards or get your own metrics. And I started like, oh, if you're a system administrator, you can wrap them like command line tools and you can like create a thousand clusters with these Pixie Scripts. and so this idea that we should pivot just a little bit change the messaging just a little bit before we come out of stealth and they gave a couple of presentations with this agentless observability and then we did a keynote or we did like a pixie day before coming out of stealth and we show system administrators this new vision it went well I did this opening keynote I showed the vision I interviewed the founders and like why did this need to exist why not just date a dog the next day They had offers. I think one of them was from maybe VMware, but the other one was from New Relic. And they got acquired by New Relic. And I was like, wow, what does this mean? And I remember the lawyer came and said, hey, we need to accelerate all your shares. And this is the money we owe. I was like, whoa, this can work. But also, I felt like I actually made an impact. We did pivot. And then the way it works in advisory with VCs, they like returns and word gets around. Kelsey's advisory can have impact. So other founders are coming, VCs are recommending some companies that need help. So over time, you know, some founders reach out, like Guillermo from Vercel reached out, like, hey, Kelsey, this is when they were making their pivot from just being purely serverless and front end to thinking about going a little deeper in the stack. So I spent a couple years with the Vercel team. Docker can go on and on. And that helps you build out a portfolio. and so I was like you know what stocks are cool remember I started to divest from that once I had my retirement plan in place I was like but I do like the concept of the entrepreneurial mindset and so helping these companies get to the next level even for my little short amount of time the little small impact and then being able to share in the outcomes became a major part of my advisory work so my advice to anyone that wants to do advisory work it really helps to be a domain expert deep So if a team is about to build out their engineering team and you've been an engineering manager or a team lead, don't just go say, oh, when we were at Google, we did it this way. That's not what a startup needs. What a startup needs is you just say, listen, as you build out your team, you have to think about growth trajectory. You're going to have to think about vesting schedules. You have to think about personality types, impactful work. junior versus senior spread when to bring in engineering leadership when not to that's the type of advisory that can help accelerate a startup from one stage to another stage so then that might be your type of advisory role so when you meet a founder and say hey listen here's my domain expertise here's an impact I can have and if you think that's going to be good for you at this stage and don't get offended if your advisory is no longer necessary because maybe they're going to move on to something much different than what you're good at Let them go get a new set of advisors because you've done your part.
text: 对，股权方面。我会说，你知道，我以前觉得顾问关系可以做四年。以我的经验来看，这其实完全说不通。我觉得 advisory 这种事，比较合适的是一次做一年。因为顾问工作应该是要有实际影响的，不该只是那种“来聊聊我们在做什么”，然后你给点很表面的建议。不，应该是真的带来改变。所以如果你真的带来了影响，那你就配得上那份股权。后来我开始改做法了，不再是四年 vesting，我会说，给我一年，没有 cliff，10 年 exercise window。再也不会因为提前行权之类的事吃亏了。对，这招效果很好。这样你去看 Carta，所有东西都会一层层累起来。但我后来意识到，我其实并没有真正避开“免费干活”这个问题。因为如果他们最后没有好的 exit，你还是一分钱都拿不到。所以我开始加 retainer 这一部分。以前我会把 retainer 想成分红。金额可以是 1500 美元，也可以是 3000 美元，或者 5000 美元。你在那一年的每个月都能拿到这笔钱。这样你心里就会觉得，好，我拿的是 6 万美元加股权。这样一来，他们大概率也会更愿意找你，因为不然就等于有个很贵的人坐在那里。我记得我第一次有一笔还不错的 exit，是我给一家叫 Pixie Labs 的公司做顾问。他们做的是 observability，可算是个小众方向吧。在 Kubernetes 上做 observability。他们的做法是利用 eBPF，这样你基本不需要再加 agent 或做额外埋点。eBPF 的工作方式是，你几乎能下探到内核层。所以如果两个应用在互相通信，在更底层我其实能看到那部分网络流量。我能看到它绑定了哪个端口。我还能看出来那是个 Go 程序。我甚至可以像 debugger 一样去遍历调用树。所以他们就是用这种方式做 observability。当时他们的想法有点像“我们要跟 Datadog 竞争”之类的。作为顾问，我看完就说，你们当然可以试试，但大多数人其实不会因为一种稍微不一样的做法就去更换整套 observability stack，哪怕它是 eBPF。那你觉得应该怎么做？当时他们还在 stealth mode，投资人已经在催了，说，差不多该出 stealth 了，得开始从这东西上赚点收入了。我说，给我们再多几个月，而且我们得换个思路。我就跟团队一起坐下来，我说，你们知道你们真正有什么吗？你们其实有个 agentless 的东西。而且他们还有个叫 Pixie Scripts 的功能，可以让你自己做 dashboard，或者拿到你自己的 metrics。然后我就开始想，如果你是系统管理员，你可以把这些东西包成命令行工具，然后用这些 Pixie Scripts 去创建一千个集群。所以我们的想法就是，在出 stealth 之前稍微 pivot 一下，稍微改一下 messaging。后来他们做了几场关于这种 agentless observability 的演讲，然后我们还办了一个 keynote，或者说办了个 Pixie Day，再正式出 stealth。我们把这个新愿景展示给系统管理员看。效果很好。我做了开场 keynote，讲了这个愿景，也采访了创始人，聊为什么这个东西必须存在，为什么不能直接用 Datadog。结果第二天，他们就收到了收购报价。我记得其中一个可能是 VMware，另一个是 New Relic。最后他们被 New Relic 收购了。我当时心想，哇，这意味着什么？我还记得律师过来说，嘿，我们得把你所有股份加速归属，这是我们该付你的钱。我当时想，哇，这事真能成。但同时我也觉得，我确实带来了实际影响。我们真的做了 pivot。然后 advisory 这件事在 VC 圈子里就是这样运作的，他们喜欢回报，消息也会传开：Kelsey 的顾问工作是能带来影响的。于是其他创始人就会找上门来，VC 也会推荐一些需要帮助的公司。慢慢地，就会有创始人主动联系我。比如 Vercel 的 Guillermo 就联系过我，说，嘿，Kelsey。那时候他们正从纯 serverless 和前端的方向，开始考虑往更深的技术栈走一点。所以我跟 Vercel 团队一起做了几年。Docker 也是，类似的例子还能讲很多。这些经历会帮你慢慢建立起一个 portfolio。所以我当时就想，股票当然也不错。你记得我说过，等我的退休计划安排好之后，我就开始逐步减持了。但我确实很喜欢这种创业者思维的概念。去帮助这些公司更上一层楼，哪怕我投入的时间不长，影响也不算特别大，但最后还能分享成果，这就成了我顾问工作里非常重要的一部分。所以如果有人想做 advisory，我的建议是：你最好在某个领域里真的很深，是个 domain expert。比如一个团队正要搭建他们的工程团队，而你以前做过 engineering manager 或 team lead，那你不要只是跑去说，“哦，我们当年在 Google 是这么干的。”创业公司不需要这个。创业公司需要的是你告诉他们，听着，当你开始搭团队时，你得考虑增长轨迹。你得考虑 vesting schedule。你得考虑不同性格类型、什么样的工作更有影响力、初级和高级工程师的比例、什么时候该引入 engineering leadership、什么时候不该。这种 advisory 才能真正帮助一家创业公司从一个阶段加速到下一个阶段。那这可能才是适合你的顾问角色。所以当你见到创始人时，你要说，嘿，这是我的领域专长，这是我能带来的影响。如果你觉得这些在你们当前阶段有帮助，那我们可以合作。还有一点，如果有一天你的顾问价值不再必要了，也别觉得被冒犯。因为他们可能要进入一个和你擅长的东西完全不同的新阶段。那就让他们去找一批新的顾问吧，因为你已经完成了你该做的部分。

speaker: Kelsey

text: 我这个人一直都特别以人为本，从头到尾都是。我对自己的状态也很敏感。还是那句话，我打扫卫生的时候也会反思。所以对我来说，这整场游戏从来都不是关于 0 和 1。虽然大家都想把它说成那样。我们太喜欢用这些标准去评判社会了。你要是个 billionaire，别人天然就会尊重你；你要是没钱，别人可能看都不看你一眼。很不幸，现实就是这样，但我确实会去想这些。奇怪的是，当你说你在乎人，人们反而会觉得很怪。如果你在 Twitter 上很刻薄、很自恋，那反而正常，大家觉得理所当然。如果你想占别人便宜，想钻系统空子，那也正常，几乎是默认的。但如果你说，我只是想善待别人，这反而显得很奇怪。大家会想，这什么鬼？不不不，我们不都是在玩这套游戏吗？我对技术的哲学其实就是这种 people-first 的立场。所以 crypto 出来的时候，大家都在说，哦，我们有这些 token，诸如此类。Kelsey 你懂 open source，也懂 blockchain。我说，这些东西我都不怀疑。我参与过 open source 运动，也参与过一些可能会威胁别人工作的事情，我懂。但 crypto 这件事，我没法不去从金融系统如何影响真实的人这个角度思考。真实的人是被迫去工作的。你往前追溯几千年，以前你可以走进森林，找点吃的，就这样活下去。现在你必须找份工作，因为森林不让你进了。所以我们把人强行塞进了这个循环里。这就是现实。然后你现在跑来说，你想改变货币。不是所有人都这么说，但有些人确实是。所以我没那么在意 blockchain、crypto 底层怎么运作，那部分没那么有意思。你说的是你想改变货币。历史上有些国家经历过货币重置，这可不是什么愉快的体验。因为如果你本来有一点钱，货币重置可能意味着你突然就没钱了。如果你本来就没钱，那可能会让你觉得自己永远也不可能再有钱。如果你已经退休了，货币重置的时候你怎么办？你已经没有办法再去赚新钱了。这些都是非常非常现实的问题。而我觉得那群技术人忽略了这些，因为他们只想着“钱会涨”。所以当我跟他们聊这些时，他们总想跟我争论 crypto 的底层细节，比如交易怎么结算、加密怎么做、blockchain 怎么运作。我就说，兄弟，那些东西都行，我们可以来回讨论。但到某个时候，我们总得谈谈它到底会怎么影响真实的人。等到 Gen.AI 出来之后，我现在对一切都更偏向哲学层面的思考了。这里面有一部分其实也挺个人的。毕竟我们整个职业生涯都在学习这些技能，训练我们自己的模型，对吧？我们学会了编程。对，我们学会了写程序。而且这很难。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: It's not just blindly typing code. There's an art form to it. This is why we have Ruby. And when you read about Ruby and Matz's vision for having something that can be almost romantic to write, Perl has its own subculture. Golang has its own culture. So we're not just writing code. and my entire career I always thought about writing code as decision making so before we do anything we all figure out what needs to happen and then we have to convince the computer to do it and every keyword every if statement every function call is a decision we're making and of course the syntax kind of gets in the way from time to time so Stack Overflow we go Gen.ai comes out and early stages is kind of like people just talking to the machine and I've never been impressed by talking to a computer. I'd rather talk to real people. So I don't really care too much about that part. Yes, it mimics human capabilities for people that want to talk to a computer. Knock yourselves out. But then we get into the code generation piece. Now we're back to where I was with the crypto stuff. I've used the compiler. It generates a lot of code for me. I've been doing that for a very long time. I haven't written any machine code. And I post things like, hey, I'm adopting the zero token architecture. People are like, what's zero token architecture? I was like, instead of burning tokens, you learn things and you think for yourself and just complete tasks.
text: 对，这不只是闭着眼敲代码而已。这里面是有艺术性的。这也是为什么会有 Ruby。你去看 Ruby 和 Matz 的理念，他想做的是一种写起来几乎带点浪漫感的东西。Perl 有它自己的亚文化。Golang 也有它自己的文化。所以我们不是在单纯写代码。我的整个职业生涯里，我一直把写代码看成是在做决策。也就是说，在我们真正动手之前，大家先要搞清楚到底需要发生什么，然后我们再去说服计算机把它做出来。每一个关键字、每一个 if 语句、每一个函数调用，都是我们在做的一个决策。当然，语法有时候确实会碍事，所以我们才会去 Stack Overflow。后来 Gen.ai 出来了，早期阶段看起来有点像是人们在跟机器聊天。而我从来没对“跟计算机聊天”这件事有多大兴趣。我宁愿跟真人聊。所以那部分我其实没那么在意。是的，它会模仿人的能力；如果你就想跟计算机聊天，那随你。但接着我们就进入代码生成这部分了。然后我们又回到了我看 crypto 时的那个位置。我一直都在用 compiler，它也会帮我生成很多代码。我这么干很多年了。我又不是自己写 machine code。我还发过一些东西，比如“我正在采用 zero token architecture”。大家就问，什么叫 zero token architecture？我说，就是别再烧 token 了，去学习、自己思考，然后把任务完成。

And I think a lot of people are just in this bubble where they believe software is the only way to solve any problem. And then they think Gen AI solves all human problems. And this is where I start to push back on that narrative. It's like, you learn to love this job. and you forgot what the rest of the world is doing. And so I feel like some people are now trapped. And that person, again, I keep going back to that person walking into the industry for the first time. Luckily for me, they were looking for people that had skills and there was a pathway, so many pathways for us. Now the new generation that's coming out, they're unsure of themselves. Hey, I'm watching the news. You guys keep celebrating people just using GNI to do everything. What am I going to do? And I just can't accept the answer being, you're just going to come in here and use Gen.AI to do everything and all of you are now just the same.
text: 我就说，因为是我们教会了机器。我不知道为什么大家总是跳过这一步。大家会说，嘿 Kelsey，会有这种人工智能，它会把所有事都做了。可那是我们训练出来的。那些年我写的代码、我出版的书、我来来回回写的评论、我帮助别人解决问题的那些讨论，全都在里面。当然，你也可以说它们基于这些东西形成了自己的世界观，也许还稍微有点不同。但无论如何，我都不可能在任何情况下把机器放在人之上。我觉得这个领域里有一部分人——我不是说所有人都这样——但确实有相当一部分人真的在想：人到底还有什么用？为什么还需要人来写代码？为什么还需要人来构建软件？这时候我就会想，也许你根本没理解这份工作一直以来到底是什么。我们一直在做的，是解决人的问题，而我们只是使用解决这些问题所需要的技术而已。有些情况下，这种技术刚好是软件。但不是每一项人类活动都需要软件。我觉得很多人都活在一个泡泡里，觉得软件是解决任何问题的唯一方式。然后他们又觉得 Gen AI 能解决所有人的问题。到这里，我就开始反对这种叙事了。就好像你爱上了这份工作，却忘了这个世界上其他人在做什么。所以我觉得有些人现在反而被困住了。然后我又会想到那个第一次走进行业的人。对我来说算幸运，当年市场是在找有技能的人，我们有很多很多条路可以走。可现在新一代出来的人，对自己是不确定的。他们会说，嘿，我在看新闻，你们一直在庆祝人们用 GNI 把一切都做了，那我以后要做什么？而我就是没法接受那个答案：你们以后进来，就是用 Gen.AI 把一切都做了，然后你们所有人都变得一模一样。
