text_en: coming out so the way I think about it so for example if I'm doing due diligence for the fund that I do due diligence for that means before you make that decision to write a check and explain to our LPs why we took this position we need to do a little due diligence and we and the way I do due diligence I want to meet the founder I would like them to walk me through the particular product and I go one step deeper Let's look at the code. Let's look at your Amazon bill. Let's look at the architecture. Let's look at GitHub. How do you manage issues? How do you all work together? I want to get a sense for the team, the product and its trajectory. And when AI is involved, the one thing I just do before the thing kicks off In this meeting, do not say AI, because what we don't want to do is use a big umbrella to describe what you're doing. Let's get concrete details. These are computers. These are computer programs. Yes, just like when I saw a regular expression for the first time is a different way of thinking about software than, you know, imperative things, if else, then. So I get that. But now you have to show me what you're actually doing. So when we do that, when I put that handicap in place, Now they're forced to show me the problem they're solving. They don't just say, hey, AI for healthcare. Nope. Show me exactly what you're doing. And so with that handicap in place, the really good founders, the really good technologists, what they do is they say, hey, here's a problem and hear how an industry currently solves the problem and hear the drawbacks from that. And since they can't say AI, they can't say agentic, they just have to show me how they make the problem better. Now, as a technologist, I know that if I gave you a random PDF there is no easy way for you to procedurally program parsing PDFs you just can never anticipate everything you will ever see but it makes sense to use OCR or if you don't know what an OCR is you might say I will go use Cloud to do it whatever yeah There's probably an easier way to do this than using a large language model. There are smaller models, smaller techniques. So my advice to them would be, you know, you don't need to use an LLM for this. There are smaller models or smaller AI techniques that are not Gen AI. So they're like, oh, good feedback. We can probably lower costs here. But then when they take these technologies and they do something novel with it, I'm like, you know what? That is a good product for the people doing this work. And I'll say, you see that? You did that without saying AI. So on your website, why are you burying all the value of this platform by putting AI in big letters before we get to the value? And so maybe they do change the website to actually talk about the value. When they do the demos they start with the value versus hand wavy text boxes where they put in a prompt and it does something magic. Physicians don't want to do that. They want to see some of the other contextual things and leverage AI to bridge the gap between what they're currently doing and now what's possible. So my advice to people that I'm advising, you know, all the startups, I gotta make an AI pivot. I said, if you all pivot to AI, then you all will have a problem. It's like kids learning to play soccer. They all run to the ball, no strategy. Spread out, figure where you add value and play your position. So when I'm advising a startup, what is your position in this big landscape? You can't all run to the AI ball. You've got to stand back and figure out which value you're going to add. So one of the companies I advise, they're called MassDriver. They have like a visual kind of infrastructures code. You take Terraform, you give it some metadata, and then you can interact with it visually. And part of that visual interaction allows you to do things like this app needs this database. And just drawing that line allows the credentials to flow to the other app under the hood. And now you have a config. Awesome. So now Cloud is the big thing. Everyone's like, no, I don't need that. I don't need Terraform. I'm just going to use Cloud to manage the cloud. Now, someone would experience, I'm like, this is about to be real fun. Because I've seen what humans do when you just give them the AWS console. Watch what Cloud's going to do when you give it to AWS console. And so knowing this, it's like, okay, here's how we can add value to this. Number one, we can take the things that you have in this visual box and split it up into value props. Number one, in order to show things visually, you have to have context. So let's call it the context engine. And then that context engine can be queried by cloud. So instead of pointing at AWS, it's now pointing at only the resources you use. And for people to understand why this is important, if you take some of these agents, they just start investigating the console like, ooh, what's Lambda? Nah, don't need that. But Lambda now is now running. what's this law balance oh don't need that but now that could be running and you don't even know the mess that it made yeah they don't even know the mess that it made yeah it doesn't even know the mess it's made so now we say guardrails or give it context so I said oh okay so instead of doing a full on pivot in a naive way let's reposition here So now some of the features we have become guard routes. Some of the things the way MassDriver does things with IC or infrastructure as code can become skills. So now if you bring Claude to the scenario, instead of starting from scratch, we can just allow the agent to interact with this platform the same way humans do. And then what we end up with is if a human wants to interact visually, it works. If you are just like want to have some automation in your pipeline, then just call the APIs and you still can interact with the context and the deployment engines. But if you really just want to use cloud code, Then now Claude can interact with the same guardrails and structure that the rest of your system does. And then Claude becomes a little clearer. We're not asking it to be magic. We're asking it to be an alternative interface for getting something done. Skills.md becomes implementation detail. And then when we do a webinar where we present this, just watch the light bulbs go off for the people watching and for the team and that to me is the type of advisory where you can look at Gen.A.I. I'm not just like a Gen.A.I. hater. I just don't like the naive promotion and adoption of it. I think it should be way strategic. And since I think about Gen.A.I. as a tool versus the great human replacement, then I can use it in way more primatic ways.
text: 我来说说我是怎么想这件事的。比如说，如果我在替我服务的基金做尽职调查，那意思就是，在你决定投钱、在我们要向 LP 解释为什么要投这个项目之前，我们得先做一些尽调。而我做尽调的方式是：我想见见创始人，我希望他们带我过一遍具体产品，然后我还会再往下一层看。我们来看看代码，看看你们的 Amazon 账单，看看架构，看看 GitHub。你们怎么管理 issue？团队平时怎么协作？我想了解这个团队、这个产品，以及它的发展轨迹。只要项目里涉及 AI，我在会议开始前一定会先说一句：这场会里不要说 AI。因为我们不想用一个特别大的伞状概念来描述你们在做什么。我们要讲具体细节。这些本质上是计算机，是计算机程序。没错，就像我第一次看到正则表达式时，那确实是一种不同于 if else 这种命令式写法的软件思维方式，我理解这一点。但现在你得给我看，你到底实际在做什么。所以当我加上这个限制之后，他们就被迫要给我展示他们在解决什么问题。他们不能只是说，嘿，我们是做医疗 AI 的。不行，你得准确给我看你在做什么。然后在这种限制下，真正优秀的创始人、真正优秀的技术人会这样说：这是一个问题，这是这个行业现在通常怎么解决这个问题的，这是现有方案的缺点。因为他们不能说 AI，也不能说 agentic，他们就只能直接给我看，他们是怎么把这个问题做得更好的。那作为技术人，我知道，如果我随手给你一个 PDF，你几乎不可能靠传统的程序式方法把 PDF 解析这件事写得很完美，因为你永远没法预判你会遇到的所有情况。所以用 OCR 就很合理；如果你不知道 OCR 是什么，你可能会说，那我就用 Cloud 来做，之类的。对，这么做大概率比直接上大语言模型更合适。还有更小的模型、更轻量的技术。所以我会给他们的建议是，你知道吗，这里其实不一定非要用 LLM。你可以用更小的模型，或者一些不属于 Gen AI 的更轻量 AI 技术。他们会说，哦，这反馈很好，我们这里大概还能把成本降下来。但如果他们把这些技术组合起来，做出了真正新颖的东西，我就会说，你知道吗？这对真正做这类工作的人来说，就是个好产品。然后我会告诉他们，你看，你刚才根本没说 AI，也把这件事讲清楚了。那为什么你们网站上要在用户看到价值之前，先用大大的 AI 两个字把这个平台真正的价值给埋掉呢？所以有些团队后来真的会改网站，开始讲价值本身。做演示的时候，他们也会先讲价值，而不是先放一个模糊的文本框，让人输个 prompt，然后神奇地变出点什么。医生不想看这种东西。他们想看到更多上下文相关的内容，然后把 AI 当成桥梁，连接他们现在的工作方式和现在新出现的可能性。所以我给我在辅导的那些人、那些创业公司的建议一直都是：大家都说，我得做个 AI 转型。我就说，如果你们所有人都一起转向 AI，那你们就都麻烦了。这就像一群小孩学踢足球，所有人都冲着球跑，毫无战术。你得拉开站位，想清楚你在哪儿创造价值，踢好你自己的位置。所以当我在给一家创业公司做建议时，我会问：在这个大格局里，你的位置是什么？你们不能所有人都去追那个 AI 球。你得退一步，想清楚你到底要增加哪部分价值。比如我辅导的一家公司叫 MassDriver。他们做的是一种可视化的 infrastructure as code。你把 Terraform 加上一些元数据，然后就可以用可视化方式去操作它。而这种可视化交互的一部分能力，就是你可以做这样的事：这个应用需要这个数据库。你只要把这条线一画，底层就会把凭证自动传给另一个应用。这样你就有了一份配置。很棒。那现在 Cloud 很火，很多人都说，不，我不需要那个，我不需要 Terraform，我直接用 Cloud 来管理云就好了。像我这种有经验的人一看就知道，这下要好玩了。因为我见过人类在只拿到 AWS 控制台时会干出什么事。那你等着看 Cloud 拿到 AWS 控制台之后会干出什么。所以知道这一点之后，我们就会想，好，那我们怎么在这里增加价值？第一，我们可以把你这个可视化盒子里的能力拆成几个价值点。第一，为了能把东西可视化，你必须得有上下文。那我们就把它叫做 context engine。然后这个 context engine 就可以被 cloud 查询。这样它就不是直接指向 AWS 了，而是只指向你实际在用的那些资源。为了让大家明白这为什么重要：如果你把一些 agent 直接扔进控制台里，它们就会开始到处探索，哦，Lambda 是什么？算了，不需要。结果 Lambda 已经跑起来了。哦，这个 load balancer 是什么？算了，不需要。结果它可能也已经跑起来了，而你甚至都不知道它搞出了多大的烂摊子。对，它们自己都不知道自己搞出了多大的烂摊子。它甚至都不知道自己闯了什么祸。所以这时候我们就会说，要加 guardrails，或者给它上下文。于是我就说，哦，明白了。那我们不要用一种很天真的方式做彻底转型，我们应该重新定位。所以现在我们现有的一些功能，就可以变成 guardrails。MassDriver 在 IC，也就是 infrastructure as code 上的一些做法，也可以变成 skills。这样一来，如果你把 Claude 带进这个场景，它就不用从零开始了。我们可以让这个 agent 像人类一样去和这个平台交互。最后的结果就是：如果人类想用可视化方式交互，可以；如果你只是想在流水线里做一些自动化，那就直接调 API，你照样可以和 context 以及部署引擎交互；但如果你真的就是想用 cloud code，那现在 Claude 也可以和系统其他部分一样，遵循同样的 guardrails 和结构。这样 Claude 的角色就清晰多了。我们不是在要求它变魔术，我们是在让它成为完成某件事的另一种接口。Skills.md 变成了实现细节。然后当我们开 webinar 去展示这套东西的时候，你就会看到，不管是观看的人还是团队自己，都会一下子恍然大悟。对我来说，这才是我认可的那种顾问式工作方式：你可以看待 Gen.AI，但我并不是单纯讨厌 Gen.AI。我只是不喜欢那种天真、盲目的宣传和采用方式。我觉得它应该被非常有策略地使用。正因为我把 Gen.AI 看成一种工具，而不是“伟大的人类替代者”，所以我反而能用更务实的方式去使用它。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Thinking about it as a tool What capabilities do you think it gives us software engineers specifically? And also maybe what are some areas where it can maybe give some overconfidence?
text: 把它当成一种工具来看，你觉得它具体给软件工程师带来了哪些能力？另外，有哪些地方它又可能让人产生过度自信？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So one thing I've tried to do is to be a little bit more positive in my thinking because it would be very easy for me to go down the rabbit hole of identifying all of its flaws, right? Like, oh, look, it makes mistakes or it hallucinates sometimes or overly confident gives me a config that then blows a production. I'm going to assume those will get slightly better over time or a human loop will catch those things. So I'm going to give it, for the sake of this discussion, I'm going to give it a little grace. The things that I think it should do well that I think don't get used often enough, there's this concept where people really think it makes sense to do inference every single time. For example, if a human writes a piece of code, we'll write it once, right? Let's say I wanted to authenticate to an endpoint. So I will call the code, I'll go look at the documentation, go to Stack Overflow, figure out the example. I'll call it once. If I had to do this twice, it's probably gonna become a function that I call multiple times in the code base. If I do this like five to 10 times, this may become a library. that I imported multiple apps. So that tends to be the human flow because there's no need to infer or write from scratch this particular thing. And we've done that. That's where open source comes into play. And when we're really doing a good job, these things get just baked into the framework. and they're just there. Sometimes even in the OS. In the OS. And then sometimes things like encryption find its way all the way to the hardware where we do offloading. So this has been the loop that software engineers go through for a long time. Gen AI to me should be no different. So if you find yourself generating the same blocks of code over and over, even though it feels fast or convenient, it is still insane. At some point you should say, whoa, Why is Cod generating the same block, copying things everywhere? Because we know where this leads.
text: 我一直在努力让自己的看法更积极一点，因为如果我要钻牛角尖，去揪它所有的问题，那实在太容易了，对吧？比如，哦，你看，它会犯错，它有时候会幻觉，它还会特别自信地给我一份配置，最后把生产环境搞炸。我先假设这些问题会随着时间推移稍微变好，或者有人类在环能把这些问题拦下来。所以为了这次讨论，我愿意先给它一点宽容。我觉得它本来应该做得很好、但现在还没有被充分利用的一点是：很多人真的觉得每一次都做 inference 很合理。比如说，一个人类写一段代码，通常只会写一次，对吧？假设我想对一个 endpoint 做认证，那我会去查文档、去 Stack Overflow 找例子，搞明白之后写一次。如果我得做第二次，那它大概率就会变成一个函数，在代码库里多次调用。如果我要做五到十次，那它可能就会变成一个库，被多个应用引入。这基本就是人类的工作流，因为没必要每次都从头推断、从头写这个东西。我们一直都是这么干的，这也是开源发挥作用的地方。而当我们做得足够好的时候，这些东西最后就会直接被烘焙进框架里，直接就在那里。有时候甚至进了操作系统。对，进操作系统。再往后，像加密这种东西，甚至会一路下沉到硬件层面，做 offloading。这就是软件工程师很多年来一直在走的循环。在我看来，Gen AI 也不该有什么不同。所以如果你发现自己在一遍又一遍生成同样的代码块，哪怕感觉很快、很方便，这件事本身依然很离谱。到某个时候你就该停下来问一句，等等，为什么 Cod 一直在生成同样的代码块，到处复制粘贴？因为我们都知道这最后会走向什么结果。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Yeah, we've seen that. Of course we do.
text: 对，这种情况我们见过。那当然。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Yeah, and then people get excited about Cod can refactor all of this. But that's a waste of energy. There's no reason to do it just because it can. So I hope what developers are really realizing, what I realized myself from looking at this is the number one thing I realized is that most of our APIs were designed incorrectly, even for humans. Right now, a lot of our APIs, if I already think about infrastructure, you have to call like seven APIs to get a VM in the cloud. Create a VM, a network, a storage device connected to a VPC, and then attach credentials. That's not intent-based. I want a VM. So the first thing you kind of see from this movement is things like MCP, where we wrap these imperative calls into an intent-based thing that says, create a VM, and then that reflects out to the other ones. But here's the thing, I saw this before that. I saw this with Kubernetes. Kubernetes does the exact same thing. When you say, give me an ingress or a service in Kubernetes, that reflects out to seven calls, too, to create load balancers as a cell certificate as DNS setup.
text: 对，然后大家又会兴奋地说，Cod 可以把这些全都重构掉。但那是在浪费精力。不是因为它能做，就代表你该这么做。我希望开发者真正能意识到的一点，也是我自己从这件事里看到的一点，就是：我们很多 API 从一开始设计得就不对，甚至对人类来说都不对。就拿基础设施来说吧，现在很多 API，如果我想在云上搞一台 VM，你得调七个 API。创建 VM、网络、存储设备、连到 VPC，再挂上凭证。这不是基于意图的。我想要的是一台 VM。所以你现在会从这波趋势里看到像 MCP 这样的东西：把这些命令式调用包起来，变成一个基于意图的动作，比如“创建一台 VM”，然后再由它去展开成后面的那些调用。但问题是，这种事我以前就见过。我在 Kubernetes 里就见过。Kubernetes 干的就是同样的事。当你说，给我一个 ingress 或者一个 service 时，它背后一样会展开成七个调用，去创建负载均衡、SSL 证书、DNS 配置。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: It's just the highest you'd be on the interface, right?
text: 只是接口层级更高了，对吧？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Exactly. So I've seen this before. So I'm looking at this like, guys, the fundamentals of this isn't like everything is going to change because of MCP. What the hell, a whole conference? Guys, this is just API design. So in this particular case, I hope developers realize that we shouldn't have fought so hard RPC versus REST. There was this big tug of war around composable APIs. Like 2000s. Yeah, and like a lot of people went down this rabbit hole of like, create VM. Oh, it's not flexible enough. It's like, so what? Create VM v1, create VM v2, who cares? Because these are intent-based APIs. So I think these new tools are reminding us of this. When I look at the way people are prompting and how they write their prompts, a lot of our programming languages were too rigid too to express what you really wanted to happen. We were so afraid maybe Ruby did a better job than some other languages where they try to give you things like until. So that way it was just flowed better as you were writing the code. Other languages are a bit more rigid. It's like, what does FN mean? So you have to go look it up every single time. So writing code becomes very laborious. So when people write a natural language prompt, it kind of tells us a lot about the intentions of querying a database. So I'm hoping developers learn better API design from that front. The other part was, I still get frustrated. I want to learn new technology. I go to the website. There's a little bit of documentation, but for some reason developers still write documentations as hints, as clues, right? I'm trying to learn a new programming language. I won't throw any of them under the bus. And I go there and I'm just going to go look at the standard library. First thing I like to do sometimes is just like, I'm going to parse a JSON file, get a feel for the language. And there's like, there's a JSON library. Sweet. We're off to a good start. Click. And you look at the documentation, you just see function calls. I'm like, can I just see a working example? What do I import? What do I put in this thing? What comes out? And then maybe what do the errors look like? I just want to see a full example. They're like, no. You just get reference. So then what do we do next? Then we search the internet. And you might land on Stack Overflow, someone's blog. They give you a full example. So now I've got to copy and paste it and see what this thing does and hopefully it's up to date with the actual documentation. This has been the loop we've been going through for so many years. So when I use things like Cloud or various tools, they close that loop. You get the example right here. And let's not pretend that Stack Overflow examples were perfect. They were not. So to me, like having something where the tool would then try to build it to tell me if this is correct syntax or thinking to make sure that it's giving me a good suggestion, even I can say that is an improvement.
text: 完全正确。所以这套东西我以前见过。我看着现在这一切就会想，大家，这些底层原理根本不是什么“因为 MCP 一切都要变了”。搞什么，一个完整大会？各位，这不就是 API 设计吗？所以在这个问题上，我希望开发者能意识到，我们当年真的不该在 RPC 和 REST 上打得那么凶。那时候围绕可组合 API 有一场很大的拉锯战。大概 2000 年代那会儿。对，然后很多人一路钻进了一个死胡同，比如“create VM”。哦，这不够灵活。那又怎样？create VM v1、create VM v2，谁在乎？因为这些是基于意图的 API。所以我觉得这些新工具是在提醒我们这一点。再看大家写 prompt 的方式、组织 prompt 的方式，我会觉得，我们很多编程语言也太僵硬了，没法很好表达你真正想让它发生什么。我们过去太害怕这些东西了。也许 Ruby 在这方面比一些别的语言做得更好，它会给你像 until 这样的东西，让你写代码时读起来更顺。别的语言就更僵硬一点。比如，FN 到底是什么意思？你每次都得去查。所以写代码就变成了一件很费劲的事。而当人们写自然语言 prompt 的时候，其实它会告诉我们很多关于“意图”的东西，比如查询数据库时真正的意图是什么。所以我希望开发者能从这里学到更好的 API 设计。另一部分是，我现在还是会很沮丧。我想学一个新技术，打开官网，有一点文档，但不知道为什么，开发者直到今天还在把文档写得像提示、像线索一样，对吧？我想学一门新编程语言，我一般会先去看标准库。有时候我最喜欢做的第一件事就是：我先解析个 JSON 文件，感受一下这门语言。然后我看到，哦，有个 JSON 库。太好了，开局不错。点进去。结果一看文档，只有函数签名。我就想，能不能直接给我一个能跑的例子？我要 import 什么？这里面放什么？出来的是什么？还有，报错大概长什么样？我就想看一个完整例子。结果他们说，不行。你只能看 reference。那接下来我们怎么办？去网上搜。然后你可能会落到 Stack Overflow，或者某个人的博客上。那里面会给你一个完整例子。于是我还得把它复制粘贴下来，看看这东西到底怎么跑，还得祈祷它和官方文档当前版本是匹配的。这就是我们这么多年来一直在重复的循环。所以当我用 Cloud 或其他一些工具时，它们把这个循环闭合了。例子就直接在你面前。我们也别假装 Stack Overflow 上的例子以前都很完美，它们根本不完美。所以对我来说，如果一个工具能先尝试把东西构建起来，告诉我这个语法对不对、这个思路合不合理，确保它给我的建议是靠谱的，那我也得承认，这就是一种进步。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: But I hope we learned that maybe we should not just give hints in the official documentation.
text: 不过我希望我们至少学会了一件事：官方文档也许不该只是给提示。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: I'm watching people write these huge markdown files to give the agent context. How about you write documentation to give me context so I can have fully working examples?
text: 我看到很多人在写那种超长的 markdown 文件，给 agent 提供上下文。那你怎么不直接写文档给我提供上下文，这样我就能拿到完全可运行的示例了？

speaker: Gergely
text_en: With agents, we could actually generate documentation a lot more intentionally. As devs, most of us just don't like... I'm not sure if there's anyone who likes writing documentation. We don't like doing this. We don't like writing tests. In general, these tools can help with that.
text: 有了 agent，我们其实可以更有意识地去生成文档。作为开发者，我们大多数人就是不喜欢……我都不确定有没有人真的喜欢写文档。我们不喜欢干这个。我们也不喜欢写测试。总的来说，这些工具在这方面是能帮上忙的。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: But this is where I think we tend to make a mistake. I remember when I was learning Java for the first time, and the Java developer was like, we don't write docs, the code is self-documenting.
text: 但我觉得这正是我们常犯错的地方。我记得我第一次学 Java 的时候，有个 Java 开发者跟我说，我们不写文档，代码本身就是自解释的。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: I was like, no, it's not. It's just documenting hints. I still have no context.
text: 我当时就想，不，根本不是。那顶多只是一些提示性的说明。我还是没有上下文。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Because to me, again, software development is to me a human endeavor assisted by tools. And so there's a style to documentation. There's a personality to documentation. It's like a movie, like you're trying to educate a person. I don't want just hints of, hey, this thing exists for these reasons. We conform to this particular specification. There are multiple ways to write this code. Here's the most popular way. here's what bad code looks like here's what high performance code looks like here's when to use this library here's when not to use this library I want that kind of in depth as I'm training my own model and what I'm seeing now is which I think is a good thing people are writing a lot more documentation to be consumed by the agent to give it context I was like man I wish we had the same motivation just a decade ago because I think a lot of us would have been way more productive if we didn't have to try to do a wild goose chase every time
text: 因为对我来说，软件开发归根结底是一项由工具辅助的人类活动。所以文档是有风格的，文档是有个性的。它有点像一部电影，你是在试图教育一个人。我不想只看到那种提示式的信息，比如，哦，这个东西存在是出于这些原因。我们遵循这个特定规范。这个代码有很多种写法。这是最常见的一种。这是烂代码长什么样。这是高性能代码长什么样。什么时候该用这个库，什么时候不该用这个库。我在训练我自己的模型时，就想要这种足够深入的内容。而我现在看到的是——我觉得这其实是件好事——大家为了让 agent 理解上下文，开始写更多文档给它消费。我就在想，天哪，要是十年前我们也有同样的动力就好了。因为如果不用每次都像无头苍蝇一样到处乱找，我觉得我们很多人的生产力都会高得多。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Well, interesting enough, Kat Cosgrove told me that one of the reasons she thinks Kubernetes 1 was documentation. They take it extremely seriously. Few other projects, if any, does it at that level.
text: 不过很有意思的是，Kat Cosgrove 跟我说，她认为 Kubernetes 成功的原因之一就是文档。他们对这件事极其认真。很少有别的项目——如果真有的话——能做到那个水平。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: So just proving a point.
text: 所以这不就正好证明了我的观点。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: One thing that a lot of experienced software engineers are worried about right now is all the AI. We're all using AI agents. They do generate code really quickly. which is something writing code used to take a lot of effort it took a lot long time to be good at it now with code reviews there's now more tools coming in and some people are like worried like okay like what is happening to my profession like the craft of writing code seems to be something that we can offload and more and more people are offloading including prominent people what will this do to software engineering and what advice would you give to people who are experienced software engineers they're a little bit worried because it's a big shift they still want to be whatever great engineer will look like in the future but what steps might be able to take and I especially like your take because I think you're pretty grounded in just looking at this from a vantage point that some of us are not
text: 现在很多有经验的软件工程师都在担心一件事，就是 AI。我们都在用 AI agent，它们生成代码的速度确实非常快。而写代码这件事，以前是很费功夫的，也要花很长时间才能真正擅长。现在再加上 code review 方面也有越来越多工具进来，有些人就会担心：好吧，那我的职业到底会变成什么样？写代码这门手艺，似乎已经变成可以被外包出去的东西，而且越来越多人都在这么做，包括一些很有影响力的人。这会给软件工程带来什么影响？你会给那些有经验、但又有点担心的软件工程师什么建议？因为这确实是一次很大的转变。他们依然想成为未来那种“优秀工程师”该有的样子，但他们现在可以采取哪些步骤？我尤其想听听你的看法，因为我觉得你看这个问题很脚踏实地，而且你的观察视角是我们很多人没有的。

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: I think as a software developer, the first step you have to do is have a bit of reflection. The last 20, 30 years, you have been automating a lot of industries away yourself. All those programs, I remember seeing, maybe it was like a diagram of every device that has been replaced by the iPhone. The radio, the calculator, the compass, all of these tools people used to buy individually. The top 30 of those electronics from the last 40 years are all in your iPhone, all of them. That means some electronic makers have gone out of business. They're gone. You did that. Not in a malicious way, but you were part of that. And so the software developer has been glorified for a very long time. The internet, some people would say, caused the downfall of magazines and newspapers because of the convenience of having a software approach. And so you have been part of the change to other industries and other people yourself. What did you think about that? Did you even think about it at all? So let's not be surprised if you find no sympathy from all the other professions that you've helped force change upon. So I think that's step one. You have to go do that reflection because if you don't do that reflection, you won't know how to behave now. You're going to be complaining and people are going to look at you crazy because where was this empathy before? You might be very excited about this, and not realize you're only excited because you're in position to benefit from this. So if you work at an Anthropic, of course this is the future because it's in your hands. If you're at NVIDIA, of course this is the future because you will be selling the picks and shovels. And so you got to ask yourself, why am I excited? Now, what I don't want you to do is necessarily feel guilty about it, but I need you to see the big, big picture. It's going to help frame everything else. The second thing I want you to do is ask yourself, what was my job? Remember, there was a point in my career where I was lying to myself. I thought my job was to be the best Linux administrator ever. You as a software developer, you may have thought your job was to be the only person in an organization that can write code. And since no one else could do it, you are safe. right and so you didn't learn any other skills networking product management design talking to customers nope all you had to do was write code and you were safe and you probably made more money than everybody and you were fine with that now you got caught the only thing you were good at is now been commoditized again you did this to others so let's say the vision you have for yourself is only in this very narrow realm You're going to be very afraid of this trajectory because all you know is software developers write code. That's it. Some software developers still don't write tests, still don't know how to deploy anything. And so they are really afraid because they can't see any other way that this plays out. Now if you're a full stack engineer you're probably like man there's so much more than just writing code you have to do architecture you have to do design you have to do so many other things that I love Claude because now I can focus on those things and I can use these tools instead so I can see why that person would have that perspective Now, I understand why that full stack person has a perspective of watching the same people commentate that the code generation piece replaces everything else. They're going to be like, no, you don't know what this job is. It's way more than just writing code. Writing code is the last step. If you're a security engineer, you're probably like, we never figured out security for the pace of the current enterprise. The one before? Yeah, like... Everyone thinks they're moving slow. I remember I took a security training thing and most of them aren't that good because they can't go super deep. They just tell you, hey, here's how to avoid phishing. Here's how to not leak information, adhere to various laws and things like that. And then they said one thing this time that I learned that was pretty good. What's the key to protecting yourself? And they say, you know what? Just go slow. A lot of attacks are, I'm about to board a flight. and let's say you're an admin or you're a VP and the CEO texts you, right now we need to wire the money to Oracle to pay for the license. They're going to cut it off. Right now, this needs to be done immediately. You look at your phone, it's definitely from the phone number of the CEO. You have a good relationship. Everything looks right and it's moving fast. So you're like, man, I'm on a 10-hour flight. I need to do this now. Turns out the attacker knows you're about to board this flight. They've seen all your previous text messages They know how your manager talks to you They know that you've moved fast in the past And so they now are primed to get you to do the exact same behavior again And you could be the VP of security So you should know better And sometimes that naive confidence will make you feel like I'm obviously not being phished I've done this many times. This is definitely the CEO. Who would know how we actually operate? And who would know that I can actually do that? So what do you do? You'd make the transfer. And just like that, 10 million has been wired to the wrong place because you moved fast. It wasn't because you were not smart. It wasn't because you were not productive because in this case, you were productive, but you did the wrong thing. So when I think about code, There is value in having a healthy pace. Let's say you're an insurance company. You sell insurance. Hey, make model. How old are you? Have you had any accidents? Okay. Here is your insurance for the year. Simple. Very simple thing. If you're an insurance company and that's all you are, you're kind of close to being done. Now you could say, with Gen AI, we should get into payments. We should compete with DoorDash, right? We have all these tools. Let's go and... We could build it. Yeah, we can build it. So the thing is, should you just build it because you can? And the answer is typically no. So we usually optimize ourselves as humans around the pace needed for the task. And when we don't need to do that work anymore, we move on to something else. So now I think what we're going to end up with is people not realizing a lot of this stuff we were doing in software engineering was decision making. What database to use. what schema should we really collect someone's social security number or should we avoid it not yeah I can write code to parse the social security number really fast no no no should you even do it and so when you write code it almost makes you slow down again because there's been times where I thought I had a good design that's that phrase writing this thinking so is writing code so as you're writing the code you're like hey wait a minute This loop is ridiculous, right? Not only is it going to make the computer warm, this is not the right thing to do. There's a better data structure than the algorithm that I'm using. So then you stop and say, hey, the data structure is wrong. We need to change the way we print receipts on the cash register. Sure, I can write this code, but this is the wrong data structure. While I can generate the code, doing reports are going to be a nightmare. Summarizing this data downstream is going to be a nightmare. Stop everything. Now that I've thought about it, we need to change the architecture from the top down. So decision making sometimes does benefit from slow. And when I'm saying slow here, we're not talking waterfall six months. We're just talking about maybe one more day before you go at it. And I think some of us are going to miss that part because clots fit it out. Ship it.
text: 我觉得，作为软件开发者，第一步你得先做一点反思。过去二三十年里，其实你自己也一直在把很多行业自动化掉。我记得以前看过一个图，大概是在讲 iPhone 替代了多少设备。收音机、计算器、指南针，这些以前大家都要单独买的工具。过去四十年里最常见的那三十来种电子产品，现在全都在你的 iPhone 里，全都在。这就意味着有些电子产品厂商已经倒闭了，消失了。这是你们造成的。不是出于恶意，但你确实是其中的一部分。所以软件开发者这个职业，长期以来一直被神化。还有人会说，互联网之所以让杂志和报纸衰落，就是因为软件这种方式更方便。所以你自己其实也一直在推动别的行业、别的人发生改变。那你当时是怎么想的？你有认真想过这件事吗？所以现在如果你发现，其他那些被你推动着改变的职业，对你并没有什么同情，也别太惊讶。我觉得这是第一步。你必须先去做这个反思，因为如果你不反思，你就不知道现在该怎么面对。你只会一直抱怨，然后别人会像看怪人一样看着你，因为他们会想：你以前的同理心去哪了？你可能现在对这一切特别兴奋，却没意识到，你之所以兴奋，只是因为你正好站在受益的位置上。比如你在 Anthropic 工作，那你当然会说这就是未来，因为未来握在你手里。你要是在 NVIDIA，那你当然也会说这就是未来，因为你卖的是镐和铲子。所以你得问问自己，我到底为什么兴奋？我不是要你因此产生负罪感，但我需要你看到那个非常非常大的全局。这会帮助你理解后面的一切。第二件我希望你做的事，是问问自己：我的工作到底是什么？我记得我职业生涯里有一段时间，其实是在骗自己。我以为我的工作，就是成为最厉害的 Linux 管理员。你作为软件开发者，也可能曾经以为，你的工作就是做组织里唯一会写代码的人。既然别人都不会，那你就安全了。对吧？所以你也没去学别的技能：网络、产品管理、设计、和客户沟通，都没有。你只要会写代码，你就安全。你可能还比所有人赚得都多，而且你也挺满意。现在你被现实抓住了——你唯一擅长的东西，现在也被商品化了。还是那句话，这事你以前也对别人做过。假设你对自己的定位，只局限在这么狭窄的一块，那你一定会非常害怕这个趋势，因为你脑子里只有一件事：软件开发者就是写代码，仅此而已。有些软件开发者到现在都还不写测试，也不知道怎么部署任何东西。所以他们真的很害怕，因为他们看不到这件事还有别的走向。可如果你是个 full stack engineer，你大概会想，天啊，这工作可远不只是写代码。你还得做架构、做设计、做很多很多别的事。所以我才喜欢 Claude，因为现在我可以把注意力放在那些事情上，同时把这些工具用起来。所以我能理解为什么那类人会有那样的看法。与此同时，我也理解为什么那个 full stack 的人，会看着另一群人评论说“代码生成会取代一切”，然后忍不住说：不，你根本不知道这份工作是什么。这工作远不只是写代码。写代码其实是最后一步。如果你是安全工程师，你大概会想，我们连当前企业这个速度下的安全问题都还没搞明白。上一代那套？是啊，就像……每个人都觉得自己已经够慢了。我记得我上过一次安全培训，大多数这种培训都不怎么样，因为讲不深。它们只会告诉你，怎么避免钓鱼、怎么别泄露信息、怎么遵守各种法律法规之类的。但那次他们讲了一句话，我觉得还挺有启发。保护自己的关键是什么？他们说，你知道吗？就是慢一点。很多攻击都是这样的：你正要登机。假设你是个管理员，或者你是 VP，这时候 CEO 给你发短信，说我们现在必须马上把钱打给 Oracle 付许可证费用，不然他们就要停服务了。现在，立刻，必须马上办。你看手机，号码确实是 CEO 的。你们平时关系也不错。一切看起来都对，而且事情很急。于是你就想，天啊，我接下来要坐十个小时飞机，我得现在就处理。结果呢，攻击者早就知道你要登机。他们看过你以前的短信，知道你经理平时怎么跟你说话，也知道你过去在这种情况下会快速处理。所以他们现在就是在诱导你，再次做出同样的行为。你甚至可能是安全 VP。按理说你应该更清楚才对。但有时候，正是那种天真的自信会让你觉得：我当然不是被钓鱼了，这种事我做过很多次了。这肯定就是 CEO。谁会知道我们平时到底怎么运作？谁又会知道我真的有权限这么做？那你会怎么做？你就把钱转了。就这么一下，1000 万就打错地方了，只因为你动作太快。这不是因为你不聪明，也不是因为你不高效。恰恰相反，这次你很高效，只是你高效地做错了事。所以当我想到写代码这件事时，我会觉得，保持一种健康的节奏是有价值的。比如你是一家保险公司，你卖保险。好，车型是什么？你多大？出过事故吗？好，这是你这一年的保单。很简单，非常简单。如果你是一家保险公司，而你做的就只是这个，那你其实已经差不多把该做的都做完了。现在你可能会说，有了 Gen AI，我们是不是该去做支付？是不是该去跟 DoorDash 竞争？对吧，我们现在有这么多工具。走吧，我们可以做。对，我们能做出来。问题是，你是不是因为能做，就该去做？答案通常是否定的。所以作为人类，我们通常会围绕任务所需要的节奏来优化自己。当某项工作不再需要我们亲自做时，我们就会转去做别的事。所以我现在觉得，接下来会发生的一件事是：很多人没有意识到，我们在软件工程里做的大量事情，其实是决策。该用什么数据库？schema 该怎么设计？我们到底应不应该收集用户的 social security number，还是应该避免？不是说“我当然可以很快写代码去解析 social security number”，不是这个问题，问题是：你到底该不该这么做？而且很多时候，写代码这件事本身，反而会让你重新慢下来。因为有很多次，我本来以为自己的设计很好。有句话叫 writing is thinking，写代码其实也是。你在写代码的时候，会突然意识到：等等，这个循环也太离谱了吧？这不光会把电脑跑热，这事本身就不对。应该有比我现在这个算法更好的数据结构。于是你停下来，说，不对，是数据结构错了。我们得改一下收银机打印小票的方式。当然，我可以把这段代码写出来，但这个数据结构就是错的。代码我能生成，可后面做报表会变成噩梦，下游做数据汇总也会变成噩梦。先停一下。现在我想明白了，我们得从上到下改架构。所以决策这件事，有时候确实会从“慢一点”里受益。我这里说的慢，不是说要回到瀑布式开发，搞六个月那种。只是说，也许在你真正动手之前，多给自己一天时间。我觉得有些人会错过这一点，因为 Claude 已经把东西吐出来了。那就发吧。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: Yeah, and that's also one thing that you always have the more experienced generation be worried about the young generation. I remember when I joined the industry, ReSharper had come out. ReSharper, and the experience of the old guard was like, nah, you're not a real developer if you use ReSharper because you're not going to learn the library and you need that. And that's what makes you a real developer. And then I remember when I was now, You know, five plus years of experience and stack overflow started to become big. And I was like, nah, you don't want to go to stack overflow because you're not going to, you know, learn the real thing. So, but now what the current old guard is saying, which is, you know, where I'm, I guess I'm part of it is like, well, if you use AI, you're going to miss learning the basics. And when you have learned the basics, it's so much easier to use AI. And I wonder if we're just repeating the same mistake as the previous ones did, which is the new generation usually figures out the tools, they understand how to do it, or are we rightly concerned that some people who are coming into this AI native, they're now learning to code, they can jump through so many layers that they will just not see what's under, understand, or are we just making assumptions that might not be true?
text: 对，而且这也是一个老问题：更有经验的那一代人，总会担心年轻一代。我记得我刚入行的时候，ReSharper 刚出来。老一辈的反应基本就是：不，你要是用 ReSharper，你就不算真正的开发者，因为你根本学不会那些库，而那些东西才是你必须掌握的，那才让你成为真正的开发者。后来我自己也有了五年以上经验，Stack Overflow 开始火起来。我那时候也会说，不，你别老去 Stack Overflow，因为那样你学不到真正的东西。所以现在这一代“老一辈”——我想我现在也算其中一员了——说的是：如果你用 AI，你就会错过基础。而一旦你把基础学会了，再去用 AI 就会容易得多。我就在想，我们是不是只是在重复前人犯过的同样错误？也就是说，新一代通常都会自己摸索出怎么用这些工具，他们也会理解该怎么做。还是说，我们现在的担心其实是对的：有些从一开始就在 AI native 环境里成长的人，他们现在学写代码，可以一下子跨过很多层，于是根本看不到底下是什么，也理解不了底层原理？还是说，我们只是在做一些未必成立的假设？

speaker: Kelsey
text_en: Here's where I think it can be right on both sides. Do you need to learn how to code to make an impact in this industry? The answer is no. You do not have to. There are some people who use these no-code platforms where they drag and drop and they produce a really good app. There are some people who have built a consultancy business by just using Wix, right? They go there, their website actually looks pretty good. and so they got really far with that. Now for what they're trying to do and accomplish in life, they'll probably be fine. But let's just say you are a software engineer and the idea behind software engineer is not limited to just producing apps. Software is the interface between hardware and things people want to do. So there's a whole bunch of things you need to learn. So if you want to be that type of software engineer, you're going to learn hardware too. If you don't understand hardware, you can never work at that level. And look, if that's not your job, then so be it. But you will never have that creativity. I remember seeing someone was like, hey, you can do isolation without a VM. I was like, how would you do that? He's like, oh, because when you boot the kernel, there's a thing you can do before the kernel loads to isolate it in a way that you can lock down processes. The only reason why this person knows is they know the full boot sequence from firmware to switching to the kernel and the tricks you can do in between. Now, for me, that doesn't work at that depth. I'm thinking there's only virtualization CPU isolation things like G-Visor where you intercept system calls but never did I think about the boot sequence and so yes you can get very far but as someone like we applaud every version of Opus that's released or ChatGPT but there are versions of yourself that get deeper from these new trainings so no you don't have to but if you ever want to get better at anything and sometimes that depth that nuance is the thing that leads to an invention If you know how a compiler works, if you know how memory management works, that might give you enough information to say, oh, I can make a new programming language. If the only thing you know is the surface, you can't even imagine how you can create another programming language that is a better fit for the task at hand because you've never gone that deep. I'm not saying everyone wants to do that, so I think it is fair to say all I want to do is come in, get a job, and if that job can be done by using AI tools, I think the side effect of that is, then that job will be commoditized. It has to. That's just the way it's going to go. But I've always seen myself for my entire career, I want to learn more. I want to go deeper. I want to go so deep that I can create. And I think a lot of people who are doing this, the reason why we're having this reaction, some of us, part of our careers have been the creation part. There is no speck for this. There's no protocol for this. We're gonna make it work. A lot of people that are doing like the reverse engineering, the hacking, there's like, there's no framework for what I'm about to do. I just know how memory works and I don't care what your security tool does. I will make it do what I want it to do. You need to go way below the surface. And so for a lot of us that are saying this, we know the value of the fundamentals that lead to the other stuff. And so if you tell the next generation, Oh, you don't need to learn these things. It's like, that may be right in the short term, but we know for a fact your career will be limited. And that may not be a problem and you have to decide. But make no mistake, if we put this much effort in training the model so that it can spit things out, you better make sure that you are willing to train your own model so my advice to people would be and maybe we should talk about it different maybe we shouldn't have so much fear mongering around it maybe we wouldn't should put it a versus this we should just say great artists tend to know how to mix colors and it isn't your benefit to understand the primary colors so you can mix them to get the other colors and it's a superpower, right? So you don't have to go buy, you know, imagine an artist trying to go buy 16 million colors and put them on a desk because they don't know how to mix colors. If you teach a person how to mix colors, you can get any color you want. And I think that's the way we have to approach it. It's just another skill that if you had it, you might just unlock some creativity. So I encourage you to learn it. Kelsey, thank you very much. This was just an amazing conversation. Awesome. Thanks for having me.
text: 我觉得这件事两边都可能有道理。你需要学会写代码，才能在这个行业里做出影响吗？答案是不需要。真的不需要。有些人用 no-code 平台，拖拖拽拽，也能做出很不错的应用。还有些人，光靠 Wix 就做起了咨询业务，对吧？他们做出来的网站其实也挺像样，所以他们也走得很远。对他们想做的事、想过的人生来说，可能这样就完全够了。但如果你说自己是软件工程师，而“软件工程师”这个概念并不只是做出几个 app，那就不一样了。软件是硬件和人们想做的事情之间的接口。所以这里面有一大堆东西你都得学。如果你想成为那种软件工程师，你连硬件也得学。如果你不懂硬件，你就永远不可能在那个层面上工作。当然，如果那本来就不是你的工作，那也没关系。但你就永远不会拥有那种创造力。我记得我看到有人说，嘿，不用 VM 也能做隔离。我当时就想，怎么做？他说，哦，因为在 kernel 启动的时候，在 kernel 真正加载之前，有个地方你可以动手脚，用一种方式把它隔离开，从而锁住进程。这个人之所以知道，是因为他了解从 firmware 到切换进 kernel 的整个 boot sequence，也知道中间能玩哪些技巧。对我来说，我的思路没到那么深。我想到的只有虚拟化、CPU 隔离，或者像 G-Visor 这种拦截 system call 的东西，但我从来没想过 boot sequence 本身。所以，是的，你可以走得很远。但就像我们会为每一个新发布的 Opus 版本或者 ChatGPT 版本鼓掌一样，你自己也有不同版本，而那些新训练会让你变得更深。所以，不，你不是非学不可。但如果你想在任何事情上变得更强，有时候正是那种深度、那种细微差别，才会带来真正的发明。如果你懂编译器是怎么工作的，如果你懂内存管理是怎么工作的，那可能就足以让你想到：哦，我可以做一门新的编程语言。如果你知道的只有表面那一层，你甚至都想象不出来，怎么去创造一门更适合当前任务的新语言，因为你从来没钻到那么深。我不是说每个人都想做这个，所以我觉得这样说也很公平：我只是想进来，找份工作，如果这份工作可以靠 AI 工具完成，那也挺好。我觉得这种选择的副作用就是，那这份工作最终一定会被商品化。一定会，这就是趋势。但我整个职业生涯里一直把自己看成那种人：我想学更多，我想钻得更深，我想深到自己可以创造。我觉得我们之所以会有这种反应，其中一部分原因就是，我们职业生涯里有一部分工作，本来就是“创造”。这东西没有 spec，没有 protocol，我们就是要把它做出来。很多做逆向、做 hacking 的人也是这样：我接下来要做的事，没有什么 framework 可套。我只知道内存是怎么工作的，我也不在乎你的安全工具怎么设计，我就是能让它按我的方式运行。你必须钻到远低于表面的地方。所以我们这些会这么说的人，是因为我们知道 fundamentals 的价值，而这些 fundamentals 会通向后面的那些东西。所以如果你跟下一代说，哦，这些你都不用学。那短期看也许没错，但我们很清楚，你的职业上限一定会受限。这未必是问题，最终还是你自己决定。但有一点别搞错了：如果我们愿意花这么大力气去训练模型，让它能把东西吐出来，那你最好也愿意训练你自己的模型。所以我给大家的建议会是，也许我们该换一种说法。也许我们不该这么制造恐慌。也许我们不该把它讲成非此即彼。我们应该说，优秀的画家通常都懂怎么调色，而理解三原色、知道怎么把它们混成别的颜色，对你是有好处的。这是一种超能力，对吧？你总不能想象一个画家去买 1600 万种颜色，然后全摆在桌上，只因为他不会调色。如果你教会一个人怎么调色，他就能得到任何他想要的颜色。我觉得我们就该这么看待这件事。它只是另一项技能，而如果你掌握了它，也许就能解锁一些创造力。所以我鼓励大家去学。Kelsey，非常感谢你。这次对话真的太精彩了。太棒了。谢谢邀请我。

speaker: Gergely
text_en: I will admit I was glued to my chair for the whole of the conversation. Apologies that it took this long, but I hope you agree that this specific one was worth it. Kelsey's past was just so unlikely. He's someone who was raised by a single mother, dropped out of college in favor of installing DSL lines door-to-door at 19, and still ended up as Distinguished Engineer at Google Cloud. Only a few hundred people who hold a title at the company. And he retired at the top three years ago when he decided that he no longer needs to work for others. What an inspiration. One thing I took notes on was when Kelsey said how every job is an interview. When Kelsey was giving that gophercon talk and PXE booting CoreOS from his slide deck, he had no idea that the CoreOS team was sitting in the audience, and that's how he ended up at CoreOS. When he was contributing to Puppet at nights and weekends, he didn't know that James Turnbull would walk into his office one day and recognize his name. He just kept showing up and doing the work in public. It's a lesson worth remembering. Do the best work you can at work. It might unknowingly be your job interview for your next step in your career. I also found the Microsoft offer fascinating. Kelsey did not use the offer from Microsoft as an ultimatum at Google, even though he could have. He just told his manager the truth, and then Google matched the offer. Obviously, at this high level, there's no universal compensation negotiating advice that always works. Being a straight shooter with high integrity is something that is good to keep in mind. I was also inspired by Kelsey's focus on minimalism. He treats money as freedom tokens and made sure that his lifestyle never inflated with his salary so that early retirement was always a real option, not just fantasy. Finally, Kelsey's AI takes. His point is grounded and pragmatic. AI does not change what software engineering is If you enjoyed this podcast, please do subscribe on your favorite podcast platform and on YouTube. A special thank you if you also leave a rating on the show. Thanks and see you in the next one.
text: 我得承认，整场对话我都像被钉在椅子上一样听得入神。抱歉让你们等了这么久，不过我希望你也会同意，这一期绝对值得。Kelsey 的过去实在太不寻常了。他由单亲妈妈抚养长大，19 岁时从大学退学，改去挨家挨户安装 DSL 线路，最后却一路做到 Google Cloud 的 Distinguished Engineer。全公司拥有这个头衔的人也就几百个。三年前，他在职业巅峰时选择退休，因为他决定自己已经不需要再为别人工作了。真的很有启发。我记下的一点是，Kelsey 说每一份工作其实都是一次面试。当年 Kelsey 在 gophercon 做演讲，在幻灯片里现场用 PXE boot 启动 CoreOS 的时候，他根本不知道 CoreOS 团队就坐在台下，而那也正是他后来加入 CoreOS 的原因。后来他在晚上和周末给 Puppet 做贡献时，也不知道有一天 James Turnbull 会走进他的办公室，一眼认出他的名字。他只是持续出现，持续在公开场合把事情做好。这是个很值得记住的教训：在工作中尽你所能做到最好。你可能并不知道，那其实就是你职业生涯下一步的面试。我也觉得 Microsoft 的 offer 那段很有意思。Kelsey 并没有把 Microsoft 的 offer 当成对 Google 的最后通牒，虽然他完全可以这么做。他只是如实告诉了自己的经理，然后 Google 就 match 了这个 offer。当然，到了这么高的层级，不存在一种永远有效的薪酬谈判建议。但坦诚直接、保持高度诚信，这一点很值得记住。我也很受 Kelsey 那种极简主义思路的启发。他把钱看作“自由代币”，并且确保自己的生活方式不会随着薪水上涨而膨胀，这样提前退休就始终是一个真实可行的选项，而不只是幻想。最后是 Kelsey 对 AI 的看法。他的观点很扎实，也很务实。AI 并没有改变软件工程到底是什么。如果你喜欢这期播客，请在你常用的播客平台和 YouTube 上订阅。要是你还能给节目留个评分，那就更感谢了。谢谢，我们下期见。
